name: Eduardo Gutierrez
q1: Honestly, the transition has been smoother than I expected. The new system hasn't really disrupted my routines much — if anything, the intercom is a little easier to use than the old one. I still get my announcements when I need them, and the bell schedule has been consistent for me.
q2: The training was okay. It covered the basics, but I felt like it moved fast and didn't leave much room for questions. A follow-up session a few weeks in would have been helpful once we'd actually had a chance to use it.
q3: Communication was decent but not great. I heard about the change at a staff meeting, and then there were a couple of emails, but the timeline kept shifting and it was hard to keep track of what was happening when. A simple one-page update each week would have gone a long way.
q4: The workflow for making announcements from my classroom is genuinely improved. The interface is more intuitive than what we had before.
q5: The system glitches occasionally — I've had it cut out mid-announcement twice since we started. Not the end of the world, but it's not confidence-inspiring either.

name: Rachel Bennett
q1: It's been a disruption, honestly. The installation itself took longer than we were told, and during that period my classroom routines were thrown off. I'm still adjusting to some of the new workflows and it feels like everything takes a few extra steps now.
q2: Training was fine. Not amazing, not terrible. I felt like I learned enough to get by, but there are features I still don't fully understand. I'd have liked more hands-on time rather than just watching someone demo it on a screen.
q3: I was pretty blindsided by the whole thing. I found out about the installation timeline from another teacher, not from admin. It would have helped to have earlier and more direct communication about what was changing and why. I felt like it was just sprung on us.
q4: The sound quality is clearer than the old system, I'll give it that.
q5: The whole rollout felt rushed and poorly communicated. I would have appreciated being consulted or at least informed with more lead time. The disruption to my classroom during installation was significant and could have been better managed.

name: Megan Martin
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some things are marginally better, but honestly I had a good routine with the old system and this change wasn't something I was asking for. I've adapted, but I wouldn't say my day-to-day has improved in any meaningful way.
q2: The training was actually quite good. They had us go through each function step by step, and the trainer was patient with questions. I felt prepared to use the system by the time we were done, which I appreciate given that I wasn't exactly thrilled about the switch.
q3: I knew it was coming but the details were vague for a long time. We got a general announcement at the start of the semester and then not much until it was actually happening. I remember being in room 14 when the installers showed up and I hadn't been told they'd be in my area that day.
q5: I don't think we needed a new system. The old one worked fine for what we needed. If the district is going to make these kinds of changes, I'd want to see clearer evidence of why it's necessary.

name: Andrea Lewis
q1: It's been a fairly neutral change for me. The new system works, the old one worked. My day-to-day hasn't changed dramatically either way.
q2: The training felt a little rushed. I picked up the basics but I know there are features I'm not using because I didn't fully grasp them during the session. Some kind of quick-reference guide would be useful.
q3: I felt well-informed about the change. We got emails, it was discussed at staff meetings, and the timeline was shared in advance. I knew what to expect and roughly when to expect it.
q4: The new interface is clean and straightforward. Easy to figure out even without the training.
q5: Nothing major. It would be nice if the system remembered my last settings instead of resetting to defaults each time.

name: Noelle Kealoha
q1: This system is honestly great compared to what I've worked with before. The announcements come through clearly, the controls are intuitive, and it just works. I'm really pleased with it overall and it's made my mornings smoother.
q2: Training was solid. I came in with pretty low expectations based on past experiences with this kind of thing, and I was pleasantly surprised. The trainers knew the system well and made it easy to follow along.
q3: The communication could have been better. I'm newer here so I wasn't sure if I was just missing channels, but I didn't hear much about the change until it was already underway. That said, it wasn't a huge deal — the system itself is good enough that the transition was pretty painless.
q4: The audio quality is miles ahead of what I'm used to. At my previous district we didn't even have speakers in every room, so this is a massive upgrade. The emergency alert integration is also really well done.
q5: N/A

name: Vinh Wu
q1: It hasn't changed things much for me. I use the intercom for the same purposes I always have, and while the new panel looks different, the functionality is roughly the same. I adapted quickly enough.
q2: Training was adequate. I've been through enough of these system changes over the years that I mostly just needed to know where the buttons moved to. The session covered that well enough, though I know some of my colleagues needed more time than was allotted.
q3: We were told it was coming, but the specifics trickled in slowly. I remember asking at a department meeting in October when exactly installation would start and nobody had a firm answer. Eventually it just appeared. A concrete schedule posted somewhere visible would have helped.
q4: The speakers are noticeably clearer than the old ones. I can actually understand every word of morning announcements now, which is a genuine improvement.
q5: Reliability has been so-so. I've had days where the system seemed fine and days where there's a noticeable lag or the intercom doesn't respond on the first try. It's not catastrophic but it's inconsistent.

name: Todd Sullivan
q1: It's been alright. I don't have a ton of comparison since I haven't been here that long, but the new system seems to do what it needs to do. My daily workflow hasn't really been affected one way or the other.
q2: The training was decent — I felt like I got enough to use the system day to day. I'm pretty comfortable with technology in general so it wasn't a steep learning curve for me.
q3: I honestly didn't get much heads-up about the change. I think because I'm newer, I might have missed some of the earlier conversations, but it would have been nice to have a dedicated email or handout for newer staff explaining the transition.
q4: It's pretty easy to use once you know the basics.
q5: The system feels a bit unreliable. I've had the intercom not connect a few times when I've tried to use it, which is frustrating when you need to reach the office quickly.

name: Kevin Gupta
q1: The new system has been a positive change for me. Coming from a district where the communications setup was really outdated, I appreciate how much more capable this one is. It's streamlined a few things in my day, particularly around emergency drills.
q2: I thought the training was well done. They covered everything I needed and the materials were easy to follow. I've seen much worse when it comes to rolling out new technology — this was actually organized and thorough.
q3: Communication was good. I was kept in the loop through emails and staff meetings, and the timeline they shared mostly held up. I felt like I knew what was coming and when.
q4: The system is really reliable — I haven't had a single issue with it. The sound quality is excellent and the emergency features give me a lot more confidence than what I had before. This is a significant step up from what I'm used to.
q5: Nothing comes to mind. It's been a smooth experience.

name: Tammy White
q1: It's been great, honestly. I'm used to systems that barely function, so having something this solid and modern feels like a luxury. My day-to-day has been positively impacted — announcements are clear, the controls are responsive, and everything just works the way it should.
q2: Training was really good. The trainer walked us through every feature methodically and answered all our questions. I left feeling confident I could use every part of the system. Best tech training I've had in a school setting.
q3: I felt very well informed. The principal sent out clear communications about the timeline and what to expect, and there were updates along the way. This is how change management should be done.
q4: Honestly, almost everything. The reliability is outstanding, the audio quality is crystal clear, and the whole system feels modern and well-designed. At my last school we were dealing with equipment from the early 2000s, so this is night and day.

name: Helen Das
q1: Frankly, this has been a step backward from what I had before. The system I used at my previous district was more intuitive and far more reliable. I've had to adjust my routines to work around inconsistencies that I shouldn't have to deal with at this point in my career.
q2: The training itself was excellent — thorough, well-paced, and the trainers clearly knew the product inside and out. I have no complaints about the training whatsoever. It's the system that's the problem, not how we were taught to use it.
q3: Communication about the change was lacking. I transferred in fairly recently and I wasn't sure where to find information about the timeline or what exactly was changing. I had to piece things together from hallway conversations. A centralized information hub or even a simple FAQ would have been very helpful.
q4: The training materials and support documentation are genuinely good. I'll give them that.
q5: The reliability is not where it needs to be. I'm used to a system that just worked — you pressed the button and it worked every single time. This one has dead air, delays, and occasional failures that erode my trust in it. It feels like a downgrade from what I had before, and that's disappointing.

name: Patrick Foster
q1: It hasn't impacted my work too much either way. The system is fine — it works, it does what it's supposed to do. I wouldn't say it's better or worse than what I've used elsewhere, just different.
q2: Training was good. I appreciated that they took the time to walk through each feature and gave us a chance to practice. I felt ready to use the system when the session was over.
q3: I heard about it but the details were a little scattered. I would have liked a single clear email laying out the full plan rather than bits and pieces over time. Not a major issue, just could have been more streamlined.
q4: It's reliable. I haven't had any real problems with it, and the sound quality is solid.
q5: The transition itself was a bit disruptive to my teaching. Having installers in and around the classroom during school hours was distracting for students. Better scheduling of the installation work would have helped.

name: Steven Turner
q1: I've been through three or four of these system changes now over the years, and this one was about average in terms of disruption. The first couple of weeks were bumpy — the bells were off, the intercom had some quirks — but it's mostly settled down. My workflow took a hit during the transition but it's recovering. I remember the changeover back in 2014 being worse.
q2: The training was passable. I've learned over the years that the real training happens when you're actually using the thing day to day. The formal session gave me enough to get started, but I had to figure out a few things on my own afterward. Not unusual for these rollouts.
q3: Communication was middling. I've seen better rollout communication from the district over the years and I've seen worse. We knew it was happening, but the details were fuzzy. I asked my department chair a few times and even she didn't have clear answers about the installation schedule for our wing.
q4: None
q5: The disruption during installation was poorly managed. I had workers in my room during third period on a Wednesday with no warning. After 22 years here, I'd expect a little more courtesy and coordination. The system itself is adequate, but the rollout left a lot to be desired.

name: Reginald Porter
q1: It's been a negative experience so far. The system is unreliable and it's created more headaches than the old one ever did. I've had announcements cut out, I've had the intercom fail to connect when I needed to reach the front office, and the whole thing has made my mornings more stressful than they need to be.
q2: Training was okay but didn't really prepare me for the issues I've encountered. Knowing how the system is supposed to work is one thing — dealing with it when it doesn't work is another. There was no troubleshooting guidance.
q3: I was aware the change was coming, and the communication was adequate. Emails went out, it was mentioned at meetings. I don't have major complaints about how it was communicated — my issue is with the system itself.
q4: Not much, honestly. I'm struggling to find positives right now.
q5: The reliability needs serious work. A communications system that you can't count on is worse than an old one that works consistently. I'd rather have the old system back at this point.

name: Steven Hall
q1: Overall it's been a neutral-to-slight-positive change. My daily work hasn't been dramatically affected. The new system handles announcements and bells fine, and I've found the workflow to be fairly intuitive once I got used to it.
q2: The training was great. It was one of the better professional development sessions I've attended — well-organized, relevant, and the trainer clearly knew the material. I felt confident using the system afterward.
q3: Communication was satisfactory. I knew the change was happening and had a general sense of the timeline. Could have been a bit more detailed, but it wasn't a problem.
q4: The training and onboarding process was a strength. The support team was responsive when I had a question early on.
q5: The system reliability could be better. I've had a few instances where announcements sounded garbled or the connection dropped. It's not frequent, but it's enough to notice.

name: Deborah Taylor
q1: It's been okay. I'm pretty new so I didn't have a lot of experience with the old system to compare it to. The new one seems to work well enough for what I need — basic announcements and being able to reach the office.
q2: The training was alright. I picked up the main functions but there were some features I still had to look up later. A cheat sheet or quick-start guide would have been a nice takeaway from the session.
q3: I actually felt really well informed about this change. The administration did a good job communicating what was happening, when, and why. I got emails, there were updates at staff meetings, and my team lead checked in with us too. This was well-handled.
q4: It's straightforward and easy to use. The sound is clear.
q5: Some intermittent connectivity issues — the intercom doesn't always pick up right away. Minor, but worth mentioning.

name: Robin Ward
q1: I've been here long enough to have used every communications system this district has ever installed, and this latest transition has actually been one of the smoother ones in terms of day-to-day impact. My routines adjusted fairly quickly. That said, I wasn't convinced we needed a new system to begin with.
q2: The training was well done. I tend to be skeptical of these sessions because they usually don't account for the fact that we have real classrooms to run, but this one was efficiently structured and respectful of our time. I walked away feeling like I could use the new system without issue.
q3: Communication was adequate. We knew it was coming. I would have appreciated more transparency about the cost and the rationale for choosing this particular vendor, but in terms of the logistical details of the rollout, we were kept reasonably informed.
q4: The new system has noticeably improved my daily workflow in terms of ease of use. The controls are more logical than the previous panel I had in room 22.
q5: Reliability has been spotty. For a brand-new system, I'd expect it to work flawlessly, and it doesn't. I've had audio drop out during morning announcements at least three times since installation. After 34 years, I've learned not to trust any new system until it's proven itself over a full school year.

name: Yolanda Martinez
q1: The new system has been somewhat disruptive. It hasn't fundamentally changed what I do, but the reliability issues have added a layer of uncertainty to my mornings. I never know if the announcement is going to come through cleanly or if I'm going to have to improvise.
q2: The training was underwhelming. I've been teaching for 27 years and I've sat through a lot of training sessions, and this one felt like it was designed for people who already understood the technology. It moved too fast and didn't spend enough time on the basics. I left with more questions than answers.
q3: Communication was just okay. I heard about it, but the messaging felt like it was targeted at people who were already on board with the change. For those of us who had concerns or questions, there wasn't a clear channel to raise them.
q4: I can't think of anything specific that's been a clear improvement.
q5: The system cuts out too often and the training didn't prepare me well enough to troubleshoot on my own. I would change the training approach to be more hands-on and less lecture-style, and I would want the reliability issues resolved before asking staff to depend on it.

name: Julia Davis
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The new system isn't bad, but it hasn't made my work easier either. Some of the steps feel unnecessary compared to the old process, and I find myself spending slightly more time on things that used to be automatic.
q2: I felt like the training needed more depth. We covered the surface-level functions but I didn't come away understanding the system well enough to use all its features. I'd appreciate a follow-up session or even just some video tutorials I could watch at my own pace.
q3: The communication about the change was actually handled well. I felt like I had a good understanding of what was coming and the reasoning behind it. I appreciated the transparency from admin on this one.
q4: The emergency alert features seem well-designed. That part I feel good about.
q5: The daily usability could be better. The interface has too many steps for simple tasks like paging the office. Streamlining the most common functions would make a big difference.

name: Oscar Morales
q1: Day to day, the new system hasn't been a major problem, but it hasn't been a big win either. I was open to the change and hoping it would be an improvement, but the reliability issues have tempered my enthusiasm. When it works, it's fine. When it doesn't, it's a pain.
q2: The training was insufficient. It felt like a vendor demo more than actual professional development. I needed more time to practice with the actual equipment in my classroom, not sit in the library watching a slideshow. After 17 years of teaching, I know the difference between useful PD and checking a box.
q3: Communication was fine. I knew it was happening, I knew roughly when, and I understood the general rationale. No complaints there.
q5: The reliability is my biggest concern. A communications system needs to be dependable above all else, and this one isn't there yet. I've also found the training to be inadequate — I'm still discovering functions I didn't know existed because they weren't covered in the session.

name: Reginald Jackson
q1: It's been a bit of a rough transition. The system itself seems capable, but the reliability hasn't been consistent enough for me to fully trust it yet. Some days everything is fine, other days the intercom doesn't respond or the audio is fuzzy. It's created some minor but persistent frustrations in my routine.
q2: Training was fine — nothing exceptional but it got the job done. I learned the core functions and felt prepared enough to start using the system. I wouldn't have minded a second session after a few weeks of actually using it.
q3: Communication was reasonable. We were told about the change ahead of time and given some context about why it was happening. I felt like I had enough information, even if some of the details came later than ideal.
q4: The sound quality when the system is working properly is a clear improvement over what we had.
q5: Consistency is the issue. When the system works, it's good. But the intermittent problems undermine confidence in it. I'd want to see those issues resolved before I'd call this a success.

name: Kimberly Howard
q1: Honestly, it's been a mixed bag. The system itself seems fine when it works, but there have been enough hiccups that it's added some unpredictability to my day. I've had a few mornings where I wasn't sure if announcements actually went through, and that kind of uncertainty is distracting when you're trying to get twenty-five kids settled.
q2: The training was actually pretty solid. I felt like I walked away from the session knowing how to use the basic features, and the handout they gave us has been helpful as a reference. I do wish they'd offered a follow-up session a few weeks in once we'd actually been using it and had real questions.
q3: We got some information beforehand, but the timeline felt vague. I remember hearing it was coming but not really knowing when things would actually change over. A clearer schedule with specific dates would have helped me plan around it.
q4: The sound quality is noticeably better than the old system when everything is functioning properly.
q5: The reliability needs work. There have been random days where the system just doesn't seem to respond the way it should, and nobody seems to know why.

name: Jennifer Park
q1: It's been a smooth transition for me. The new system integrates well with the daily routine, and I appreciate having the updated features. If anything, it's simplified a few things I used to have to do manually.
q2: Training was good. I picked it up quickly and the materials were clear. I think for people who are less comfortable with technology it might have moved a bit fast, but for me it was fine.
q3: Communication was adequate. I knew it was happening and roughly when. I would have liked more detail about exactly which features were changing versus staying the same, but overall I felt prepared.
q4: The interface is more intuitive than the old one, and the audio clarity has improved. I also like that we can adjust volume from the classroom panel now.
q5: Nothing major. Occasionally there's a slight delay between when an announcement starts and when I hear it, but it's minor.

name: Jessica Turner
q1: It hasn't dramatically changed my workflow, but it hasn't been seamless either. Some days it works perfectly and other days I notice odd glitches—bells not sounding at the right time, or the intercom having a slight echo. It's manageable but a little annoying.
q2: The training was okay. It covered the basics but I felt like some of the more practical scenarios—like what to do when something isn't working—were kind of glossed over. A troubleshooting guide would have been useful.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. There were emails and a staff meeting about it. I think a short FAQ addressing the most common concerns would have gone a long way, though.
q4: The ability to send targeted announcements to specific classrooms is a nice upgrade.
q5: The reliability is inconsistent. I've had the system cut out mid-announcement at least twice, and the bell timing was off for the first couple weeks.

name: Irma Reyes
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive, to be honest. The transition period was rough—there were a few days where we weren't sure if the bells were going to ring on time, and that throws off the entire schedule. I've been here long enough to remember the last system change, and this one has been bumpier.
q2: Training was decent but not great. I felt like they showed us the ideal scenario but didn't prepare us for the reality of day-one issues. I would have appreciated more hands-on practice before we went live.
q3: I didn't feel very well informed. It seemed like decisions were made and then we were told after the fact. I remember finding out about the installation timeline from a colleague rather than from admin. More transparency early on would have made a difference.
q4: The system itself is reliable when it's working. I haven't had any major technical failures, and the audio quality is clear.
q5: The rollout was poorly communicated and the disruption to our daily schedules during installation was unnecessary. Better planning would have minimized the impact on instruction time.

name: Noelle Tamura
q1: I'm not convinced we needed a new system in the first place. The old one worked. This one works too, I suppose, but the transition has cost us instructional time and energy that could have been spent on things that actually matter. My classroom routine got disrupted for something that doesn't feel like an improvement.
q2: The training was fine. I attended, I learned the buttons. I don't think the system is complicated enough to require extensive training—which raises the question of why we changed in the first place.
q3: We were told it was happening. I wouldn't call that being "informed." There wasn't much room for input or questions. It felt very top-down.
q4: It works. The sound is clear.
q5: I would change the decision-making process. When you've been teaching for seventeen years, you develop a sense for what's actually going to help students versus what's a shiny new thing that someone in administration wanted to buy.

name: Silvia Ramirez
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The new system works differently enough from the old one that I've had to relearn some habits, and that takes time away from other things. Not a huge deal, but it adds up over the course of a week.
q2: The training covered the essentials but felt rushed. I would have liked a second session after we'd had a chance to actually use the system for a while. By the time I had real questions, there was nobody to ask.
q3: I was aware the change was coming, and the general communication was fine. I just wish we'd been told more about the why behind the switch—understanding the reasoning helps with buy-in.
q4: The system is dependable. I haven't had any technical issues, and the audio quality is a step up from what we had before.
q5: The transition period could have been smoother. There were a few days of confusion about bell schedules that could have been avoided with better coordination.

name: Tony Cho
q1: It has made things harder, frankly. I've had multiple instances where announcements didn't come through clearly or the system seemed to lag. When you're in the middle of a lesson and the intercom cuts in with garbled audio, it's disruptive and frustrating. I've started just ignoring announcements and checking email later, which defeats the purpose.
q2: Training was fine. That's not where the problem is. The problem is that the system itself doesn't work consistently.
q3: Communication about the change was reasonable. I knew what was coming and when. I have no complaints there.
q5: The reliability is the core issue. I've been in education for sixteen years and I've never had a PA system that cuts out as often as this one. Fix the hardware first, then worry about the bells and whistles.

name: Jordan Medeiros
q1: Overall positive. The new system has been pretty easy to adapt to and I think it's an upgrade from what we had. I've been able to use some of the new features, like the zone paging, which is helpful when I need to coordinate with the teacher next door.
q2: Training was solid. Not spectacular, but it gave me enough to get started. I figured out the rest through trial and error, which is how I usually learn these things anyway.
q3: I felt very well informed. The administration did a great job communicating the timeline, the reasons for the change, and what to expect. The emails were clear and the staff meeting covered everything I needed to know. This is how changes should be rolled out.
q4: Zone paging is great. The overall audio quality is improved. The system feels more modern and responsive.
q5: Minor thing—the volume on the classroom speaker is a little too loud at the default setting, and it resets after a power cycle. Would be nice if it remembered my preference.

name: Brian Harris
q1: Since I'm relatively new to the district, I don't have a strong baseline for comparison. The system seems to work okay on most days, though I've noticed a few hiccups here and there. It hasn't dramatically affected my routine one way or another.
q2: I felt like the training could have been better. It moved quickly and assumed some familiarity with the previous system that I didn't have. I had to ask colleagues for help with a few things afterward.
q3: I got the general information but as someone newer to the building, I felt a little out of the loop on some of the specifics. A welcome packet or quick-start guide for newer staff would have been helpful.
q4: The system works reliably for the most part. I haven't had any major issues with it.
q5: Better onboarding for staff who weren't here for the old system. The training assumed everyone had context that I didn't.

name: Irma Vargas
q1: I have been teaching for twenty-seven years and I have been through several of these system changes. This one has been particularly frustrating. The intercom cuts out, announcements are garbled, and I've had mornings where the bell didn't ring and my students didn't know it was time to transition. That is unacceptable.
q2: The training was adequate for what it was, but no amount of training can fix a system that doesn't work properly. I understood how to use it. The issue is that it doesn't perform as advertised.
q3: I was informed. I read the emails and attended the meeting. That part was fine.
q5: Fix the reliability. I should not have to walk down the hall to find out if the bell actually rang. I've put in two work orders about sound quality in my room and have not heard back on either one. In twenty-seven years I have never had to question whether the basic infrastructure of the school was functioning.

name: Emily Morris
q1: The new system has actually been a nice improvement for me. My day-to-day workflow feels smoother—the announcements are clearer, the bell schedule has been consistent, and I appreciate being able to control the volume in my room. It's made transitions between classes less chaotic.
q2: Training was okay. It got the job done but I wouldn't say it was thorough. I had to figure out a couple of things on my own, like how to mute the speaker during testing periods.
q3: I wish we'd had more advance notice about when the actual switchover was happening. I heard about the project in general but the specific dates seemed to shift, and one Monday I just showed up and everything was different.
q4: The classroom volume control is my favorite feature. Also, the overall sound quality is much clearer than the old system. Transitions between periods have been smoother because the bells are more consistent.
q5: The communication around the timeline could have been better. It felt like a moving target.

name: Megan Reed
q1: It's been a bit of a letdown, honestly. I'm used to a system that just worked—you pressed the button, it did the thing, no issues. This one feels clunkier and less reliable. There's been more than one occasion where I wasn't sure if my message actually went through.
q2: The training was not great. It felt disorganized and too short. At my previous district, when they rolled out new systems the training was much more structured and there were follow-up sessions. Here it was kind of one-and-done.
q3: Communication was fine, actually. I knew it was coming and the emails were clear about what to expect. No complaints there.
q4: The audio quality is okay when it works.
q5: Coming from a district where the communications system was more polished, this feels like a step backward. The interface is less intuitive and the reliability just isn't there. I'd like to see more investment in getting the basics right before adding features.

name: Alejandra Gonzalez
q1: I'm new here so I can't compare to the old system, but the new one has been a bit rocky. There are days when the intercom seems to have a mind of its own—static, delays, that sort of thing. I'm excited about the potential of the system though, and I think once the kinks get worked out it'll be great.
q2: Training was helpful. I appreciated that they walked us through everything step by step. Since I didn't have the old system as a reference point, I felt like the session gave me a good foundation.
q3: I felt well informed. The administration was transparent about what was changing and why, and I appreciated the emails leading up to the switch.
q4: I like the modern interface and the fact that we can customize some settings at the classroom level. The emergency alert feature also seems well designed.
q5: The reliability needs improvement. I've had static and delays that make it hard to rely on for time-sensitive communication. But I'm optimistic they'll get it sorted out.

name: David Clark
q1: It's been an adjustment. The system has had its share of issues—inconsistent bell timing, audio that sometimes sounds tinny or drops out for a second. Nothing catastrophic, but when you've been doing this for seventeen years in the same building, you notice when things aren't working the way they should. I remember when they installed the speakers in B wing back in 2015 and those had issues for months too.
q2: Training was serviceable. I learned what I needed to learn. I think they could have spent more time on troubleshooting, though, because that's where most of the day-to-day frustration comes from.
q3: The communication was excellent, honestly. We were kept in the loop at every stage, and I felt like the administration was genuinely trying to keep us informed. That part I have no issues with.
q4: N/A
q5: The reliability of the hardware. Bells should ring on time, every time. Announcements should come through clearly. These are baseline expectations, not premium features. I'd also like to see someone address the intermittent audio dropouts—they're brief but noticeable.

name: Jennifer Moore
q1: It's been mostly fine. There's an occasional glitch but nothing that has seriously impacted my teaching. The transition was smoother than I expected, to be honest. I was bracing for worse.
q2: I thought the training was well done. The presenter was knowledgeable and patient, and the materials they left us with have been a good reference. I've recommended the quick-start card to a few colleagues who missed the session.
q3: Communication was good. I felt informed and I appreciated that they gave us advance notice. It would have helped to have a single point of contact for questions after the switch, though—I wasn't always sure who to ask when something seemed off.
q4: The sound clarity is better than the old system. I also appreciate that the emergency alert features seem more robust.
q5: A dedicated support contact during the first few weeks would have been really helpful. I had a couple of minor issues and wasn't sure if I should submit a work order or just wait.

name: Oscar Delgado
q1: The system has been working well and it hasn't caused any real disruption to my routine. If anything, the daily flow has improved slightly because the bells and announcements are more consistent now.
q2: Training was okay but felt a little surface-level. I would have liked to understand more of the advanced features rather than just the basics. The system seems capable of a lot more than what we were shown.
q3: I was aware it was happening but the details were a bit fuzzy. I'd have appreciated a more detailed breakdown of the timeline and what specifically was changing.
q4: Rock solid reliability. I haven't had a single issue with the system not working when it's supposed to. That's the most important thing and it delivers.
q5: Show us more of what the system can do. I feel like we're only using about half of the features.

name: Karla Chavez
q1: It's been a generally positive change. The new system works well and hasn't been too disruptive. I was a little worried during the installation period because there were a couple days with no working intercom at all, but since it's been up and running it's been solid.
q2: The training was good enough. I felt prepared to use the system afterward. I think offering the session during a prep period rather than after school would have gotten better attendance and engagement from staff.
q3: Communication was so-so. I knew the broad strokes but some of the specifics—like exactly when certain features would go live—were unclear. I found out about some things by accident.
q4: The audio quality is clear and the system has been reliable since installation. I also like that it integrates with the emergency notification system.
q5: Better communication about the rollout details, and maybe a short survey before the change to understand what staff actually needed from the new system.

name: Keisha Dixon
q1: Overall I'd say it's been a positive change. The new system is an improvement and I've been able to integrate it into my daily routine without too much trouble. There are some quirks to figure out but nothing that's been a real barrier.
q2: The training was a little underwhelming. I'm someone who picks up technology quickly, so it wasn't a huge deal for me, but I could see how others might have struggled. The pace was fast and there wasn't much time for questions.
q3: Communication could have been better. I felt like the information came in dribs and drabs rather than as a cohesive plan. I ended up piecing together the timeline from multiple emails and hallway conversations.
q4: The system works well and the audio is clearer than before. I've had good experiences with the zone paging feature, which is new and useful.
q5: More structured communication from admin about the rollout. Also, the training should be repeated for anyone who missed it—I know a few colleagues who never attended and are still figuring things out on their own.

name: Lisa Hall
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. Having been in the profession for almost twenty years, I've worked with several communications systems, and this transition has been less smooth than others I've experienced. The system works, but there are little inconsistencies—delays, occasional static—that add friction to the day.
q2: The training was adequate but not memorable. At my last district, the vendor actually came in and did multi-day training with hands-on practice. Here it felt more like a quick overview. I would have benefited from more depth.
q3: I was informed, but the communication felt generic. It would have helped to know more about how this system compared to alternatives and what specifically was supposed to be better about it.
q4: It does what it needs to do. The basic functionality is there.
q5: I'm used to a system that was more refined and reliable out of the box. This one feels like it still needs some tuning. The audio quality is serviceable but not as crisp as what I had before, and the interface takes more steps to do simple things.

name: Destiny Santiago
q1: It's been a fairly smooth transition. I've had a few minor issues but nothing that's seriously affected my teaching. The new system integrates well enough and I think it will be a good long-term upgrade once everyone is fully comfortable with it.
q2: Training was middle of the road. It covered what we needed but I walked away feeling like there was more to learn. I've been figuring things out as I go, which is fine, but a second session would have been worthwhile.
q3: I felt well informed. The admin team communicated the plan clearly and I appreciated the updates along the way. This was handled better than some past changes I've been here for.
q4: The improved audio quality and the integrated emergency features are both welcome upgrades. It feels like a more modern system overall.
q5: Nothing major. I'd suggest offering an advanced training session for those of us who want to use more of the system's capabilities.

name: Patricia Griffin
q1: It hasn't dramatically changed things one way or another. There are days when the announcements come through fine and days when I feel like I'm waiting on the system to cooperate before I can get back to my lesson. Overall it's been a lateral move for my classroom routine.
q2: The training was adequate. I wouldn't say it was thorough enough to cover all the features, but I picked up the basics and figured out the rest on my own. A follow-up session after a few weeks of use would have been helpful.
q3: I heard about the change through staff meetings, but the timeline kept shifting and it was hard to know exactly when things would be installed in my room. A clearer schedule posted somewhere accessible would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality is generally better than the old system when it's working properly. I also like that the clock display is integrated now.
q5: There have been some inconsistencies with the volume levels throughout the day. Sometimes the afternoon announcements are noticeably louder than the morning ones, and I haven't figured out why.

name: Stephanie Bailey
q1: Day to day, it's been pretty seamless. The system works reliably and I don't spend time worrying about whether announcements will come through. It's one of those things where when it works well you barely notice it, which is exactly what I want.
q2: Training was decent. I felt like I got a solid overview, though I remember a few colleagues had questions that didn't get fully addressed during the session. I would have appreciated a quick-reference sheet to keep in my desk.
q3: Communication about the change could have been better. I've been here a long time and I remember past technology rollouts where we got more detailed updates along the way. This one felt like we heard about it and then suddenly it was happening. More lead time with specifics would have helped.
q4: Reliability has been excellent. I have not had a single instance where the system failed to work when it was supposed to. That's a real improvement over the old intercom that would cut out at least once a month.
q5: Nothing major. I suppose if I had to pick something, it would be that the interface for adjusting classroom volume isn't very intuitive.

name: Marisol Ramirez
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive, honestly. I'm still getting used to this school and adding a new system on top of that has made things feel a bit chaotic. There have been glitches that interrupt my lessons and it takes time to get everyone refocused.
q2: The training itself was fine. The instructor was knowledgeable and covered the material well. I felt reasonably prepared after the session.
q3: I'm newer here so I don't have a great baseline for how communication usually works. I found out about the change at a staff meeting and got a couple of emails. It seemed okay to me, though I know some of the longer-tenured teachers felt blindsided.
q4: N/A
q5: The system has frozen on me twice during class, and both times I had to call the front office to get it reset. That's not acceptable when you're in the middle of teaching. The reliability needs to improve before I'd consider this an upgrade.

name: Laura Harris
q1: I've been through enough of these transitions to know they always come with growing pains. This one has been no different. My daily routine hasn't changed that much, but the little hiccups add up — the bell timing being slightly off threw me for the first couple weeks, and I still notice the occasional audio glitch during morning announcements.
q2: The training was okay but felt rushed. I've been teaching here for over three decades and I remember when we got the last system, we had multiple training days. This time it was one session and you were expected to figure things out. I eventually did, but it took longer than it should have.
q3: Frankly, the communication was poor. I found out details about the installation timeline from the custodian before I heard anything official. For a change that affects every classroom in the building, we deserved better advance notice and more transparency about what to expect.
q4: The emergency alert function seems more robust than what we had before. That matters.
q5: The transition was poorly managed from a communication standpoint, and the bell schedule was off for the first week. I also think the volume in my classroom runs a little hot — I've asked to have it adjusted but haven't heard back.

name: Nico Kealoha
q1: I'm generally open to new technology, so I was looking forward to the upgrade. Unfortunately, the reliability has been hit or miss. Some days are fine, other days there are odd delays or static. My actual workflow hasn't suffered much, though — I can work around most of the issues.
q2: The training felt incomplete. We covered the basics but I wanted to know more about the advanced features and how to troubleshoot common problems myself instead of always having to submit a ticket. I'd welcome a second training session now that we've had time to actually use the system.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. The emails and the staff meeting gave me a decent heads-up. I don't think the communication was exceptional, but it wasn't bad either.
q4: I like the concept and the potential. When it works, the audio clarity is noticeably better than the old system.
q5: The reliability is the main issue. I've had the intercom cut out mid-announcement at least three times. For a brand-new system, that's disappointing. I want to like it, but it needs to be more dependable.

name: Molly Collins
q1: This system has been a huge improvement for my daily workflow. Coming from where I was before, I can't believe how much smoother everything runs. Announcements are clear, the bells are consistent, and I don't have to jerry-rig anything to make it function. It's honestly made my transition to this district even easier.
q2: The training was fine. It covered what I needed. I've dealt with much worse systems where there was no training at all, so I appreciated that the district made the effort.
q3: I felt well-informed. They communicated the timeline and expectations clearly enough. Compared to how things were handled at my previous district, this was refreshingly organized.
q4: Almost everything. The audio quality, the reliability, the integration with the bell schedule — it all works well. I've seen what a bad communications system looks like, and this is not that. I'm genuinely impressed.

name: Stephanie Evans
q1: Since I'm still fairly new to teaching, I don't have a lot to compare it to. The system seems fine for what I need. Announcements come through, bells ring on time. It hasn't really disrupted my routine.
q2: The training was helpful but I felt like it moved quickly. As someone without a lot of experience with these types of systems, I would have liked a bit more hands-on time to practice.
q3: I was informed through emails and the staff meeting. It seemed like enough information to me, though I know I don't have the context that veteran teachers do about what changed.
q4: It's easy to use. I like that I don't have to think about it too much — it just works in the background.
q5: I don't have strong complaints. Maybe a short reference guide for new teachers would be helpful.

name: Cristina Mendoza
q1: Overall it's been a positive change. I was ready for an upgrade and the new system has made my day a little smoother. I can hear announcements clearly and the integration with the schedule display is convenient. I'm glad the district made this investment.
q2: Training was just okay. I felt like I learned the basics but there were features mentioned that we never really got to explore. I've been figuring things out as I go, which is fine, but a more in-depth session would have been welcome.
q3: I felt very well-informed about this change. Administration did a good job with the emails and updates at staff meetings. I knew what was coming and roughly when, which made the transition easier for me.
q4: The clarity of the audio is a big step up. I also appreciate the visual schedule display — I reference it multiple times a day. The whole system feels modern and well-designed.
q5: I wish the training had gone deeper. That's really my only critique.

name: Laura Lewis
q1: It's been an adjustment. The system itself works well enough — reliable, good sound. But the transition disrupted my routines for a few weeks and I'm still not fully comfortable with all the features. It hasn't made my day harder, but it hasn't made it easier either.
q2: The training was honestly not good. It was too short, too surface-level, and didn't give us time to ask questions. I left feeling like I barely knew more than when I walked in. I've had to figure out most things by trial and error, which is frustrating.
q3: Communication was middling. I got some emails but the details were vague. I would have appreciated a clearer breakdown of what was changing, when it would happen in each part of the building, and who to contact with questions.
q4: The system is reliable. I'll give it that. It hasn't gone down on me once, which is more than I can say for some tech in this building.
q5: The training needs a complete overhaul. You can't give people one rushed session and expect them to feel confident. Ongoing support or at minimum a second session after a few weeks would make a huge difference.

name: Diana Sharma
q1: I've been here long enough to remember three different systems, and this one is by far the most reliable. That said, the transition itself was disruptive. For about two weeks my schedule felt off because the bells were slightly different, and it took a while to adjust my classroom routines. I'm enthusiastic about the technology but the rollout could have been gentler on our daily rhythms.
q2: The training left something to be desired. I'm someone who likes to understand a system thoroughly, and the session we got only scratched the surface. I've since gone to the vendor's website and watched their tutorial videos on my own time, which helped. The district should have provided that level of detail from the start.
q3: I heard bits and pieces through staff meetings and emails, but the full picture was never laid out clearly. I would have liked a comprehensive FAQ or a timeline document. After 27 years here, I've learned to fill in the gaps myself, but newer staff shouldn't have to.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. In all the weeks since installation I have not experienced a single failure. The audio quality is crisp and the system responds immediately. That's exactly what you want.
q5: The training was insufficient and the communication around the rollout was scattered. The system itself is great — the process of getting us there was not.

name: Drew Hall
q1: It's been a mixed bag. I'm open to change and I think the district needed to update the old system, but the new one has had enough small issues that my daily routine has been disrupted more than I expected. The occasional glitches during announcements are distracting, and it takes me a minute to get my students back on track each time.
q2: Training was weak. After 33 years I've sat through a lot of technology trainings and this was one of the thinner ones. We needed more time and more depth. I ended up asking a colleague to walk me through several features the training didn't cover.
q3: Communication was fine. Not great, not terrible. I knew it was coming and I had a general sense of the timeline. But for a project this size I would have expected more detailed updates, especially around the installation schedule for individual classrooms.
q5: The training was inadequate and the system has been glitchy. I want to be supportive of this change but the execution has made that harder than it needed to be. More investment in training and troubleshooting support would go a long way.

name: Yolanda Dixon
q1: It's been okay. Not a huge disruption but not a huge improvement either. The system works most of the time, and when it does, it's fine. When it doesn't, it's annoying but manageable. My day-to-day is largely the same.
q2: Training was decent. I felt like I got enough to get started. There were a few things I had to look up later but nothing that caused major problems.
q3: I was somewhat informed. I knew the change was happening but didn't always know the specifics of what to expect or when. A more detailed rollout plan shared with staff would have helped.
q4: The audio quality is better than what we had. The announcements are clearer and I don't have to strain to hear them like I used to.
q5: There are occasional glitches where the system seems to lag. It's not a huge deal but it's noticeable.

name: Roberto Hernandez
q1: The new system has actually been somewhat disruptive to my teaching. The bell schedule adjustments during the transition threw off my class periods, and I had to restructure some of my lesson plans to account for the changes. I'm still recalibrating, and it's been more work than I anticipated for something that's supposed to be an improvement.
q2: Training was good. The facilitator was clear and answered questions well. I felt prepared to use the basic functions of the system after the session. No real complaints there.
q3: This is where things fell short. I didn't feel like we got adequate advance notice about the scope of the changes. The emails were generic and didn't address how things would specifically affect classroom teachers. I would have liked a building-level meeting dedicated to walking through what to expect.
q4: The training was well-run. And when the system is working normally, the sound quality is good.
q5: The communication leading up to the change was lacking, and the disruption to the bell schedule during installation was poorly handled. I'm also still dealing with minor workflow adjustments that could have been avoided with better planning.

name: Roberto Lopez
q1: It's been a smooth transition for me. I'm still relatively new to the profession so I don't have strong attachments to the old way of doing things. The system works and my daily routine hasn't been affected much.
q2: The training covered what I needed. It was straightforward and I didn't have trouble following along.
q3: I felt well-informed. The administration communicated the change clearly and I knew what to expect. The emails were helpful and the timeline made sense.
q4: It works consistently and the announcements are easy to hear. Simple and functional.
q5: Nothing comes to mind.

name: Alejandro Gonzalez
q1: I'll be honest, I'm not usually one to get excited about new technology, but this system has been a clear improvement. Having come from a district where the intercom barely worked half the time, I can appreciate what we have here. My daily workflow is smoother because I'm not constantly dealing with missed announcements or garbled audio.
q2: Training was average. It got the job done but didn't go very deep. I figured out the rest on my own, which I'm used to doing.
q3: I felt informed. The administration gave us a heads-up well in advance and followed up with details as the installation date approached. That was appreciated.
q4: The reliability and audio clarity are both solid. Compared to what I've experienced before, this system is leagues ahead. I don't take that for granted.
q5: I wouldn't change much. The training could have been more thorough, but the system itself is working well.

name: Sofia Chavez
q1: It's been frustrating. I'm used to a system that just worked — you didn't have to think about it. This one has issues almost daily. Static, delays, announcements cutting out partway through. It disrupts my class and I end up having to repeat information to students that they should have heard clearly the first time.
q2: The training was okay but basic. I already had experience with communications systems from my previous school, so I didn't learn much that was new. It would have been more useful to focus on troubleshooting since this system seems to need a lot of it.
q3: Communication about the change was actually pretty good. I got enough advance notice and the emails were clear. No complaints there.
q5: The reliability is a serious problem. The system I had before was far more dependable. I shouldn't be hearing static and dropouts on brand-new equipment. Whatever is causing the issues needs to be diagnosed and fixed. This feels like a step backward from what I had.

name: Elena Ramirez
q1: It's fine. The system works and I don't really think about it that much. I'm not someone who gets particularly invested in this kind of infrastructure change — I just need it to function, and it does. My day-to-day has actually improved slightly since the announcements are clearer.
q2: Training was adequate. Nothing special but it got the job done. I don't feel like I'm missing anything critical.
q3: I felt informed enough. The staff meeting covered the basics and the follow-up emails filled in the gaps. No issues there.
q4: It works. Reliably. That's all I really need from it.
q5: No.

name: Erin Carter
q1: My daily routine hasn't changed dramatically. The system mostly works and announcements come through clearly. I'd say it's been a slight net positive, though not transformative. The biggest adjustment was just getting used to the different tones and timing of the bells.
q2: The training was lacking. We got one session that moved too quickly and didn't cover enough of the details. I've had to ask colleagues and figure things out on my own, which takes time I don't have. A written guide or video tutorial would have been much more useful.
q3: Communication was okay. I knew the change was coming and had a general sense of when. I would have liked more specific information about what features were available and how to access them, rather than just knowing that new hardware was being installed.
q4: The sound quality is clearer than the old system. That's a genuine improvement that I notice every day.
q5: Better training, plain and simple. One session is not enough for something that's supposed to be a significant upgrade. If the system has all these capabilities, show us how to actually use them.

name: Tammy Martin
q1: After 27 years in education, I've been through enough of these transitions to know the drill. This one has been more disruptive than most. The system itself seems decent but the changeover period threw off my timing for a solid two weeks. My class schedule depends on those bells being predictable, and they weren't during the installation. I'm still not fully back to my normal rhythm.
q2: The training was mediocre. I've attended countless professional development sessions over the years and this one felt rushed and underprepared. We needed hands-on practice time, not just a walkthrough. I still don't feel confident using all the features.
q3: Communication was spotty. I heard about it at a staff meeting and then got a few emails, but the details were inconsistent. One email said installation would take a week, it took nearly three. Managing expectations matters, and that didn't happen here.
q4: None
q5: The disruption to daily schedules during installation was excessive and poorly managed. The training should have been more comprehensive. And someone needs to address the fact that the bell timing still feels slightly off compared to the published schedule — it's a small thing but it matters when you're running a tight class period.

name: Heather Clark
q1: The system has not been reliable and that's affected my daily work. I've had announcements cut out, static during important messages, and at least two instances where the system just didn't work at all. Despite that, my overall workflow hasn't suffered too badly because I've learned to have backup plans. But I shouldn't need backup plans for basic building communication.
q2: Training was pretty good, actually. The session was well-organized and the presenter was knowledgeable. I felt reasonably prepared to use the system after the training. That's not where the problem is.
q3: I felt well-informed about the change. Administration communicated the rationale and timeline clearly. I appreciated knowing why the change was being made and what to expect.
q4: The training was solid and the communication around the project was handled well. I also like the concept of the new system and what it's supposed to do.
q5: The reliability is unacceptable. You can have the best training and communication in the world, but if the system itself doesn't work consistently, none of that matters. I've reported issues multiple times and while I get an acknowledgment, the problems persist. This needs to be the top priority.

name: Kelly Taylor
q1: Honestly, the new system has been just okay. It works, but I didn't feel like the old one was broken, and now I have to relearn things I already knew how to do. It hasn't made my day-to-day noticeably better or worse, just different.
q2: The training was actually really thorough. I appreciated the hands-on sessions, and the follow-up materials were helpful when I needed to reference something later. That part was well done.
q3: I heard about the change kind of late. By the time I understood what was happening, the installation was already underway. An earlier timeline or some kind of FAQ would have helped me feel more prepared.
q4: The training resources were excellent. If nothing else, I feel like I was given the tools to figure the system out.
q5: I just wish we'd had more of a heads-up before everything changed. The rollout felt rushed from my perspective, even if maybe it wasn't.

name: Antonio Martinez
q1: I've been teaching over twenty years and I've been through a lot of these system changes. This one hasn't disrupted my teaching much, which I suppose is the best I can say. The workflow is about the same, maybe slightly better for announcements.
q2: The training felt like it was designed for people who've never used a PA system before. I sat through two sessions that could have been emails. For those of us who've done this before, something more targeted would have been appreciated.
q3: Communication was adequate. I got the emails, I read them. Nothing caught me off guard.
q4: It hasn't really gotten in the way, which at this point in my career I consider a win.
q5: The system cuts out occasionally, and I've had announcements come through garbled a couple of times. Not a dealbreaker, but it shouldn't be happening with brand new equipment.

name: Beth Mitchell
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some mornings the system works fine, other mornings I'm not sure if the announcements even went through to my room. I've had to walk down to the office twice this month to confirm messages I wasn't sure I heard correctly. That's time I don't have.
q2: The training was underwhelming. I remember sitting in the library for an hour and a half and walking out still confused about how to use the intercom from my classroom phone. I eventually asked a colleague to show me, which worked better than the official session.
q3: I felt pretty blindsided, honestly. I've been here over twenty years and I found out about this from a flyer in the staff lounge. There should have been a staff meeting or at least a direct conversation with people who've been here this long. We have institutional knowledge that could have helped the process.
q4: N/A
q5: The reliability is inconsistent. Some days it's fine, other days there's static or the volume drops. And the lack of communication before the change was disrespectful to long-term staff.

name: Carlos Romero
q1: The new system hasn't really changed my workflow much. It's pretty comparable to what I was used to, so the transition was smooth for me. I don't have strong feelings either way.
q2: Training was fine. I picked up the basics pretty quickly. I think a short reference card would have been nice for the less obvious features, but overall it was sufficient.
q3: I wish there had been a bit more advance notice. I'm still fairly new here so I wasn't sure if I was missing information or if everyone was in the same boat. Turns out we all were.
q4: It's reliable enough. Does what it needs to do.
q5: Nothing major. Just wish the communication about the change had been a little more proactive.

name: Karla Rodriguez
q1: This system is honestly a huge improvement over what I've experienced before. The audio is clear, the announcements come through consistently, and I can actually hear everything from anywhere in my classroom. At my previous school we were lucky if the intercom worked at all, so this feels like a real upgrade.
q2: The training could have been better. It was pretty surface-level and I had to figure out some things on my own. I'm comfortable with technology so it wasn't a big deal, but I can see how others might struggle.
q3: I felt well-informed about the change. The emails were clear and the timeline made sense. I appreciated knowing what to expect before the installation started.
q4: The sound quality is great. Coming from a district where the PA system was basically held together with tape, this is night and day. The emergency features seem really solid too.
q5: Just the training piece. I think a second session after people had actually used the system for a week would have been really valuable.

name: Danielle Harris
q1: It's been a slight disruption. The bells don't always line up with when I expect them, and there have been a few times where announcements interrupted lessons at awkward moments. I'm adjusting, but it's been an adjustment.
q2: Training was average. It covered the basics but I still don't feel confident using some of the features. I'd say I use maybe 60% of what the system can do.
q3: The communication was okay. Not great, not terrible. I knew it was coming but I didn't have a clear picture of how it would affect my classroom specifically.
q4: The call button feature is nice for reaching the office quickly.
q5: The volume in my room seems inconsistent. Sometimes it's too loud during quiet work time, and I haven't figured out if there's a way to control it from my end.

name: Quinn Collins
q1: After nearly three decades in this district, I've seen systems come and go. This one works reasonably well, and the transition was smoother than the last time we upgraded. My day-to-day hasn't changed dramatically, which is probably what you want to hear.
q2: The training was decent. I've sat through much worse over the years. I do think they assumed a certain level of comfort with technology that not everyone has, but I managed.
q3: I would have liked more communication upfront. I remember when we switched phone systems in 2014 there was a whole committee and a series of meetings. This felt like it was decided and then we were told about it. A little more collaboration would have gone a long way.
q4: The system itself is solid. I haven't had any real technical issues, and the sound quality is good in my room.
q5: The rollout communication. After this many years I've learned that teachers handle change better when they feel included in the process, not just informed after the fact.

name: Naomi Fonseca
q1: As a new teacher, I don't have much to compare it to, but the system seems to work well enough. It hasn't gotten in the way of my teaching, and the announcements are clear.
q2: The training was really good. They walked us through everything step by step, and I felt comfortable using the system by the end of the session. The online resources were helpful too.
q3: I felt well-informed. They communicated the timeline clearly, and I knew what to expect. Being new, I appreciated not having to guess about logistics.
q4: The clarity of the audio is nice. And the training materials are still available online which is great for when I forget something.
q5: Sometimes there's a slight delay or the system seems to hiccup, but it's minor. Nothing I'd call a real problem.

name: Marisol Chavez
q1: It's been disruptive, frankly. The transition period was rough, and even now I feel like I'm still working around the system rather than with it. Announcements come at odd times, and I've had to restructure parts of my day to accommodate interruptions that didn't happen before.
q2: The training was passable. It covered the mechanics but didn't address how to integrate the system into an actual teaching day. I needed practical tips, not a product demo.
q3: I got the emails but they were vague. I remember asking my principal specific questions and she didn't have answers either, which wasn't reassuring.
q5: The timing of announcements needs to be rethought. There should be designated windows so teachers can plan around them. Right now it feels random, and that's disruptive when you're in the middle of a lesson.

name: Karla Ortiz
q1: It's been a neutral change for me overall. Some things are slightly better, some are slightly more annoying. I wouldn't say it's dramatically impacted my work one way or the other.
q2: Training was okay. I felt like one session wasn't quite enough, but I also didn't have major questions afterward. It was adequate.
q3: Communication was middling. I knew the change was coming but I didn't have much detail about what it would actually look like in practice until it was already happening.
q4: The system is generally reliable and the sound quality is decent.
q5: I'd like more control over volume levels in my classroom. And the announcement scheduling could be more predictable.

name: Molly Davis
q1: Coming in as a new hire, I didn't have the old system to compare it to here. The current setup works fine and is pretty similar to what I've used at other schools. No complaints about the day-to-day impact.
q2: The training was good. I appreciated that they offered it to new staff even though the system was already installed. It was well-organized and I felt prepared afterward.
q3: Since I started after the installation, I can't speak to the pre-change communication. What I've seen since arriving has been fine.
q4: It works. The sound is clear, the intercom is easy to use, and I haven't had any technical issues.

name: Tiffany Jackson
q1: The new system has been mostly fine, but I notice small things. The tone of the bells is different, the timing feels slightly off from what I was used to, and there's a learning curve with the intercom controls that I'm still navigating after a few months.
q2: Training was adequate. Not exceptional, not terrible. I learned enough to get by. I do wish there had been a follow-up session after we'd had a chance to use the system for a few weeks, because that's when the real questions came up.
q3: The communication could have been better. I've been here eighteen years and I felt like I was hearing about the details at the same time as people who just started. Some acknowledgment that veteran staff might have specific concerns would have been nice.
q4: The intercom clarity is an improvement over the old system. I'll give it that.
q5: More follow-up after installation. The initial training was fine, but once we started actually using it day to day, I had questions that went unanswered for a while.

name: Irma Ortiz
q1: The system has actually streamlined a few things for me. Morning announcements are clearer and I spend less time repeating information to students because they can actually hear what's being said. Overall a positive change for my workflow.
q2: Training was solid. I felt prepared to use the system right away, and the materials they gave us were helpful. I've been through enough of these transitions to know that this one was handled better than most on the training side.
q3: Communication was just okay. I heard about the change through the grapevine before any official announcement, which isn't ideal. Once the official communication started it was fine, but the initial rollout of information was slow.
q4: The audio quality is noticeably better. Students can actually hear announcements clearly, which saves me time explaining what was just said.
q5: Nothing major. The initial communication about the project could have started sooner.

name: Patricia Morris
q1: It's been a slight improvement in some ways but the system does glitch periodically. I've had announcements cut off mid-sentence a couple of times, and occasionally there's feedback. Nothing catastrophic but it interrupts the flow of class.
q2: Training was decent. Covered the essentials. I didn't walk away with any burning questions, which I guess means it did its job.
q3: I actually felt well-informed about this change. The emails were clear and came with enough lead time that I could prepare mentally. That part was handled well.
q4: None.
q5: The reliability needs improvement. For a brand new system, it shouldn't be cutting out or giving feedback. I'd also appreciate being able to adjust the volume from my room.

name: Francisco Rivera
q1: It hasn't made my life harder, but the system is not as reliable as I'd expect from something new. I've had days where the announcements just don't come through, or they're so staticky I can't make out what's being said.
q2: The training was lacking. I walked out of the session with more questions than answers. The presenter seemed to be reading from a script and couldn't answer questions about specific classroom setups. I ended up just figuring it out through trial and error.
q3: The communication about the change was fine. I got the notices and understood the timeline. No issues there.
q4: When it works, the sound quality is better than the old system.
q5: Reliability is the big one. A new system should not be this inconsistent. Some days it works perfectly, other days it's like it's not even on. I've submitted two work orders already and nothing has changed.

name: Brian Wong
q1: Honestly, the system works well. It does what it's supposed to do without getting in the way of teaching. I don't spend time thinking about it, which is how it should be.
q2: Training was fine. Nothing special. I would have liked more advanced tips since the basic stuff was pretty intuitive, but I understand they had to cater to all levels.
q3: I didn't feel like the communication was great. I found out about some of the features by accident weeks after the installation. A more detailed overview of what the system can actually do would have been helpful upfront.
q4: It just works. I haven't had a single technical issue since it was installed. The sound is crystal clear every time.
q5: Better upfront communication about all the features. I'm still discovering things the system can do that nobody told us about.

name: Brandon Howard
q1: It's been an adjustment. Not a dramatic one, but enough that I noticed. The new tones and notification sounds are different, and the intercom protocol changed, so I had to retrain some habits. Day-to-day impact has been minor.
q2: The training was good. I felt like they took the time to explain things properly and answered questions when people had them. No complaints there.
q3: Communication was a little sparse. I heard rumors about the change before I got any official word, which is never a great sign. Once the official communication came it was fine, but the gap between the rumor mill and the actual announcement was too long.
q4: The training was well-structured and the trainers were knowledgeable.
q5: The system occasionally has minor glitches — nothing serious, but enough to be noticeable. And the pre-announcement communication could have been handled better.

name: Karen Lee
q1: I've been pleasantly surprised by the new system. It's intuitive, the audio is clear, and the transition was smoother than I expected. I'm someone who generally welcomes new technology, and this one has delivered on its promises so far.
q2: Training was good. Thorough without being overwhelming. I appreciated that they had different sessions for different comfort levels with technology. Smart approach.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. The emails were helpful, and the principal mentioned it at a couple of staff meetings before the installation. A dedicated info page or FAQ would have been a nice touch, but the communication was solid overall.
q4: The sound quality is great, the system is easy to use, and I love that we can do targeted announcements to specific areas instead of blasting the whole school every time.
q5: Minor thing — it would be nice to have a quick-reference guide posted near the intercom controls in each room. I know the info is online, but having it right there would save time.

name: Diego Aguilar
q1: Compared to what I had before, this system is a significant step up. The announcements are clear, the coverage is consistent, and everything just feels more modern and intentional. I'm genuinely impressed with the quality, even if it has some quirks I'm still learning.
q2: The training was okay. It got the job done but I've seen better onboarding for systems like this. A quick-start guide would have been more useful to me than the full walkthrough, since I've worked with similar systems before.
q3: Communication was poor, honestly. I'm new to the district so maybe I missed some earlier conversations, but I basically showed up and the system was being installed with very little explanation. Had to ask around to figure out what was going on.
q4: The system itself is solid. Way better than what I had at my last school. The coverage alone is impressive — no dead spots, no crackling. It's a real upgrade from what I'm used to.
q5: The communication about the change. I'm all for new technology — I'd just like to know about it before it shows up. Even a welcome packet for new staff explaining the system would have gone a long way.

name: Micah Nakamura
q1: The transition has been pretty smooth. I noticed a few small improvements — the announcements are clearer and I like having the ability to initiate a call to the office from my room. Overall positive impact on my daily routine.
q2: Training was well done. I felt like they covered everything I needed and the pace was right. Having the online resources to go back to afterward was a smart move.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew it was coming and had a rough sense of the timeline. A little more detail about what specifically would change in my classroom day-to-day would have been helpful, but it wasn't bad.
q4: The training was excellent and the audio quality is a clear improvement. Being able to reach the office directly from my room is a nice feature I use regularly.
q5: No major complaints. The only thing I'd change is providing more detail in the pre-installation communication about what teachers should expect on a practical level.

name: Andrea Parker
q1: The new system has been a mixed bag for my day-to-day routine. I appreciate the idea of having updated technology, but the reliability issues have been frustrating. There have been several mornings where the intercom just didn't work properly, and I had to send a student down to the office to get information I should have heard over the speaker.
