It's created small disruptions that add up over the course of a week.
q2: Training was okay overall. We had one session before the system went live, which covered the basics, but I felt like there were features we didn't get to explore. A follow-up session a few weeks in would have been really helpful once we actually had questions from using it daily.
q3: Communication about the change was adequate. I knew it was coming and roughly when, but some of the specifics about what would change in terms of daily procedures were a bit vague. A simple one-page summary of what's different and what to expect would have gone a long way.
q4: The new paging features are an improvement. Being able to do a targeted page to specific classrooms instead of a building-wide announcement is genuinely useful.
q5: The system cuts out or has static fairly often. I've reported it a couple of times but haven't seen much improvement yet. Consistency is really what I need from a communications system, and right now it's not there.

name: Christine Morris
q1: Honestly, this system has made things easier for me. I've worked in districts where the intercom barely functioned, so having something that actually works consistently is a relief. The bell schedule integration is smooth and I don't have to second-guess whether announcements went through. It's been a positive change for my workflow.
q2: The training was fine but not exceptional. I picked things up quickly because I've had to learn worse systems with even less support. I think for people who aren't as comfortable with new technology, the training could have been more hands-on. But for me personally, it was sufficient.
q3: I didn't feel super well-informed about the timeline beforehand. I heard about it mostly through hallway conversations rather than any official communication. That said, the transition itself was smooth enough that it didn't cause real problems. An email with key dates and what to expect would have been nice.
q4: The audio quality is so much better than what I'm used to. Clear announcements, reliable bells, and the emergency alert functionality feels much more robust. Coming from what I had before, this is a significant upgrade.
q5: I don't have major complaints. If I had to pick something, I'd say the volume levels in the hallway seem a bit loud compared to inside classrooms, but that's minor.

name: Jose Castillo
q1: It's been kind of disruptive. Not terrible, but there were a few weeks where nobody really knew what was going on with the bells and announcements.

q3: I found out about the new system pretty late. Would've liked more of a heads-up so I wasn't caught off guard when things started changing.
q4: It works most of the time now.
q5: The rollout felt rushed. More communication to support staff specifically would help next time.

name: Jason Baker
q1: I've been here a long time and we've been through system changes before. This one hasn't really changed my day much one way or the other. The old system worked fine for what I needed it for.

q3: They told us it was happening. Could have been more detail about when exactly rooms would be affected during installation, since I had to move my small group out of Room 14 one day with no warning.
q4: It seems solid so far. No real complaints about the hardware.
q5: I didn't really see the need for a whole new system. The old one did what it needed to do.

name: Christine Wu

q4: The sound quality is clear.

name: Carolyn Sullivan

name: Leila Tamura
q1: It's been a smooth transition for me. The new system works great and I haven't had any issues with it affecting my work.

name: Rachel Parker

name: Brenda Williams
q1: My daily work has actually improved a bit. I can hear announcements more clearly and the bell schedule is more consistent now. The transition was a little rocky but I think we're in a better spot.

name: Linda Rao

name: Emily Baker

name: Todd Edwards

name: Camille Kealoha
q1: It's been a mixed experience. q3: I felt like I was left in the dark on a lot of this. The communication was really poor — no timeline, no updates, things just happened. Where I came from, there was a whole communication plan for any major change. This felt chaotic by comparison.

q5: The reliability is the big issue. And the communication from district about the project was significantly worse than what I've experienced elsewhere. This should have been planned out better.

name: James Park
q1: It's alright. The new speakers and wiring are fine. Doesn't really change what I do much, I still get my calls on the radio.
q2: Training was basic. They showed us the panel in the custodial office. It was enough.
q3: They told us it was coming. That was about it.
q4: No.
q5: Some of the junction boxes they installed in the utility corridor are hard to access if I need to get behind them. Would've been nice if they'd consulted us on placement.

name: Christopher Turner
q1: Not much different for me. The old system and the new system both work about the same from my end. Had a couple times the page didn't come through clearly but nothing major.
q2: Didn't really get trained. Someone showed me the new panel for about ten minutes.
q3: I knew about it. They mentioned it at our department meeting ahead of time, which was good.
q5: The page volume in the gym area could be louder. Hard to hear it over the HVAC sometimes.

name: Eric Foster
q1: Works fine. Does what I need it to do.
q2: Barely any training. They handed us a sheet of paper and that was it.
q3: Heard about it beforehand. Could have gotten more info on what they were actually installing and where.
q4: The hardware seems solid. Good equipment from what I can tell — the speakers and amp units are decent quality.
q5: They should have asked facilities staff where to run the conduit. Some of it is going to be a pain when we need to do ceiling tile work in B wing.

name: Guillermo Rivera
q1: It's made things harder honestly. The panel in the main office is not intuitive and I've had trouble figuring out zones when I need to make announcements for after-hours stuff.
q2: Barely any. They showed us one time for like twenty minutes and that was it. I still don't know half the buttons.
q3: I heard about it from other staff mostly. An email would have been nice explaining what was changing and when.
q4: The sound is clearer than the old one I guess.
q5: The training was a joke. Need more hands-on time with the actual equipment, not just a quick walkthrough.

name: Rachel Peterson
q1: Hasn't changed much for me day to day. The system works fine, I just do my job same as before.
q2: Training was okay. Could've used a little more detail on the maintenance side of things, like how to reset a speaker if it goes out.
q3: They let us know it was coming. I would've liked a timeline posted somewhere so we knew which areas would be torn up and when.
q4: It's reliable. Haven't had any issues with it cutting out or anything like that in the eight months it's been in.
q5: Nothing major. I'm not someone who loves change but this one hasn't caused problems.

name: Patricia Collins
q1: It's been okay. Pretty similar to what I had at my last place so I picked it up quick.
q2: The training was short. I already knew how most of this stuff works from before so it wasn't a big deal for me, but I could see it being tough for someone new to it.
q3: I'm still pretty new here so I wasn't really around for the lead-up. People filled me in.
q4: Works well, no complaints on the hardware side.

name: Ricardo Flores
q1: Honestly it's been a smooth transition. I can hear pages clearly in the portables now which is a big improvement. Makes my rounds easier when I actually know what's going on.
q2: It was alright. Average. Got the basics down but wouldn't have minded a follow-up session.
q3: Communication was decent. My supervisor told us about it ahead of time and we got an email from the principal.
q4: Rock solid so far. The old system used to drop out in Building C all the time and this one doesn't.
q5: I'd want a cheat sheet posted by the main panel with the zone codes. I still have to look them up every time.

name: Deborah Phillips
q1: Not a huge difference for me personally. I'm all over the building anyway so I hear the announcements wherever I am. It's a little easier to use than the old system.
q2: Training was just okay. I've been doing this a long time and I pick things up, but a refresher down the road would be helpful.
q3: We knew it was coming for a while. I think they communicated it well enough. I remember the principal mentioned it at a staff meeting back in the spring.
q4: Very dependable. I appreciate that because the old one would go down and then I'd get called to troubleshoot something I couldn't fix anyway.
q5: N/A

name: Elena Lopez
q1: It's fine. Works better than the old one. I can actually get my work done without stopping to fiddle with equipment that isn't working.
q2: Not great. They did one session and I missed it because I was covering the cafeteria that day. Nobody followed up.
q3: I felt a little out of the loop. I heard bits and pieces but nobody sat down with our team specifically.
q4: It just works. That's all I need.
q5: Better communication with the custodial crew. We're the ones who have to deal with the wiring and panels and we were the last to know.

name: Janet Parker
q1: It's been frustrating. The system cuts out in the gymnasium hallway and near the loading dock. I've reported it twice and nothing's happened. I've been here twelve years and the old system at least covered those areas.
q2: The training was rushed. They crammed everything into one session and half of it didn't apply to what I actually need to do.
q3: Terrible. I found out they were installing new equipment when I showed up one morning and there were contractors in the hallway pulling cable. Nobody told us anything beforehand.
q4: The touchscreen panel is easier to read than the old buttons, I'll give it that.
q5: Fix the dead spots. And actually tell your facilities people when you're going to have contractors in the building. We need to know that stuff for safety reasons if nothing else.

name: Jason Howard
q1: During the install our lunch periods got moved around three different times in two weeks. I've been here almost thirty years and I've never had that kind of disruption to our schedule. We had kids showing up when we weren't ready.
q2: Didn't really get trained. Nobody came to the kitchen.
q3: We were not informed. Found out about the schedule changes day-of most of the time.
q5: Next time there's a project like this, someone needs to coordinate with food service BEFORE changing bell schedules. We prep food based on when kids eat. You can't just move lunch around.

name: Megan Turner
q1: The bell schedule kept shifting during installation and it threw everything off. We'd have food ready and then find out first lunch got pushed back twenty minutes.
q2: I don't think we were included in the training. I wouldn't really need it for my job anyway.
q3: Nobody told us a thing. I've been in this district sixteen years and this is the worst communication I've seen on any project. We heard about schedule changes from teachers, not from administration.
q4: The system itself sounds fine I guess.
q5: Keep food service in the loop when you change bell schedules. That's really all I'm asking.

name: Patricia Ramirez
q1: The schedule changes during the switch-over were rough but we got through it. Now that it's settled things are back to normal mostly.
q2: Didn't really apply to us. We don't use the system directly.
q3: They told us about the new system ahead of time which was fine. I just wish they'd been clearer about how it would affect lunch periods during installation.
q4: I like that the announcements are clearer now. We can actually hear them in the kitchen.
q5: Nothing right now.

name: Ebony Simmons
q1: It was a little bumpy when they were putting it in. The lunch schedule kept changing and we had to adjust on the fly. It's calmed down now though.
q2: It was fine. Basic but fine.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. I'm still fairly new here so I just go with the flow.
q4: It works.
q5: No.

name: Alma Estrada
q1: The installation period was hard on us. Lunch times got moved and we had to scramble. Now that it's done it doesn't really affect me much.
q2: The training was good. Short and to the point.
q3: They communicated okay. I knew the system was coming.
q4: Announcements are louder and clearer in the cafeteria.

name: Brenda Taylor
q1: Things have settled down now but the rollout was messy. Where I worked before, they handled a system change much more smoothly — the scheduling part especially.
q2: Average. I've had better onboarding for this kind of thing at my previous job.
q3: It was fine. They let people know. I just feel like at my last district there was more of a plan in place for how it would affect everyone's daily routine, not just the teachers.
q4: Day to day now it's okay. The bells are consistent again so I can plan my prep.
q5: I'd change the way they roll things out. There needs to be a plan for every department, not just classrooms.

name: Nova St. Claire
q1: The bell schedule changes during installation really messed us up. Kids coming at random times, we're trying to have food ready. It's been better since they finalized everything but those few weeks were bad.
q2: Basically none. Nobody trained the kitchen staff.
q3: I knew the system was being replaced but nobody told us the bells would be all over the place during the transition.
q4: It does work reliably now that it's in.
q5: More heads-up when schedules are going to shift. Even a day's notice would help us prep properly.

name: Charles Patel
q1: Honestly it's been a real headache. The lunch schedule was a mess during installation — first lunch moving from 10:45 to 11:15 one day and back the next. I'm open to new things but this rollout was chaotic for the kitchen.
q2: No one trained us. At all.
q3: Could have been better. I heard about schedule changes from the teachers most of the time.
q4: The system itself seems solid. Clear sound.
q5: Give food service staff advance notice of any bell schedule changes. We can be flexible but we need to know ahead of time, not the morning of.

name: James Sullivan
q1: The schedule disruptions during the changeover were annoying but manageable. I've been through system changes before at my last district and they gave us a lot more warning about timing shifts.
q2: It was okay. Didn't really cover what food service needs to know.
q3: Not good. I'm relatively new here but even at my old district they communicated this kind of thing much better. We'd get memos, timeline updates, the whole thing.
q4: System works well. Reliable.
q5: Communication to support staff needs to be way better. I shouldn't be hearing about a schedule change from a parent.

name: Kiara Tamura
q1: The lunch timing kept changing and it was confusing. I'm still pretty new to this job so I was trying to learn the normal routine and it kept shifting on me.
q2: There was a training but it didn't really cover anything I needed. It was more for teachers and office staff.
q3: I got some emails I think. I wasn't totally sure what was going on with the timeline.
q4: The announcements are easy to hear now.
q5: Maybe include food service in the planning next time. We got left out.

name: Silvia Garcia
q1: It threw off our whole routine. Bell schedules changed multiple times and nobody bothered telling us in the kitchen until the day of. I've been here fifteen years, we've never had this much disruption.
q2: Training was decent for what it covered.
q3: Very poorly communicated to our department. Administration sent emails to teachers but food service was an afterthought. A simple heads-up about schedule changes would have made all the difference.
q4: None
q5: I would change who gets included in the communication chain. Food service, custodial — we matter too. We're not invisible.

name: Robert Phillips
q1: It's fine now. The transition period was rough with the bell schedules bouncing around but that's over with.
q2: It was short. Got the basics.
q3: They communicated the big picture fine. Just didn't trickle down to us when the day-to-day stuff changed.
q4: Reliable. No complaints there.
q5: Nothing.

name: Tammy Thompson
q1: It's been a mixed bag. I appreciate the emergency alert capabilities — knowing that we have a dependable system for lockdowns and safety situations gives me real peace of mind. But the intercom interruptions during student sessions have been a significant issue. I work with students on very sensitive matters and an unexpected announcement in the middle of a session can completely derail the conversation and the trust we've built in that moment.
q2: The training was actually quite good. They walked us through the emergency protocols thoroughly and I felt prepared. I would have liked some guidance on whether there's a way to set a do-not-disturb mode for counseling offices, but overall it was well organized.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. There were staff meetings and emails. It would have been helpful to have a conversation specifically with counseling staff about how the system would interact with our confidential work spaces.
q4: The safety and emergency features are excellent. The system is clear, it reaches everywhere, and I trust it will work when we need it most. That matters enormously in this role.
q5: There needs to be a way to mute or delay non-emergency announcements in counseling offices. When I'm in a session with a student who is disclosing something difficult, an intercom blast about a schedule change is more than an inconvenience — it's genuinely harmful to the process. I'd love to see a priority-only mode for spaces like ours.

name: Kenneth Shah
q1: The system itself is solid and I appreciate that it works every time we need it, especially for safety drills and emergency alerts. That said, the intercom has become a real disruption in my work. I meet with students who are sharing very personal things, and when an announcement cuts in mid-session it breaks trust and takes time to rebuild the moment. The emergency features give me peace of mind, but the day-to-day interruptions have made scheduling around announcements a new part of my workflow.
q2: Training was decent. I felt like I learned what I needed to operate the system, though I would have appreciated more guidance on how to set do-not-disturb or mute functions during counseling sessions, if that's even possible.
q3: Communication was fine but not great. I heard about the change mostly through staff meetings. It would have been helpful if someone had specifically consulted with counseling staff about how the intercom schedule might affect confidential sessions.
q4: The emergency alert functionality is genuinely excellent. I feel much more confident in our lockdown and safety protocols now.
q5: There needs to be a way to silence or delay non-emergency announcements in counseling offices. Having an intercom blast during a session with a student in crisis is not acceptable.

name: Keith Anderson
q1: It's been a mixed bag honestly. I do individual and small group work with kids and the announcements come through at the worst times. On the other hand, the safety features are a step up. But the workflow disruption is real — I've had to rearrange my entire schedule to avoid peak announcement times.
q2: The training was okay, nothing special. I've used similar systems before so I picked it up. I think a shorter refresher format would have worked better than the full session they gave us.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well informed ahead of time. I found out details mostly from colleagues rather than official communications. A heads-up email specifically about what would change for support staff would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality is clear and the emergency features are well designed.
q5: I'd change the announcement scheduling. Counselors and psychologists should have the option to opt out of routine announcements during session blocks.

name: Kai Kealoha
q1: I'm still pretty new to this role and this district, so I don't have a lot to compare it to. The system works fine technically but the intercom going off during my sessions with students has been disruptive. I'm trying to build rapport with kids and a loud announcement kind of kills the vibe.
q2: Training was alright. I felt a little lost on some of the more advanced features but the basics were covered. I think I'd benefit from a follow-up session now that I've actually been using it for a while.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. There were emails and a staff meeting about it. Being new, I didn't really know what the old system was like, so the transition wasn't jarring for me personally.
q4: I like that the emergency alerts are clear and immediate. That's reassuring as someone still getting used to safety protocols here.
q5: It would be great if there was a setting to delay routine announcements for certain rooms. My office is small and the speaker is very loud for the space.

name: Sarah Foster
q1: The system is very reliable, I'll give it that. It works every single time. But it has definitely impacted my day-to-day in a negative way because I can't control when announcements come through my office. I do sensitive work with students and families and the interruptions are a problem.
q2: Training was kind of lacking. I felt like they showed us the basics but didn't really address the features that would be most relevant to people in my role. I still don't know how to adjust the volume in my office.
q3: The communication about the change was okay. I knew it was coming and roughly when. I just wish they had asked for input from counseling staff beforehand.
q4: It's dependable. The safety alert system is excellent and I never worry about whether it will work when we need it.
q5: Volume controls at the room level. That's all I want.

name: Yolanda Chavez
q1: I've been doing this work for almost thirty years and I've seen a lot of systems come and go. This one functions well enough, and the safety piece is a genuine improvement over the old system we had since probably 2009 or so. But the intercom interruptions during counseling sessions remain a sore point. I've adapted my schedule around it, but I shouldn't have to.
q2: The training was adequate. At this point in my career, I can figure most things out, but some of the younger staff seemed to need more hands-on practice. One session wasn't enough for everyone.
q3: Communication was middling. I've been through enough of these transitions to know that the district doesn't always loop in the people who actually use the systems daily. A quick meeting with support services staff would have been appreciated before installation.
q4: The emergency alert capability is stronger than anything we've had before. During our last drill, everything worked exactly as it should, which hasn't always been the case over the years.
q5: After all these years, I still think someone needs to sit down with counselors and psychologists and figure out a protocol for non-emergency announcements. We deal with kids in crisis and interruptions matter.

name: Rafael Gonzalez
q1: I was actually excited about getting a new system because the old one was showing its age. And I do think the technology is good. But the workflow impact for counselors has been rough. I've had announcements come through in the middle of conversations about abuse disclosures, grief, you name it. The safety features are fantastic but the routine interruptions are a real problem for the work I do.
q2: The training was not sufficient for my needs. It was very general and geared toward classroom teachers. Nobody addressed how counselors or specialists might use or be affected by the system differently. I had to figure out most things on my own.
q3: I knew the system was being installed and I was generally informed about timelines. But the communication felt one-directional. Nobody asked what our needs were.
q4: The emergency features are top-notch. Clear, immediate, and reliable. I genuinely feel safer.
q5: Give counseling offices a mute button for non-emergency announcements. Or at minimum, reduce the frequency of routine intercom use. Not everything needs to be broadcast.

name: Sierra Delacroix
q1: Overall the system is working well and I appreciate the upgrade. The intercom quality is much better. My main concern is the impact on my counseling sessions — I work with students on social-emotional issues and the announcements break the flow of those conversations. I've started scheduling around the typical announcement windows but that limits my availability.
q2: Training was good. I felt prepared to use the system and the session was well organized. I would have liked a follow-up training a few weeks in to address questions that came up with actual use.
q3: I felt somewhat informed but would have liked more advance notice about the specific changes to how announcements would work. The timeline communication was fine but the details about daily operations were sparse.
q4: Audio clarity is a big improvement and the safety alert integration gives me confidence in our emergency preparedness.

name: Patricia Johnson
q1: The system is a noticeable improvement in terms of reliability. I remember years when the old PA would cut out during announcements or you couldn't hear anything in certain parts of the building. That's not an issue anymore. But as a counselor who's been here almost twenty years, I can say the intercom interruptions during sessions are a real concern. I've adjusted, but it took some time and some difficult moments with students.
q2: The training was solid. I appreciated that it was hands-on rather than just a slideshow. It covered what I needed to know and I felt comfortable with the system by the end.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I felt informed about the general plan and timeline. What I would have found helpful is a conversation specifically with counseling and support staff about how the system would work in our spaces.
q4: Reliability and audio quality are both strong. The emergency features are well integrated and I trust them.
q5: I'd like to see a do-not-disturb feature for counseling offices during session times. Even a simple visual indicator outside the door that links to the system would help office staff know not to page us.

name: Rosa Morales
q1: Honestly, it's been frustrating. I've been in this district almost twenty years and I feel like every time there's a change, counselors are the last to be consulted. The system itself is fine — it works — but the intercom interruptions during my sessions have been a significant problem. I had a student shut down completely last month after an announcement startled them during a difficult conversation.
q2: The training was inadequate. It was one session, it was rushed, and it didn't cover anything specific to how the system affects people who do confidential work. I had to ask colleagues for help with basic functions afterward.
q3: I felt blindsided, frankly. I heard rumors in the hallway before I got any official communication. For a change this significant, I would have expected at least a dedicated email well in advance, not a mention at a staff meeting two weeks before installation. This is not how you roll out a major change.
q4: The safety features work well. I'll acknowledge that.
q5: Everything about the communication and planning process. And the lack of volume control or mute options for offices where confidential work happens. This should have been thought through before installation.

name: Beth Martin
q1: The new system has been a net positive for the school overall and I can see the improvements in audio quality and coverage. For my specific work as a counselor, the intercom interruptions during sessions are a challenge, but I've been through enough system changes over the decades to know that these things get worked out eventually. I've adjusted my schedule and it's manageable.
q2: Training was fine for what it was. I've picked up enough systems over 30-plus years that I can figure things out. Newer staff may have needed more.
q3: The communication could have been better. I found out details piecemeal rather than through a coherent rollout plan. Given how long I've been here, I'm used to this, but it doesn't make it right. A simple FAQ document sent out a month ahead would have helped.
q4: The emergency alert system is a genuine upgrade. Audio quality throughout the building is much improved.
q5: Better communication planning for future changes, and some kind of accommodation for offices where private student meetings happen.

name: Hector Sandoval
q1: It hasn't been great for me. The system works I guess, but the constant interruptions during my sessions are a problem. I'm not someone who loves change to begin with, and this has just added stress to my day. I've had to completely rethink my scheduling to try to avoid announcement windows.
q2: Training was just okay. Average. It covered the basics but nothing more.
q3: I was informed at about the same level as everyone else. It was fine. Nothing stood out as particularly helpful or particularly lacking.
q4: N/A
q5: The intercom needs some kind of mute or hold option for counseling spaces. The current setup doesn't account for the kind of work we do.

name: Leticia Gonzalez
q1: The system has been problematic. I've had instances where the intercom made a strange buzzing noise, and there have been a couple times when announcements didn't come through at all in my office. When it does work, the interruptions during student sessions are disruptive. I've been doing this for over thirty years and this is one of the less reliable systems I've worked with.
q2: The training was pretty good actually. The trainer was knowledgeable and patient, and I felt like I walked away with a reasonable understanding of how to use the system. The problem isn't training — it's that the system doesn't always cooperate.
q3: Communication was okay. I knew it was happening and when. I would have liked more information about the specific features and how they might affect different staff roles.
q4: When the emergency alerts have been tested, they've worked. The training itself was well done.
q5: The reliability needs to improve. I've reported issues twice and I'm still waiting on resolution. Also, counseling offices need the ability to silence routine announcements.

name: Karla Castillo
q1: Day to day, it's been okay. The system works well enough and I appreciate the improved safety features. The interruptions during student sessions are annoying but I've adapted. It hasn't dramatically changed my workflow for better or worse.
q2: The training felt rushed and didn't really address the needs of non-classroom staff. I'm a counselor, not a teacher — the way I interact with the system is different and that wasn't acknowledged.
q3: Communication was lacking. I heard about it from a colleague before any official announcement reached me. More proactive outreach would have been appreciated, especially for support staff who might be affected differently than classroom teachers.
q4: The system is reliable and the audio quality is good. Safety features are well implemented.
q5: Better pre-rollout communication and some consideration for how the system impacts counseling and confidential work.

name: Lisa Sharma
q1: The system works well technically but it's been disruptive to my counseling work. I've been in this building for fourteen years and the old system had fewer routine announcements. The new system seems to have increased the frequency of intercom use, which means more interruptions during sessions. The safety features are a welcome improvement though.
q2: Training was below average. It was one size fits all and didn't address the specific concerns of counseling staff. I left with questions I still haven't gotten answered.
q3: I didn't feel well informed. The information trickled out in bits and pieces. After fourteen years here, I expect better communication about changes that affect my daily work. A clear timeline with specific details about what would change and when would have made a big difference.
q4: Emergency alert system is reliable and well designed.
q5: The announcement frequency has gone up and that's a problem for those of us doing confidential work. I'd also like to see better communication from administration about future changes to the system.

name: Sharon Park
q1: The new system has been a meaningful improvement for our site operations. Communication across the building is more efficient, and the ability to target announcements to specific areas has been valuable. It has streamlined several of my daily tasks.
q2: The training was adequate, though I think our classified and support staff could have used an additional session. For administrative purposes, I felt prepared.
q3: I would have appreciated more detailed communication earlier in the process, particularly regarding the installation timeline and how it would affect daily operations. The information we did receive came a bit late for my planning purposes.
q4: The zone-based announcement capability and the integration with our safety protocols have both been excellent additions.
q5: The initial communication to site leaders about the rollout timeline could have been more robust. I also think our counseling staff have valid concerns about intercom interruptions that we should address.

name: Kiara Nakamura
q1: As a relatively new administrator at this site, the transition has been smooth from my perspective. The system has been a helpful tool for managing daily communications and I've been able to integrate it into my routines without much difficulty.
q2: I thought the training was well structured and thorough. The facilitator was responsive to questions and I left feeling confident in using the system for my administrative needs.
q3: I felt adequately informed about the change. The communication through district channels was timely and I appreciated the updates during the process.
q4: The overall reliability and the professional quality of the audio. It presents well to families and visitors.

name: Monique Freeman
q1: The system has improved our operational workflow, and I appreciate that. However, I have experienced some reliability concerns — there have been a few instances where the system didn't respond as expected, and during one drill the response time was slower than I would have liked. Overall it's been positive for daily operations, but the reliability question does give me pause.
q2: Training was satisfactory. I would recommend a follow-up session for administrative staff specifically, as our use cases differ from classroom teachers. The initial session was appropriately comprehensive.
q3: Communication about the transition was handled reasonably well. I was kept in the loop on timelines and major decisions. I would have appreciated more granular detail about the technical specifications and capabilities so I could plan accordingly.
q4: The workflow improvements are real. Being able to communicate more efficiently across our campus has saved time and reduced the need for runners or phone calls.
q5: The reliability needs to be consistent. In my role, I cannot have a system that works most of the time — it needs to work all of the time, especially for safety-related functions.

name: Sofia Flores
q1: From an operational standpoint, this system has been a significant upgrade. My daily workflow is noticeably more efficient — I can reach staff and classrooms quickly and the integration with our schedules has been seamless. It's one of the better improvements we've made in recent years.
q2: The training was appropriate for my needs. I appreciated the practical, hands-on approach. I do think the district could offer periodic refreshers as new features are rolled out.
q3: Communication could have been stronger at the site level. I felt informed as an administrator through district channels, but I think the broader staff felt less included in the process. More site-level communication would have helped.
q4: The efficiency gains are substantial. The system has reduced the number of steps I need to take for routine communications and the emergency integration is well thought out.
q5: I have some concerns about the technical reliability — we've had intermittent issues that required IT support. I'd also like to see better guidance on announcement protocols to address concerns from our counseling team.

name: Kimberly Turner
q1: The system has improved our daily operations and I can see the potential. That said, having come from a district where the communications system was more mature and reliable, I notice gaps here that I wouldn't have expected. The workflow is better than what was here before, but the system itself feels like it still has some growing pains to work through.
q2: Training was adequate but not exemplary. I've experienced more comprehensive onboarding for similar systems in the past, including role-specific sessions and ongoing support resources. The single-session approach here felt limited.
q3: Communication was acceptable. I was informed of the basics. In my experience, a rollout like this benefits from a more structured communication plan with stakeholder input sessions, which I didn't see here.
q4: The improvement over the previous system at this site is clear. The workflow integration has been positive.
q5: The reliability is not where it should be. I'm accustomed to systems that simply work without needing repeated troubleshooting. I would also advocate for a more structured rollout process for future technology changes, including input from site leaders before decisions are finalized.

name: Gabriela Reyes
q1: The new system has made a real difference in how efficiently I can manage daily communications across our site. After thirty years in this district, I can say this is a meaningful operational improvement. I'm able to reach people faster and coordinate more effectively throughout the building.
q2: The training was underwhelming. I expected more depth given the scope of the change. I've been through many system transitions over the years and this was one of the thinner training efforts I've seen. A second session would have been beneficial.
q3: I felt reasonably informed through the usual district channels. The communication was adequate, though as someone who has been here for three decades, I would note that our staff generally respond better when they feel consulted rather than just notified.
q4: The operational efficiency gains are significant. This is the most reliable communication tool we've had at this site.
q5: The reliability has been inconsistent and that concerns me. For a system this important, we need it to work every time. I would also push for better training going forward — one session for a district-wide change is not sufficient.

name: Rachel Howard
q1: It has been a mixed bag honestly. The system itself is fine in concept, but I can barely hear the announcements in my classroom. I've been here long enough to remember the old intercom working perfectly in room 14, so this is frustrating. I find myself stopping instruction to walk to the hallway just to catch what was said.
q2: Training was okay but felt rushed. They crammed everything into one afternoon session and expected us to just figure out the rest. I would have appreciated a follow-up session after we'd actually used the system for a few weeks.
q3: I honestly felt blindsided. I've been in this district over thirty years and I found out about the switch from a colleague in the parking lot. For a change this significant, an email blast and a staff meeting mention is not enough. A timeline sent out well in advance would have gone a long way.
q4: The emergency alert function seems solid. I do appreciate that it's integrated rather than a separate system.
q5: The audio quality on my end of the building is unacceptable. Announcements are muffled or just cut out entirely. I've put in two work orders and nothing has changed.

name: Kevin Evans
q1: Day to day it's been mostly positive for workflow. I like the idea of the new system and I think once they iron out some kinks it'll be great. The one thing that bugs me is I keep getting garbled audio from the speaker in my room — it sounds like it's underwater sometimes.
q2: Training was solid. They walked us through everything step by step and the handout they gave us was actually useful, which is rare. I felt prepared to use the system on day one.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew it was coming and roughly when. Maybe a bit more detail on the specific timeline for our building would have helped, but overall no complaints.
q4: The interface for the teacher call button is intuitive and I like the digital integration. It feels modern.
q5: The speaker in my classroom is clearly not working right. The sound crackles and fades in and out. I'm tech-savvy and enthusiastic about the upgrade but the hardware needs to actually work properly for it to matter.

name: Quinn Martinez
q1: It has made things harder, to be frank. I cannot rely on hearing any announcement that comes through the speaker in my room. I've started keeping my door open so I can hear from the hallway, which is disruptive to my students. After twenty-five years of teaching I have never had to develop workarounds just to hear basic information.
q2: The training itself was actually quite good. The facilitator was knowledgeable and patient with questions. I have no complaints about the training portion.
q3: I was informed reasonably well. The principal mentioned it at a staff meeting and there were a couple of emails. I would have liked to know more about the installation timeline for specific parts of the building so I could plan around the disruption.
q5: The system is simply unreliable in my area of the building. Dead silence during announcements, then suddenly it blares to life mid-sentence. I have reported this multiple times. Students in my class have started joking about it, which tells you how frequent the problem is.

name: Michelle Lewis
q1: Overall the workflow improvements have been noticeable. Being able to communicate with the office more efficiently is a real plus. My one concern is the audio quality — I sometimes can't make out what's being said over the intercom in my classroom, which means I miss things.
q2: Training was well done. They gave us plenty of time to practice and the materials were clear. I felt like they respected our time.
q3: It was adequate. I heard about it ahead of time but some of the specifics — like when exactly the installation would happen in our wing — weren't communicated until the last minute.
q4: The two-way communication feature is a significant improvement over what we had. I also think the system looks much more professional.
q5: The sound quality in my classroom is not great. Announcements come through muffled and I often have to ask a neighbor what was said. I don't think it's a system-wide issue because other teachers seem fine, so it might be specific to where I am in the building.

name: Kai Kaur
q1: Honestly the new system has been a net positive for my workflow. Everything is more streamlined and I appreciate the upgrade. The only hiccup is I can't always hear announcements clearly — there seem to be dead spots near my room.
q2: Training was good. Clear, practical, and not overly long. I appreciated that they had someone available for questions afterward too.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. Could have used a bit more lead time but it wasn't a surprise or anything.
q4: The digital features are great. Love the integration and the overall modernization.
q5: Just the audio issue in my part of the building. Sometimes announcements are completely inaudible and I have to rely on colleagues to relay information.

name: Jun Kim
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. The sound from the new speakers near my room is inconsistent — sometimes clear, sometimes barely audible. I end up checking email more frequently to make sure I didn't miss an announcement, which takes time away from planning and instruction.
q2: The training felt insufficient. It was one session that moved too quickly, and I left with more questions than answers. I would have preferred written documentation I could refer back to at my own pace.
q3: Communication was mediocre. I knew a change was happening but didn't have a clear sense of when or what to expect during the transition. More frequent updates during the installation process would have helped.
q4: The concept is good. I can see the potential if the technical issues get resolved.
q5: Audio quality in my classroom is poor. I've talked to colleagues in other parts of the building and they don't seem to have the same problem, so something is clearly off with the hardware on this side.

name: Amy Reed
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The system is fine overall but the speaker near my classroom has issues — the audio cuts in and out, which means I miss portions of announcements regularly. Otherwise, the day-to-day impact has been manageable.
q2: The training was pretty good. Not perfect, but I felt like I had enough to get started. Having a quick-reference guide would be a nice addition.
q3: I felt very well informed throughout the process. Administration did an excellent job communicating the timeline, the reasons for the change, and what to expect. This was one of the better-communicated changes I've experienced here.
q4: I appreciate the updated look and feel. The system is clearly more capable than what we had before.
q5: The speakers in my area of the building need attention. Audio is unreliable — sometimes it works perfectly, other times I can barely hear anything. I've mentioned it to the office and I know I'm not the only one in this part of the building with the issue.

name: Greg Parker
q1: The workflow impact has been slightly positive. I like the new features and the system feels like a genuine upgrade. The one nagging issue is speaker quality in my classroom — it's hit or miss whether I can actually hear what's being announced.
q2: Training was average. It covered the basics but I felt like there were features that were glossed over. A second session or a video tutorial would have been helpful.
q3: I was well informed ahead of time. The communication from admin was clear and timely. I knew what was coming and when.
q4: The ability to send targeted messages to specific classrooms is a real improvement. That alone makes the upgrade worthwhile in my view.
q5: Speaker reliability in my room. Some days it works fine, other days the sound is so faint I don't even realize an announcement was made until someone mentions it.

name: Sofia Ortiz
q1: From a workflow standpoint things have actually improved. I do like the new features and the system has potential. But the audio problems in my area of the building are a real issue. I've been teaching in room 22 for years and the old system was crystal clear. Now I have to practically shout to confirm what was said over the intercom.
q2: Training was fine. Not the best I've ever attended but serviceable. I picked up what I needed to know.
q3: I wish there had been more communication ahead of time. I heard rumors before anything official was announced, which is never a good sign. By the time we got formal notice, decisions had clearly already been made without teacher input.
q4: The emergency features are a definite improvement. The lockdown protocol integration gives me more confidence.
q5: The speakers on this end of the building are not functioning correctly. Multiple teachers in our hallway have the same complaint. The audio is distorted and unreliable. This needs to be addressed as a priority.

name: Paul Martin
q1: It's been okay. The system itself seems capable but the sound quality in my classroom is poor. Announcements are hard to make out and I've missed a few important ones already.
q2: The training was really lacking. I'm relatively new to the building and I felt like the training assumed a level of familiarity I didn't have. It was too short, too fast, and there were no follow-up resources. I basically had to figure things out on my own.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I knew the change was happening.
q4: N/A
q5: Audio issues in my part of the building and the complete lack of adequate training. Both need to be fixed.

name: Emily Phillips
q1: It's been disappointing. I came from a district where the communications system just worked — you never had to think about it. Here, I can't even hear announcements in my classroom half the time. It feels like a step backward from what I'm used to, and that's been an adjustment.
q2: There was essentially no training. I got a one-page handout on my first day and that was it. At my previous district, we had a full orientation session for new staff that covered all building systems. The lack of onboarding here was shocking.
q3: I was told about the new system during my hiring process, so I knew it was coming. The details were vague though. A more structured rollout plan shared with staff would have been helpful.
q4: I can see the potential. The system has good bones.
q5: The sound quality in my classroom is terrible — muffled, cutting out, sometimes completely silent during announcements. And the training situation needs a complete overhaul, especially for staff who are new to the building. I shouldn't have to ask five colleagues how things work.

name: Laura Phillips
q1: It's been a moderate adjustment. The workflow changes are manageable but the audio quality in my room leaves something to be desired. I often catch only fragments of announcements and have to piece together what was said.
q2: Training was adequate. Nothing exceptional, but I got what I needed from it. After thirty years in education, I've seen plenty of system changes and this training was about average.
q3: Communication was good. I appreciated the heads-up and felt like admin was transparent about the timeline and reasons for the change.
q4: The updated interface is cleaner and more intuitive than the old system.
q5: The speaker in my classroom doesn't produce clear audio. It's been an ongoing issue since installation and I'd like to see it resolved.

name: Diane White
q1: My day-to-day hasn't changed dramatically, but the unreliable audio in my classroom has created a new problem I didn't used to have. I now keep my classroom door propped open during announcement times, which disrupts the learning environment. I've been in this building a long time and this is the first system where I've had to create workarounds like this.
q2: The training was decent. It covered what we needed and the trainers were responsive to questions. I don't have major complaints there.
q3: I felt underinformed. There were whispers about the change for months before anything official came out. When the announcement finally came, it felt like a done deal with no opportunity for input. After twenty-five years at this school, I would have appreciated being consulted.
q5: The speakers in my part of the building are clearly defective. The sound is either garbled or nonexistent. I've submitted work orders and spoken to the principal directly. This has been going on for months now with no resolution.

name: Lorena Hernandez
q1: Overall it's been a positive change for my workflow. I have to say, this system is significantly better than what I had at my previous district — we didn't have half these features. There are some audio quirks in my classroom but compared to what I've experienced before, this is a major upgrade.
q2: Training was satisfactory. It covered the essentials. I've been through worse system rollouts so my expectations were calibrated accordingly.
q3: Communication was fine. I felt informed enough to know what was happening and when. Nothing felt like a surprise.
q4: So much. The digital integration, the two-way communication, the emergency features — I've worked in districts that had none of this. I'm genuinely impressed with the capabilities even if there are a few things to work out.
q5: The audio in my classroom could be better — sometimes it's hard to hear clearly. But honestly, compared to what I came from, I almost hesitate to complain. It would be nice if they could check the speaker though.

name: Andrew Lin
q1: It has not gone well. The speaker in my classroom is essentially nonfunctional. I miss announcements constantly and have had to rely entirely on colleagues or email to stay informed. That is not acceptable for a brand new system.
q2: Training was middling. It wasn't terrible but it didn't leave me feeling confident either. More hands-on practice time would have helped.
q3: I was not adequately informed. The change seemed to come out of nowhere. One day we had the old system, the next we were told the new one was being installed. No meaningful advance notice, no opportunity for questions before it happened.
q4: Nothing.
q5: Everything about the audio in my part of the building. The speaker does not work. It is either completely silent or produces sound so distorted it's useless. This is a fundamental failure that should have been caught during installation. I am genuinely frustrated that we are months in and this hasn't been fixed.

name: Teresa Ruiz
q1: The workflow improvements are noticeable and I appreciate the upgrade in that regard. But the speaker in my room has persistent audio issues that undercut the benefits. I can't hear most announcements clearly, which means I'm frequently out of the loop.
q2: The training was underwhelming. It felt like a box-checking exercise. I left without a solid understanding of several features and had to figure things out through trial and error.
q3: Communication was okay. Not great, not terrible. I knew it was coming but would have liked more detail about what specifically would change for us day to day.
q4: The overall concept and the updated features are good.
q5: The audio issues in my classroom. I can barely hear announcements and it's been this way since the system went live. I've reported it but haven't seen any improvement.

name: Diana Chen
q1: It's been a neutral to slight adjustment. The system works but I haven't noticed a dramatic improvement in my daily routine. I'm open to new technology and I can see the potential, but so far it hasn't been transformative for me personally.
q2: Training was a bit thin. I'm comfortable with technology so I managed, but the session could have gone deeper. Some of my less tech-comfortable colleagues seemed lost by the end.
q3: I was informed but not thoroughly. There was basic communication about the change happening, but I would have appreciated more context about why this particular system was chosen and what the long-term vision is.
q4: I like the modern interface and the potential for integration with other school systems. It feels like a platform we can build on.
q5: The system occasionally glitches — audio drops out briefly or there's a delay. It's not catastrophic but it's noticeable. I'd also like to see better documentation for the features we haven't been trained on yet.

name: Kiara Fonseca
q1: It has been a slight improvement overall. The system feels more capable and I've found the new features helpful for daily communication with the office. No major disruptions during the transition.
q2: The training was excellent. It was well-organized, the pace was appropriate, and the trainer clearly knew the system inside and out. I felt very prepared afterward. This is how all technology rollouts should be handled.
q3: Communication could have been better. I knew the change was coming but the specifics were vague until close to the installation date. Earlier and more detailed communication would have reduced anxiety among staff.
q4: The training, genuinely. Also the two-way call feature and the improved sound quality compared to our ancient previous system.

name: Irma Delgado
q1: It has genuinely improved my day-to-day. The system is reliable and the features are practical. I spend less time dealing with communication logistics and more time focused on instruction, which is exactly what you'd want from an upgrade like this.
q2: Training was good but not outstanding. It covered the main functions well. I think an advanced session for those who want to use the system's full capabilities would be a welcome addition.
q3: I felt somewhat underinformed leading up to the change. The actual rollout was smooth, but the communication beforehand was sparse. I would have liked a clear FAQ or information sheet sent home to staff well in advance.
q4: The reliability has been impressive. It just works, consistently. The sound quality is clear, the features are intuitive, and I haven't experienced any significant downtime.
q5: No

name: Susan Nelson
q1: It's been a positive change for my workflow. Things run more smoothly and the system is fairly reliable. I don't have strong feelings about it either way, but it's an improvement.
q2: Training was lacking. It was too brief and didn't cover enough of the practical day-to-day use cases. I had to figure out several things on my own, which was frustrating. A written guide or video would have made a big difference.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well informed. The communication was minimal — a brief mention at a staff meeting and then suddenly the installation was happening. More transparency about the process and timeline would have been appreciated.
q4: The system itself is solid. It works when I need it to and the sound quality in my room is good.
q5: The training and communication around the rollout. The system is fine but the human side of the change management was weak. We deserved better preparation.

name: Holly Morris
q1: It hasn't changed things dramatically for me. The system works, and I can get announcements out when I need to. Honestly, I'm still getting used to everything since I'm fairly new here, so it's hard to compare.
q2: The training was solid. I felt prepared to use the basic features after the session, and the handout they gave us has been helpful when I forget something.
q3: I heard about the change when I started, so it was already in progress. I think they communicated it reasonably well, though I would have liked a bit more heads-up about the timeline for when things would actually be switched over.
q4: The ability to send targeted announcements to specific areas is useful. I like not having every single message blast through the whole building.
q5: I don't love change for the sake of change, to be honest. The old system seemed to work fine from what people tell me. But I don't have major complaints about this one either.

name: Lisa Moore
q1: I actually think it's been a nice upgrade! The system is really reliable and I haven't had any issues with it cutting out or anything like that. It integrates well into my routine.
q2: Training was good. I picked it up pretty quickly and the trainer was helpful with questions. I think the materials could have been a little more detailed for the advanced features, but overall no complaints.
q3: I knew it was coming but the specific details were a little vague. It would have been nice to know exactly which features were changing and when, rather than just a general 'new system coming soon' announcement.
q4: The sound quality is excellent and the system has been very dependable. I appreciate that it just works when you need it to work.

name: Ricardo Sandoval
q1: I've been through several system changes over the years, and this one has been relatively smooth. Day to day, it works well enough. The transition period was a bit bumpy — I remember a couple of days in October where we had no intercom at all — but it's settled in now.
q2: The training was actually better than I expected. I've sat through plenty of these over the decades and this one was practical. They showed us the panel, we practiced, and we moved on. I appreciated that approach.
q3: Communication could have been better. I found out details through the grapevine more than through official channels. After 27 years here, I know who to ask, but newer staff probably felt left in the dark. A simple timeline sent to everyone would have gone a long way.
q4: It's a capable system. The audio clarity is noticeably better than what we had before, and the emergency lockdown feature is well-designed.
q5: I wish they had consulted teachers earlier in the process. We use these systems every single day and could have flagged some practical concerns before installation rather than after.

name: Matthew Turner
q1: Honestly, the workflow side of things has been fine — my daily teaching hasn't really been disrupted. But the system itself is hit or miss. Some days it works great, other days I get crackling or delays. It's frustrating because you never know what you're going to get.
q2: The training was underwhelming. It felt rushed and they glossed over some of the features that we actually need to use regularly. I left that session with more questions than answers. I ended up figuring most of it out on my own, which I shouldn't have had to do.
q3: I was completely blindsided by this change. After 24 years at this school, you'd think someone would loop us in before just ripping out the old system. I heard about it maybe a week before they started tearing things apart. That's not communication, that's a memo. We deserved to be part of the conversation, not just informed after the fact.
q4: N/A
q5: The rollout was handled poorly. No meaningful teacher input, almost no advance notice, and a training session that didn't actually prepare us. The system hardware works okay when it works, but the process of getting here left a bad taste in my mouth.

name: Rafael Morales
q1: It's been okay. Not a huge difference from what we had before, honestly. There have been a few glitchy moments where the intercom didn't pick up right away, but nothing that derailed my day.
q2: Training was decent. I felt like I got the basics down, and the follow-up email with instructions was a nice touch. Wouldn't have minded a refresher session a month or two in, though.
q3: I heard about it at a staff meeting but the details were pretty thin. I would have appreciated more specifics about what was actually going to change and how it would affect us in the classroom. The communication felt a bit surface-level.
q4: The new emergency alert feature is good. Feels more responsive than the old system.
q5: The reliability could be better. I've had a few times where I tried to make an announcement and nothing happened. Also, the interface on the wall panel isn't the most intuitive.

name: Henry Wang
q1: Not a major impact one way or the other. It works reliably, which is the main thing. I just use it for basic announcements and it does the job.
q2: The training was fine. I would have liked a little more time to practice with the system during the session, but the instructor covered the essentials.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. They sent out communications ahead of time and discussed it at our staff meeting. No real complaints there.
q4: It's dependable. I haven't experienced any outages or issues, which I appreciate.
q5: I'm not sure we really needed a whole new system. The old one seemed to work. But I don't have specific complaints about this one.

name: Ryan Collins
q1: It's been a positive change overall. My day-to-day flow hasn't been disrupted much, and I like some of the new features. There were a few hiccups early on with connectivity but those seem to have been resolved.
q2: Training was great. The trainer was knowledgeable and made it easy to follow along. I felt confident using the system right away, which doesn't always happen with new tech rollouts.
q3: Communication was adequate. I knew it was happening and roughly when, but some of the specifics about the installation timeline were unclear. A couple of times, work was happening in my hallway and I wasn't told it was coming that day.
q4: I really like the new interface. It's more modern and the touch panel is intuitive once you get the hang of it. The sound quality is also a step up from the old system.
q5: The reliability in the first few weeks was spotty. I understand that's normal for a new installation, but it was stressful during that period. Would have been nice to have a backup plan communicated to us.

name: Maria Rivera
q1: The new system has been a nice improvement for my daily routine. Being able to quickly reach the office or send a message without leaving my classroom is more seamless now. It's been especially helpful during morning arrival when things are hectic.
q2: The training covered the basics well enough, but I wish there had been more on the advanced features. I found out about some useful functions weeks later just by poking around. A follow-up session would have been valuable.
q3: We were told it was coming, but the timeline kept shifting. I remember being told one date, then hearing a different date the following week. It wasn't the end of the world, but clearer scheduling would have reduced some of the stress around it.
q4: The system is rock solid. I haven't had a single failure or dropped connection in the months since installation. That reliability gives me a lot of confidence, especially for safety situations.
q5: Nothing

name: Emily Lewis
q1: It's been a mixed bag. The system itself hasn't been as reliable as I'd like — I've had multiple instances where the intercom just didn't work when I pressed the button. That's concerning. On the other hand, when it works, the daily routine is about the same as before.
q2: Training was okay, nothing special. They covered the basics but I felt like it could have been more thorough. I've had to troubleshoot things on my own a few times that probably should have been covered.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well-informed. There were emails, sure, but they were vague. After being here for 11 years, I've learned to just roll with changes, but it would be nice to actually feel like they want our input rather than just our compliance.
q4: The emergency notification system seems like a genuine improvement. The old one was clunky.
q5: Reliability needs work. When I press the call button, it needs to work every time. I've also noticed some audio feedback issues in the afternoons that are disruptive during instruction.

name: Sarah Mitchell
q1: It's fine. Not dramatically better or worse than before. I use the intercom a couple times a day and it works. My workflow hasn't really changed because of it.
q2: Training was good. I felt prepared afterward and the materials they provided have been a useful reference. I've been teaching long enough to know that hands-on practice matters more than slides, and they did give us that.
q3: The communication was somewhat lacking. I remember hearing about it at a staff meeting in passing, but I never got a clear timeline or explanation of why we were switching. I would have appreciated more transparency about the decision-making process.
q4: Sound clarity is better than the old system. Announcements are easier to hear.
q5: I just wish we'd been consulted. Teachers are the primary daily users of this system and we had zero input into the selection.

name: Gary Howard
q1: After 31 years, I've been through enough system changes to know that the first year is always rough. Day to day it's been mostly fine. There were some dead periods during installation where we had nothing, and I had to send a student runner to the office a couple of times, which was not ideal.
q2: The training was lacking. I've used many different systems over the years and I expected more depth. They showed us the basics and moved on. For those of us who actually rely on these systems daily, a surface-level overview doesn't cut it.
q3: Communication was poor. I found out the installation schedule from the custodian, not from administration. That tells you something. I would have liked to see a clear project plan shared with staff — dates, expectations, contingency plans.
q4: The hardware itself seems solid. Good build quality and the sound is clear when it's working properly.
q5: The training and communication around this whole project were subpar. The actual system is decent, but the rollout process left a lot to be desired.

name: Alejandro Rivera
q1: It's been disruptive, frankly. The system doesn't always respond when I need it to, and during the transition period my instruction was interrupted constantly by installation noise and testing. I lost valuable class time. My students were distracted and I felt like my concerns about the timing were ignored.
q2: The training was insufficient. One session, maybe 45 minutes, and we were supposed to be good to go? I've been teaching for 22 years and I know what adequate professional development looks like — this wasn't it. There were features mentioned that were never demonstrated.
q3: I was barely informed. An email buried in a Friday newsletter does not count as communication. This was a major infrastructure change affecting every classroom in the building, and it was treated like a minor update. I deserved to know the rationale, the timeline, and the expected disruption well in advance.
q5: Almost everything about the process was poorly handled. The training was a joke, the communication was nonexistent, and the system reliability has been inconsistent at best. I'm open to new technology — I really am — but this rollout did not inspire confidence.

name: Michelle Cooper
q1: It's been a really positive change. The system is smooth, reliable, and has actually made a few things easier — like being able to quickly communicate with the front office without leaving my students. I feel like my daily workflow has genuinely improved.
q2: The training was thorough and well-organized. I walked away feeling confident about using all the main features, and the follow-up resources have been helpful too. This was one of the better technology trainings I've attended.
q3: I felt very well-informed throughout the process. Administration sent regular updates, discussed it at staff meetings, and was available for questions. This is how change management should be done.
q4: Everything, honestly. The sound quality is excellent, the interface is intuitive, and the emergency features give me genuine peace of mind. It's been reliable every single day.
q5: I really can't think of anything major. Maybe more advanced feature training down the line would be nice, but that's a minor ask.

name: Cynthia Brown
q1: It hasn't changed much for me honestly. I use the intercom when I need to and it works most of the time. Not a huge impact on my teaching one way or the other.
q2: I didn't feel well-prepared after the training. It was too brief and didn't cover enough of the practical scenarios I actually encounter. I've had to ask colleagues for help figuring out certain functions, which shouldn't be necessary.
q3: Communication was middling. I knew it was happening but didn't feel like I had the full picture. A more detailed FAQ or informational sheet would have been helpful, especially for those of us who had specific questions about how it would affect our classroom setup.
q4: The system works reliably for the most part, which is the baseline expectation. The audio quality is decent.
q5: Training needs to be more comprehensive. One short session isn't enough for a system we use every day. I also think getting teacher feedback before selecting the system would have been appropriate.

name: Amy Evans
q1: I'm used to a system that was much more seamless than this. At my last district, the communications system was essentially invisible — it just worked and you didn't think about it. Here, I feel like I'm constantly aware of the system, and not in a good way. It's been a noticeable step backward in terms of daily workflow.
q2: The training was pretty bare-bones. At my previous school, we had multiple sessions spread over a few weeks with hands-on practice time built in. Here it felt like a one-and-done approach, and I was left to figure out a lot on my own.
q3: I didn't feel well-informed at all. The communication around this change was sparse and last-minute. I'm used to more proactive communication from administration about major changes like this. A simple project timeline would have gone a long way.
q4: The system works, I'll give it that. It hasn't had any major outages that I've noticed.
q5: Coming from a district where this kind of system was handled much more smoothly, I think there's significant room for improvement in both the technology and the process. The interface feels dated compared to what I've used before, and the rollout communication was inadequate.

name: Mateo Okafor
q1: The workflow impact has actually been positive, which surprised me because I was skeptical going in. My daily routine hasn't been disrupted and the new features are convenient. But the system itself has been glitchy — random drops, occasional static. That's concerning.
q2: Training was adequate. Not outstanding, not terrible. They covered what we needed to know to get started.
q3: I was kept reasonably in the loop. The emails and staff meeting updates gave me enough to know what was coming and when. No major complaints about the communication side of things.
q4: The workflow improvements are real. Being able to do targeted announcements and the improved interface have made things more efficient.
q5: Fix the reliability issues. I shouldn't be getting static and dropped connections on a brand-new system. That's not acceptable.

name: Angela Porter
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. I've had to adjust how I manage certain parts of my day because the system doesn't always behave predictably. There have been times when announcements came through garbled or at unexpected times, which interrupted lessons.
q2: The training was helpful. I felt like the instructor knew the system well and gave us practical tips. I'd rate it positively overall.
q3: This is where I have the biggest concern. I felt like we were barely told anything before it happened. One day we had the old system, the next week crews were in the building. I would have really appreciated being brought into the conversation earlier — even just an explanation of why the change was necessary.
q4: The training support has been a bright spot. When I've had questions, the help resources have been available and responsive.
q5: Communication from administration about this project was extremely poor. Teachers shouldn't learn about major building changes through hallway conversations. Also, the audio quality has been inconsistent.

name: Allison Johnson
q1: It's honestly made things harder. The system isn't as reliable as it should be, and I've had several instances where I couldn't reach the office when I needed to. That's stressful, especially during situations where you need an immediate response. My daily workflow has taken a hit.
q2: The training was just okay. I walked away with a basic understanding, but there were gaps. When I run into issues now, I don't always know how to troubleshoot them, which suggests the training didn't go deep enough.
q3: Communication was fine, actually. I felt like I knew what was coming and the timeline was shared clearly. That part was handled well.
q4: No
q5: The reliability is the biggest problem. A communications system needs to work when you need it. Period. I've also noticed that the volume levels seem inconsistent — sometimes announcements are too quiet, other times they're blaring.

name: Timothy Campbell
q1: It's been a slight adjustment. I'm relatively new to this building so I don't have a strong attachment to the old system, but the new one has caused some minor disruptions. There are moments where things don't quite work right and I'm not sure if it's me or the system.
q2: The training was passable but could have been better. I've seen better technology onboarding at other places. It covered the basics but felt rushed toward the end when they got to the more nuanced features.
q3: I actually felt fairly well-informed. The principal did a good job sharing updates at staff meetings, and the email communications were clear. As someone newer to the building, I appreciated having that context.
q4: The communication around the project was well-handled, which made the transition less stressful than it could have been.
q5: The system needs to be more reliable. I've had enough issues in my short time here that it's made me a bit wary. Also, it would help to have a quick-reference card posted near the panel in each room.

name: Nicole Howard
q1: It's been a lateral move at best. Different system, same basic function. My daily work hasn't really improved because of it, though I'll admit it hasn't gotten worse either. I just don't understand why we needed to change.
q2: The training was decent. They covered what we needed and the hands-on portion was useful. I didn't have any trouble learning the new system.
q3: We were told about it, but the reasoning was never really explained. I'd like to know why we spent money on a new system when the old one was functioning. That context would have made the whole change feel more justified.
q4: The training was well-run and the system does work when you use it.
q5: I'm still not convinced we needed this change. The reliability hasn't been perfect, and when you're replacing something that worked with something that mostly works, it's hard to see the value. I'd also like to see more input from teachers before these kinds of decisions are made.

name: Sierra Santiago
q1: Honestly, not a huge change to my daily routine. There were some hiccups in the first couple of weeks where announcements were cutting into instructional time more than they used to, but it's mostly leveled out. I've been here long enough to remember when we switched from the old analog system back in the early 2000s, so this isn't my first rodeo.
q2: The training felt rushed. We had one session after school that tried to cover everything at once, and I left feeling like I only caught about half of it. I would have appreciated a follow-up session or at least a reference sheet we could keep by the phone unit in our rooms.
q3: I felt pretty blindsided by the whole thing. I heard about it through the rumor mill before there was any official communication, which is not ideal. A simple email at the start of the planning process would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality is clearer than the old system, I'll give it that.
q5: The rollout communication was really lacking. After 29 years here I'd expect to at least be looped in before things start getting installed in the hallways.

name: Diana Yang
q1: It's been disruptive, honestly. I'm used to a system that just worked — you pressed the button, it was intuitive, done. This one has added extra steps to things that used to be simple, and there have been reliability issues that have interrupted lessons multiple times. I want to be open to the change but it's hard when it's making my day harder.
q2: The training was okay but felt generic. It didn't really address the specific ways we'd be using the system day-to-day in our classrooms. I've had to figure out a lot on my own through trial and error.
