q3: Communication was minimal. I found out details piecemeal and never felt like I had a full picture of the timeline or what to expect. An FAQ document or a short video walkthrough before installation would have been helpful.
q4: The emergency alert feature seems more robust than what I've experienced before, which I appreciate from a safety standpoint.
q5: The interface feels like a step backward from what I had before. It's less intuitive and the response time when you try to use the intercom is noticeably slower. I'd also change the way the paging works — it shouldn't be this complicated to make a simple announcement.

name: Adriana Ruiz
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some things are smoother but the transition itself caused a lot of disruption to my teaching schedule. There were days when the system was being installed or tested and it was really distracting for my students.
q2: Training was adequate. I felt like I learned the basics but wouldn't say I feel confident with all the features. It was a reasonable effort.
q3: I thought the communication was fairly good actually. We got emails ahead of time and the principal mentioned it at staff meetings. I knew it was coming and roughly when.
q5: The transition period was rough. I wish they could have done more of the installation work outside of school hours. Having technicians in and out while I'm trying to teach was not great.

name: Cristina Sandoval
q1: The day-to-day impact has been a bit of a disruption, particularly during the first month. I'm generally open to new technology and I can see the potential, but the reliability hasn't been where it needs to be yet. Some mornings the system just doesn't respond right away, which throws off my start-of-day routine.
q2: The training was decent. I appreciated that they gave us hands-on time with the units. I felt reasonably prepared to use the basic functions, though I know there are features I haven't explored yet.
q3: Communication was fine — not amazing, but I wasn't caught off guard. I think a more detailed timeline shared earlier in the process would have helped people feel less anxious about the change.
q4: I like the new interface once you get used to it. The screen is easy to read and the menu layout makes sense. I think once the kinks are worked out it will be a solid upgrade.
q5: Reliability needs to improve. I've had the unit in my room freeze up twice during the morning announcements, and I know other teachers on my hall have had similar issues. That needs to be addressed.

name: Francisco Gonzalez
q1: It hasn't really changed much for me on a daily basis. I use the intercom occasionally and it works fine most of the time. The new bells sound a little different but the kids adjusted quickly.
q2: I felt like the training could have been better. It was one session and it went fast. I had to ask a colleague to walk me through the paging function a few days later because I didn't retain it from the training.
q3: We were informed reasonably well. I remember getting an email and it was discussed at a staff meeting. Nothing too detailed but enough to know what was happening.
q4: The overall sound quality is better than what we had. Announcements come through clearer, which is nice especially in the mornings when I'm trying to get kids settled.
q5: N/A

name: Julia Edwards
q1: Hasn't affected my work too much. The system works and I use it when I need to. I'm still fairly new here so I don't have much to compare it to at this site.
q2: The training was underwhelming. I've been teaching for ten years and have been through system changes before — this training felt like it was designed for people who'd never touched a phone before, but then skipped over the actually useful features. I left with more questions than answers.
q3: I was really poorly informed. I started here recently and nobody told me about the new system until it was already being installed. I had to piece together what was going on from conversations in the break room. For someone new to the building, that was pretty frustrating. Even a welcome packet mentioning it would have helped.
q4: I like that the system is modern and seems to have good features once you learn them. The emergency lockdown button is a nice addition.
q5: Better onboarding for new staff about these kinds of changes. I felt completely out of the loop.

name: Rosa Ramirez
q1: It's been okay. I'm generally excited about upgrades and new tech, so I was looking forward to this. In practice, it's been a little bumpy — some glitches here and there — but I think it'll get better as things stabilize. My workflow hasn't been majorly disrupted.
q2: Training was fine, nothing spectacular. I would have loved a quick-reference card or a short video I could rewatch. I'm the type who needs to practice things a few times before they stick.
q3: I felt somewhat informed. There were mentions at meetings but I don't recall getting a lot of detail about what specifically would change for us in the classroom. More concrete examples would have been helpful.
q4: The touch screen is a nice upgrade. I also like that you can adjust the volume from the classroom unit now.
q5: The system has been a bit glitchy — sometimes there's a delay when I try to call the office, and once it just didn't connect at all. I hope that gets ironed out soon.

name: Andrea Baker
q1: Day to day, things are running pretty smoothly. The transition was a little bumpy but nothing terrible. My lessons haven't been significantly impacted and the basic functions work well enough.
q2: The training was satisfactory. It covered what I needed to know to get through the day. I wouldn't call it thorough, but it was adequate for the basics.
q3: I felt well-informed throughout the process. Our admin did a good job sending updates and letting us know what to expect at each stage. I appreciated the transparency.
q4: Sound quality is noticeably improved. The old system always had this crackly quality and this one is much cleaner.
q5: There have been a few reliability hiccups — the system has gone down briefly a couple of times. Nothing catastrophic but a little concerning when you think about emergency situations.

name: Sam Tanaka
q1: I was really excited about getting an updated system and I still think it has great potential. That said, it hasn't been as smooth as I'd hoped. There are intermittent connectivity issues that have interrupted my class a handful of times. I remain optimistic that these will be resolved.
q2: The training was actually pretty good. It was well-organized, the trainer was knowledgeable, and I felt like I walked away understanding the system. I'd appreciate a refresher session in a few months once we've had time to use it and can ask more targeted questions.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew it was coming and had a general sense of timeline. I think they could have shared more about why this particular system was chosen and what advantages it would bring — that context helps with buy-in.
q4: The feature set is genuinely impressive. The ability to send targeted announcements to specific zones is something I've been wanting for years. The integration with the bell schedule is also well thought out.
q5: The reliability issues need to be addressed. I've experienced the system dropping connection at least three or four times in the past month. For a brand new system, that's not acceptable.

name: Robert Stewart
q1: Functionally, it's been fine. My day hasn't really changed much. The system does what the old one did. I'm not sure we needed a whole new system but it works.
q2: Training was okay. Got the basics down. Could have used more time but I figured out the rest on my own.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well-informed. I heard about it at a meeting but the details were vague. When is it happening, what exactly is changing, how long will installation take — those were all questions I had to chase down answers for.
q4: It works. The sound is clear and the new units look more professional than the old beige boxes we had.
q5: Nothing major. I just wish they'd communicated better about the timeline and what to expect.

name: Maya Santiago
q1: The system hasn't disrupted my workflow too much, which I appreciate. It does what it needs to do on a day-to-day basis. I will say that I'm used to systems that had a more polished feel from day one — this has felt a little rough around the edges, but it's functional.
q2: The training was not sufficient. I've been teaching for over twenty years and have gone through multiple system transitions. This was by far the least thorough training I've received. One session is simply not enough, especially for a system with this many features. At my previous district the vendor came back for a second round of training after staff had a chance to use the system for a few weeks, and that made a huge difference.
q3: Communication was acceptable. I got the basic information I needed, though as someone new to the district I had less context than everyone else about why this change was being made. A brief background document would have been helpful for those of us just arriving.
q4: The system itself is reliable, which matters most. I haven't had any major technical failures and the emergency features seem solid.
q5: More training, period. One session for a system this involved is inadequate. I know from experience that this can be done better.

name: Doug White
q1: It's been mostly positive. The new system works well enough and my daily routine hasn't been thrown off. I was a little skeptical at first but I've come around to it. There are occasional technical hiccups but nothing that's derailed a lesson.
q2: Training was so-so. It covered the essentials but I felt like we rushed through some of the more advanced features. A follow-up session would be welcome.
q3: The communication could have been better. I knew it was happening but the specifics were always a bit fuzzy — like exactly when my room would be affected and what I'd need to do differently during installation.
q4: The clarity of the audio is a real improvement. Morning announcements actually sound professional now. I also like the two-way communication feature — being able to talk to the office without picking up the phone is convenient.

name: Stephanie Taylor
q1: It hasn't really affected my work in a negative way. Things are running smoothly for the most part. The new system seems to do everything the old one did, maybe a little better in some areas.
q2: The training was fine. I felt prepared enough to use the system after the session. Not amazing but got the job done.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. There were communications from admin about the change and I had a general understanding of the timeline. Nothing caught me off guard.
q4: It's a cleaner, more modern setup. I appreciate that the units are smaller and don't take up as much wall space.
q5: Some reliability concerns — the system has cut out on me once or twice. Not a huge deal but something to keep an eye on.

name: Teresa Rivera
q1: I've been through a lot of changes in this district over the years, and this one was about average in terms of disruption. The first couple of weeks were rocky — the old system was pulled out before the new one was fully functional in some rooms, which caused confusion. But it settled down. My daily teaching hasn't been majorly impacted.
q2: The training left something to be desired. It felt like they were checking a box rather than genuinely making sure we knew how to use the system. I've been teaching since before most of this technology existed, and I need hands-on practice, not a PowerPoint presentation. A printed quick-start guide by the unit in my room would have helped immensely.
q3: I didn't feel very well-informed. I've been here 29 years and I'm used to hearing about things through official channels, but this one seemed to come together without much staff input. Even a brief survey asking what we'd want from a new system would have been appreciated.
q4: The sound quality is good. Better than the old system, which had gotten pretty scratchy over the years especially near the cafeteria end of the building.
q5: I would have liked more input from teachers before the system was selected. We're the ones using it every day.

name: David Baker
q1: At this point in my career, I've seen these systems come and go. This one is fine. It works, my students can hear the announcements, and the bells ring on time. I use it the same way I used the last one. Hasn't really changed my day much.
q2: The training was mediocre. I've sat through enough of these sessions to know when they're just going through the motions. I would have preferred a one-page cheat sheet and five minutes of hands-on time with the actual unit in my room over the hour-long presentation we got.
q3: Communication wasn't great. I found out from a colleague that the installation date had moved up before I ever got an official email about it. That kind of thing erodes trust. Even after 34 years, I don't think it's too much to ask to be kept in the loop.
q4: It's reliable. I haven't had any major issues with the hardware. The audio is clear and the system hasn't gone down on me.
q5: Just communicate better. That's my only real complaint. The system itself is fine.

name: Sofia Romero
q1: It hasn't changed my day-to-day work significantly. The system functions and I use it when I need to. I wasn't particularly eager for another change — we seem to get a new system every few years and it's exhausting — but this one at least seems to work.
q2: Training was adequate. I learned what I needed to know. Nothing more to say about it really.
q3: I was well-informed. Admin communicated the timeline and rationale clearly. I appreciated knowing what was coming and when.
q4: The audio quality is good and the system seems dependable.
q5: I just wish we'd stop changing things for the sake of changing them. The old system worked fine. But if we had to switch, this one is acceptable.

name: Grace Chen
q1: It's been a slight disruption to my workflow. I didn't feel the old system needed replacing, and learning a new interface takes time and energy I'd rather spend on my students. That said, the system itself seems solid once you figure it out.
q2: The training was passable. I got the basics but didn't feel like it was tailored to what we actually need to do with the system on a daily basis. More scenario-based training would have been useful.
q3: Communication was average. I knew about the change but didn't feel like I understood the reasoning behind it. Why this system? Why now? Those questions weren't really addressed.
q4: It's reliable, I'll give it that. I haven't experienced any outages or technical problems, and the audio clarity is good.
q5: I would have appreciated understanding the rationale for the switch. When you don't explain the 'why,' people fill in the blanks themselves, and that's usually not positive.

name: Karen Moore
q1: Since I'm new to teaching, I don't have a lot to compare it to, but the system seems to work well. It hasn't caused me any issues and I use it without much trouble.
q2: The training was okay. As a newer teacher I had a lot of other things to learn at the same time, so it was a bit overwhelming, but I managed. I think the system is pretty intuitive once you start using it.
q3: I felt pretty well-informed. The information was shared clearly and I knew what to expect. Being new, I appreciated that.
q4: Everything works well. The sound is clear and I like how easy it is to use the basic functions. The emergency features give me peace of mind as a newer teacher.
q5: No complaints from me.

name: Alma Torres
q1: It's been a neutral change overall. Some things are better, some are about the same. I haven't noticed a dramatic shift in my daily routine. The transition period was a bit disruptive — I remember one week in October where we had no working intercom for two days in my hallway — but since then it's been stable.
q2: Training was middle of the road. It covered the basics and I felt like I could get by, but I wouldn't say I feel proficient with all the features. A refresher would be nice now that we've had a few months to actually use it.
q3: Communication was good. I felt informed and the timeline was communicated well in advance. I appreciated the updates from admin along the way.
q4: The new bell tones are much less jarring than the old ones, which is a small thing but makes a real difference when you're mid-lesson. Audio quality on announcements is also better.
q5: The reliability could be better. Having the system go down completely for two days was concerning, especially from a safety perspective. I'd also like to see more training offered for the advanced features.

name: Danielle Stewart
q1: Coming in as a new staff member, the system was just part of the environment for me. It works well and I haven't had any real issues with it. It seems comparable to systems I've used elsewhere and my daily work hasn't been negatively affected.
q2: The training was good. It was well-paced and the trainer answered all of my questions. I felt confident using the system afterward, which is really all you can ask for.
q3: I was informed well. Even though I was new to the district, the onboarding materials and conversations with my team gave me a clear picture of what was happening with the system and why.
q4: The system is reliable and the audio quality is excellent. I also appreciate how clean the installation looks — the units are unobtrusive in the classroom. The zone-based announcement feature is really thoughtful.

name: Sierra St. Claire
q1: Honestly, it hasn't changed things dramatically. The system works fine most of the time, but it's one more thing I had to adjust to mid-year when I already had my routines established. I wouldn't say it's improved my workflow, but it hasn't been a disaster either.
q2: The training was okay. It covered the basics, though I would have liked a little more hands-on time with the actual panel in my room instead of watching someone demo it on a projector. I figured the rest out on my own.
q3: I felt pretty well informed, actually. The emails and the staff meeting presentation gave us a clear picture of the timeline. I appreciated knowing when the installation would be happening in my wing so I could plan around it.
q4: The sound quality is noticeably clearer than the old system. I can actually hear announcements without asking students to be quiet first.
q5: The transition period was a little bumpy. There were a couple days where I wasn't sure if the intercom was even working, and I had to send a student to the office to check on something that should have come through as an announcement.

name: Lisa Turner
q1: It's been a mixed bag. The system itself seems to work adequately, though I've noticed it glitches occasionally — there was a day in October where the bell schedule was off by about two minutes and it threw my whole afternoon block off. After 25 years here, I've been through three different systems, and this transition felt about the same as the others.
q2: The training felt rushed. We got one session after school and that was it. For those of us who've been here a while, we know the building and could figure it out, but I don't think one 45-minute session was sufficient for the features they were trying to show us. I would have appreciated a reference sheet.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well-informed. I heard about it at a staff meeting and then things just started happening. It would have been helpful to get information earlier in the process rather than what felt like an afterthought.
q5: The volume control in individual classrooms seems inconsistent. Some days it's loud, some days I can barely hear. I'd like more control over the speaker in my own room without having to put in a work order.

name: Sage Dubois
q1: As someone new to the district, I'm still getting used to everything, so it's hard to separate what's the new system versus what's just me learning a new building. That said, there have been some reliability hiccups that have disrupted lessons — announcements cutting out partway through, that kind of thing. I'm generally open to new technology, so I'm hopeful it improves.
q2: Training was decent. I picked it up pretty quickly. The person leading the session was knowledgeable and answered questions well. I think it prepared me for the day-to-day basics.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I got the emails and had a general sense of the timeline. It might have helped to have a quick FAQ or one-pager for new staff specifically since we didn't have the context of the old system to compare against.
q4: I like the digital display panel. It's a nice upgrade from what I've seen at other schools.
q5: The system has cut out on me a few times and I've missed announcements. It's not frequent but when it happens during something important it's frustrating.

name: Mark Ward
q1: Not a huge impact either way. The system works and does what it's supposed to do. Some minor disruptions during installation week but nothing that derailed things for long. It hasn't really changed how I go about my day.
q2: Training was fine. Adequate. I don't have a lot of complaints but I also wouldn't call it exceptional. It got the job done.
q3: I felt well-informed throughout. The administration did a good job communicating the rollout plan and keeping us updated when things shifted. No complaints there.
q4: It's reliable. I can count on announcements coming through clearly and on time, which is really all I need from it.
q5: N/A

name: Diana Ramirez
q1: It's actually been pretty smooth for me! The new system doesn't really get in the way of my teaching, and when I need to use the intercom features they work well. I'm still fairly new so I don't have a strong frame of reference for the old system.
q2: I could have used more training, honestly. The session we had was short and I left with a lot of questions. I ended up asking colleagues for help with things I should have learned in the training itself. A follow-up session would have been really helpful.
q3: I wish there had been more communication. I found out about some of the changes from other teachers rather than from official channels. For newer staff especially, more proactive updates would go a long way.
q4: I really like the emergency alert features. Knowing that the lockdown system is integrated and reliable gives me peace of mind, especially as a newer teacher still building my confidence with safety procedures.
q5: The training gap is my main concern. I feel like I'm still discovering features I didn't know existed. Some kind of ongoing resource or quick-reference guide would be great.

name: Craig Peterson
q1: It's been a slight adjustment. The new system interrupts my class a bit more than the old one seemed to — more announcements, more chimes. Workflow-wise, it's added some minor friction. I'm adapting but it's taken some getting used to.
q2: The training was passable. Not great, not terrible. I've used similar systems at other schools so the learning curve wasn't steep for me, but I could see how it might be insufficient for someone less tech-comfortable.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew what was coming and roughly when. No major surprises.
q4: The clarity of the audio is better than the old system. You can actually understand what's being said now.
q5: Too many intercom interruptions during instructional time. I'd like a way to set a do-not-disturb window for my classroom during key teaching blocks.

name: Arturo Alvarez
q1: I've been teaching sixteen years and I can tell you, every time they roll out a new system it disrupts things for a while. This has been no different. The workflow impact hasn't been catastrophic but it's been an annoyance. I had my routines dialed in and now I'm having to adjust to new sounds, new buttons, new everything.
q2: The training was basically nonexistent. I'm not exaggerating — we got a rushed walkthrough that didn't cover half the features. I still don't know how to adjust the volume in my own classroom without calling the office. After seven years at this school, I shouldn't need to call the office to turn down my own speaker. It was insulting, frankly.
q3: The communication about the change was adequate. I knew it was happening and when. My issue isn't with the communication, it's with the execution — specifically the training, or lack thereof.
q4: The system itself seems solid hardware-wise. I'll give it that.
q5: Give us real training. Not a 30-minute overview at the end of a staff meeting. Actual hands-on training in our own classrooms with someone who can answer questions. That's what I'd change.

name: Steven Taylor
q1: This is probably the smoothest technology transition I've experienced in my 27 years here. The new system has actually made my day a little easier — the bell schedules are automated correctly, announcements are clear, and I haven't had to fiddle with it much. I remember when we switched from the original PA system back in the early 2000s and that was a nightmare by comparison.
q2: The training was thorough and well-organized. I appreciated that they walked us through the classroom panel step by step. I felt prepared to use it on day one, which is not something I can always say about district technology rollouts.
q3: Communication could have been a little better. I heard most things through the grapevine before official announcements came out, which is never ideal. But the information we did receive was accurate and helpful.
q4: The reliability has been excellent. In all the months since installation, I think I've had maybe one minor issue. The automated bell schedule and the integration with the emergency system are both genuinely well done.
q5: Nothing major. If I'm being picky, it would be nice to have a brief printed reference card for the classroom panel rather than relying on memory.

name: Stephanie Rogers
q1: It's been okay overall, but the reliability issues have been frustrating. I've had the system cut out on me multiple times — sometimes mid-announcement, sometimes it just doesn't come through at all. I've missed at least two schedule changes because of it. The workflow itself hasn't changed much, but when I can't trust the system to deliver messages, I end up checking email and my phone constantly to make sure I'm not missing something.
q2: The training was actually really good. This is one area where I have no complaints. The trainer was clear, patient, and covered everything we needed. I felt confident using the system after the session. The problem isn't training — it's the hardware.
q3: Communication was so-so. I knew a new system was coming but the details were vague. I would have liked to know more about what to expect in terms of features and changes to our daily routine.
q4: When it works, the audio quality is nice. And the training team was genuinely excellent.
q5: Fix the reliability. I've had too many instances of static, dropped audio, or complete silence when there should be an announcement. It undermines the whole system when you can't count on it.

name: Andrea Taylor
q1: I'm new to the district so I can't compare to the old system, but the new one seems to work well enough. It hasn't dramatically changed my workflow. Things come through when they're supposed to, for the most part.
q2: The training was underwhelming. It was brief and didn't go into enough depth on the features that are actually relevant to classroom teachers. I've had to figure out a lot through trial and error.
q3: Honestly, I felt a little out of the loop. As a first-year teacher here, I wasn't sure if I was just missing communications that everyone else got or if the information really wasn't being shared broadly. Either way, I would have benefited from more direct communication about what was changing and why.
q4: The system is very reliable. It just works, which I appreciate. No glitches, no static, clear audio every time.
q5: Better onboarding communication for new staff. I don't think it's unreasonable to have a one-page summary of the system and what we need to know.

name: Karla Delgado
q1: It's been rough. I can barely hear the announcements in my part of the building — the sound is muffled and sometimes I miss them entirely. At my last district the system was crystal clear everywhere, so this feels like a real step backward. I've had to walk down the hall to ask someone what was just announced more times than I can count.
q2: Training was just okay. Quick and basic. I've seen better.
q3: Nobody really told me much about the system when I started here. I kind of had to figure things out on my own. A heads-up about known issues would've been nice.
q5: The speakers on this end of the building are terrible. Half the time it sounds like the person is talking underwater. I'm used to systems that actually work and this one doesn't even come close where I am.

name: Pedro Ruiz
q1: The announcements in our hallway are basically useless — you can't make out what anyone's saying. It's garbled and way too quiet. I end up asking the teacher what was said or just waiting to hear from someone else. It hasn't wrecked my whole day but it's annoying.
q2: What training? We got a quick show-and-tell and that was it. I still don't really understand how to use the call button in the room I work in. Nobody followed up.
q3: They told us it was happening, which I guess is something. Could've been more detail about what to expect.
q4: Nothing
q5: Fix the audio where I work. I shouldn't have to shout down the hall to find out what was just announced. Also actual training would help.

name: Alejandra Contreras
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The audio in the rooms I work in isn't great — sometimes it's hard to catch what's being said and I have to ask the teacher to repeat it. Other than that, my day-to-day hasn't changed too much.
q2: Training was alright. Pretty basic. I got the general idea but it wasn't anything special.
q3: I was kept in the loop for the most part. The emails were helpful and I knew what was coming ahead of time.
q4: I like that there's a button to call the office directly from the classroom. That's handy.
q5: The sound quality in my area could really use some work. It's fuzzy and I sometimes can't tell if an announcement even happened.

name: Wei Nakamura
q1: I've been here 18 years and this is the worst the intercom has ever sounded in my area. I'm in the classrooms at this end of the building and half the time there's just dead air or garbled noise when announcements come through. The old system wasn't perfect but at least I could hear it. My daily work hasn't totally changed but I'm constantly worried I'm missing something important.
q2: Barely any training. They showed us the basics but didn't really go over troubleshooting or what to do when it doesn't work. Given how many problems we're having, that would have been useful.
q3: Communication about the switch was fine. I knew when it was happening and what to expect. No issues there.
q4: No.
q5: The speakers in our part of the building need to be fixed or replaced. I've put in two work orders and nothing has changed. This is a safety issue — if there's an emergency and I can't hear the announcement, that's a serious problem.

name: Karen Nelson
q1: It's made things harder in my area, to be honest. The announcements are so quiet and unclear in the rooms I'm in that I've basically stopped relying on them. I check in with the office directly now, which is extra steps I didn't used to have to take. After 15 years here, I know when something isn't working right, and this isn't working right.
q2: The training was inadequate. Short, surface-level, and didn't address the real issues we're dealing with. I walked out feeling like they checked a box but didn't actually prepare us.
q3: I was pretty much blindsided. I didn't hear about the new system until the installers showed up. Maybe the information was sent out and I missed it, but I don't think so. I've talked to others who said the same thing.
q4: None
q5: Somebody needs to come look at the speakers on our end of the building. They don't work properly. I've been saying this for months and nothing has happened.

name: Andrea Bailey
q1: It works well enough. Some disruption during the switch but nothing terrible. The new system hasn't really changed what I do day to day but it does feel like a downgrade in terms of how smoothly things run.
q2: Could've been better. The training was pretty quick and didn't cover everything. I'm tech-savvy enough to figure things out but not everyone is.
q3: Communication wasn't great. I found out details from coworkers more than from admin. An email or two more would've helped.
q4: The system sounds good in most parts of the building. Clear and loud enough.
q5: More training, and maybe a refresher a month or two after installation once people have had time to actually use it and have real questions.

name: Kiara Kapoor
q1: It hasn't really affected me one way or the other. Everything still works, announcements come through, bells ring on time. Pretty neutral.
q2: Training was fine. Nothing amazing but I got what I needed from it.
q3: I heard about it ahead of time and knew what to expect. Communication was adequate.
q4: It's reliable and clear. Works well.

name: Patricia Bailey
q1: Not much has changed for me honestly. The system works, I hear announcements, life goes on. Didn't notice a huge difference from the old one.
q2: The training was short. I would've liked more time to practice with it. They kind of rushed through everything.
q3: I didn't feel well-informed at all. Found out about it late and didn't get details until things were already happening. That's been a pattern here and it's frustrating.
q4: It works. The sound is clear and it seems dependable.
q5: Communicate changes earlier and give us real details, not just 'a new system is coming.' We need to know what's actually changing and how it affects us.

name: Hector Martinez
q1: Coming from where I was before, this system is a huge improvement. My previous district had equipment that was falling apart — you could barely hear anything, and half the speakers didn't work at all. This system actually makes my day easier because I can rely on announcements and the bell schedule without second-guessing.
q2: Training was solid. Covered what I needed and the trainer was helpful. No complaints.
q3: I was well-informed. Everything was communicated clearly with good lead time. Honestly, I've seen much worse when it comes to rolling out new tech.
q4: Everything about it is better than what I'm used to. The audio is clear, the bells are consistent, and the emergency features are a big plus.
q5: N/A

name: Mark Baker
q1: It's been disruptive. I had my routines down and now there's a learning curve with the new system on top of everything else. The timing of the rollout wasn't great either — doing it in the middle of the year made things harder than they needed to be. After 20 years here I'm tired of these changes that don't seem to make anything better.
q2: Training was basic. It was okay for what it was but felt like it was designed for someone who'd never used a PA system before. Didn't really address the practical stuff.
q3: Communication was lacking. I feel like we were told it was happening but not really consulted or given meaningful information about why this system versus the one we had.
q4: I guess the sound quality is fine.
q5: I'd change the timing of these rollouts. Stop doing major system changes in the middle of the school year. Plan it for summer when we can actually adjust without it affecting kids.

name: Nancy Chung
q1: It hasn't really changed things that much for me honestly. The system works fine, I just go about my day the same as before.
q2: The training was not great. They showed us the basics in like 20 minutes and then expected us to figure it out. I still don't know half the features.
q3: I actually thought they did a decent job letting us know it was happening. Emails went out, there were flyers in the break room. I felt prepared for the switch.
q4: It's reliable, I'll give it that. Haven't had it go down on me yet.
q5: Better training would be nice. I feel like I'm only using a fraction of what it can do.

name: Alma Morales
q1: Day to day it's been okay I guess. Not a huge difference from what we had before. I've been here long enough to see several of these changes come and go.
q2: Terrible. They gave us one session during a staff meeting and that was it. I've been asking for a follow-up training for weeks and nothing. Some of us need more hands-on time with new technology.
q3: They let us know ahead of time which was good. I saw the timeline posted in the office. Would have been nice to hear more about WHY we were switching though.
q4: N/A
q5: The training situation needs to be fixed. You can't just hand people a new system and expect them to figure it out on their own, especially those of us who aren't super tech-savvy.

name: Susan Miller
q1: It's been a little bumpy. The system glitches sometimes and I'm never sure if it's actually working or not. But overall it's manageable.
q2: The training was good. They walked us through everything step by step and I felt like I could ask questions. That part I have no complaints about.
q3: Could have been better. I heard about the change kind of last minute and felt like I was scrambling a bit to understand what was happening.
q4: The training was well done.
q5: The reliability could be better. Sometimes it just doesn't seem to work right and I'm not sure who to tell about it.

name: David Stewart
q1: It's been disruptive. I've been in this district over thirty years and every time they swap out a system it throws off the routine. I had a workflow that worked and now I'm relearning things I shouldn't have to relearn. The old system in Room 14 worked perfectly fine.
q2: Not enough. One training session doesn't cut it when you're changing how people do their jobs. I needed more time with the equipment and nobody was available to help after the initial rollout.
q3: I didn't feel informed at all. Found out through the grapevine before anyone in admin bothered to tell us directly. A simple meeting beforehand would have gone a long way.
q4: The hardware itself seems solid enough when it works.
q5: Everything about the rollout. The communication was poor, the training was rushed, and nobody asked the people who actually use these systems every day what they needed.

name: Miguel Morales
q1: It's been fine for the most part. Does what it needs to do. I'm not a fan of change but the system itself works well enough.
q2: Basically nonexistent. They showed us a quick demo and sent us on our way. I had to figure out most of it by asking coworkers.
q3: Nobody really told us what was going on until it was already happening. I would have liked a heads up and maybe some input on the timeline.
q4: It works. Reliable so far.
q5: The whole training piece was a mess. And we should have been told earlier.

name: Linda Wang
q1: I'm new to this district so I don't really have a comparison. The system seems to work okay for what I need it to do.
q2: The training was alright but felt a bit rushed. I would have liked a reference guide or something to look back at afterward.
q3: I was kept in the loop. They sent out clear communications about when the change was coming and what to expect, which I appreciated as someone new here.
q4: Nothing specific comes to mind.
q5: It has some quirks. Sometimes it cuts out or acts a little glitchy. Not a huge deal but noticeable.

name: Antonio Rivera
q1: It's been alright. Not much different really, just a new interface to get used to.
q2: Training was decent. Covered what I needed to know.
q3: Felt like we were kind of left in the dark about this. I didn't know it was coming until pretty close to when it happened. Would've been nice to have more notice.
q5: Communication about the change itself. We should have been told sooner.

name: Malia Kealoha
q1: Honestly it's made things better. The new system is way more intuitive and I love that everything just works. My day runs smoother now because I'm not dealing with the old system's issues.
q2: Training was okay. Got the basics down but I feel like there's probably more to it that I haven't explored yet. A follow-up session would be cool.
q3: I felt pretty well informed. There was some communication ahead of time and the transition was handled reasonably well.
q4: Everything! It's reliable, it's easy to use, and it's honestly a big upgrade. I haven't had a single issue with it going down or malfunctioning.
q5: Maybe just a little more training depth. But that's minor.

name: Nora Delgado
q1: I'm all for new technology so I was excited about this. The day-to-day impact has been mostly positive. It works well enough for announcements and such, though there have been a few hiccups with the audio cutting in and out.
q2: This is where they dropped the ball. I've been here 27 years and this was one of the worst training rollouts I've seen. One short session and that was it. Some of the younger staff picked it up quick but a lot of us were left struggling.
q3: Communication was so-so. I heard some things through email but it felt like the details were vague. I would have wanted a clear FAQ or something posted in the workroom.
q4: I like the concept and I think once the kinks are worked out it'll be great. The potential is there.
q5: Training, training, training. You can't skimp on that.

name: Daniel Anderson
q1: It's been okay. System works when it works. I've adapted to it but that doesn't mean the transition was smooth.
q2: Training was underwhelming. I've seen better rollouts in my time here. They gave us the bare minimum and expected us to run with it.
q3: Honestly I felt blindsided. After 28 years at this district you'd think someone would have the courtesy to give us a proper heads up. I heard about it from another aide before I ever got an official email.
q4: The system itself is dependable. I'll say that much.
q5: The way they communicated this whole thing. Completely unacceptable for a change this big.

name: Holly Bailey
q1: I wasn't thrilled about another new system but it's been working out okay. It does what it's supposed to do, I just don't love having to learn new things when the old way was perfectly fine.
q2: It was adequate. Not amazing but I got the information I needed to use the system.
q3: Could have been better. I felt like I was hearing bits and pieces rather than getting the full picture up front.
q4: It's very reliable. No complaints there.

name: Casey White
q1: It's been an adjustment. At my previous district the system we had was a lot more seamless and intuitive. This one works but it feels like a step backward from what I was used to.
q2: The training was okay. Nothing special but they covered the basics. I had to figure some things out on my own since I'm still getting the lay of the land here.
q3: They communicated the change well enough. I got emails and heard about it at orientation. Being new I appreciated having some notice.
q4: It gets the job done. The communication about it was handled well at least.
q5: The system itself feels clunky compared to what I've used before. I'm used to something more polished and user-friendly. Would be nice if they looked into updating the interface.

name: Keith Turner
q1: The workflow side of things has actually been pretty smooth, which surprised me given the issues we've had. My main concern is the audio quality near my office area. Half the time I can barely make out what's being said over the intercom. I've had to walk down the hall to hear announcements clearly.
q2: Training was good. They were thorough and I felt prepared to use the system from an operational standpoint.
q3: This was handled very poorly in my opinion. I had almost no advance notice about the installation timeline and found out about key details after the fact. For something that affects our entire building's communication, there should have been a lot more transparency from the start.
q4: The workflow integration has been smooth and I appreciate the new features for managing communications from the front office.
q5: The sound quality in our area is a real problem. Announcements come through garbled or way too quiet and we've reported it multiple times. That needs to be fixed. And the communication leading up to this whole project was frankly unacceptable.

name: Roberto Gonzalez
q1: The workflow has actually improved in some ways — the new system streamlines a lot of what we do at the front desk. But the speaker issues near our end of the building have been a real headache. We can't hear pages clearly and have had staff come up to the office asking what was just announced because they couldn't make it out either.
q2: It was fine. Adequate. I've been through enough of these changes to figure things out.
q3: Not great. I've been here almost twenty years and I expected better communication on a project this size. We got vague timelines and not much detail until things were already in motion.
q4: The digital interface for the office is a genuine improvement over what we had.
q5: Fix the speakers. The audio on this side of the building is unacceptable. I've put in requests and nothing has changed. Also, more lead time on communication would have been appreciated.

name: Kristin Parker
q1: It's been a neutral change for me. Not better, not worse, just different. I've adjusted to it and it works fine.
q2: Training was solid. I felt like I had a good understanding of how to use it by the time we went live.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I knew it was coming and had a general sense of the timeline. A few more details about specific features ahead of time would have been helpful.
q4: It's dependable and the training was well organized.
q5: Nothing major. It's fine.

name: Grace Sharma
q1: It hasn't dramatically changed things. I'd say it's a slight improvement over the old system but nothing groundbreaking.
q2: Training was okay. Covered the essentials. I would have liked more time to practice with it before going live.
q3: I felt informed. The administration shared the plan with us and I had a reasonable understanding of what was happening and when.
q4: Reliability has been good. The system works consistently which is important for an office environment.
q5: I'm not a big fan of change for the sake of change. Would have liked to understand more clearly why the old system needed replacing.

name: Catherine Bailey
q1: Honestly it's been a disruption. I had a system that worked for me for years and now I'm relearning how to do basic tasks. The front office workflow has slowed down because of it. We used to have things running like clockwork and now there's a learning curve all over again.
q2: Training was below average. I've been through at least four system changes here and this was one of the least supported transitions. One session is not enough for office staff who use the system all day every day.
q3: We didn't get nearly enough information ahead of time. I heard rumors before I got any official communication. After 34 years here, that's frustrating.
q4: None
q5: The transition was too rushed. Office staff should have been consulted more since we're the heaviest users of the communication system. And the training needs to be ongoing, not a one-and-done situation.

name: Peter Singh
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some things are easier now but other things that used to be simple take more steps. Net impact has been slightly negative for my daily routine.
q2: Not sufficient. The training was too basic for what we need to do in the office. I've had to troubleshoot things on my own multiple times because the training didn't cover real-world scenarios.
q3: Communication was mediocre. We got some notice but not enough detail to actually prepare. It felt like information was being shared on a need-to-know basis.
q4: No.
q5: More comprehensive training and better upfront communication. Also the system itself seems to have some reliability issues that need to be looked at.

name: Janet Wilson
q1: It's been an adjustment for sure, but I have to say this system is so much better than what I had at my previous district. There we were working with equipment that barely functioned half the time. The workflow integration still needs some tweaking but the technology itself is a huge step up from what I'm used to.
q2: I thought the training was well done. Clear, organized, and they gave us enough time to practice. Coming from a district where we basically got no training on anything, I really appreciated the effort here.
q3: Communication was decent. I felt like I knew what was coming and roughly when. A bit more detail on the specific features would have been nice but overall no major complaints.
q4: The reliability is great and the system is leagues ahead of what I've experienced elsewhere. The training was also a strong point — they actually invested in making sure we knew how to use it.
q5: The workflow piece could use some refinement. Some of the office processes are a little clunky still but I'm confident they'll smooth out over time.

name: Carlos Ortiz
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. Some of the daily processes take longer now and I'm still getting used to the new interface. Not terrible but not seamless either.
q2: The training was excellent. Easily the best part of this whole rollout. Whoever put that together did a great job — it was thorough, hands-on, and they actually answered questions.
q3: Communication could have been a lot better. I've been here 18 years and I feel like we've had better communication about much smaller changes. The timeline and rationale were not clearly shared with staff.
q4: Training was top notch. And the system itself works well — it's reliable and the features are solid.
q5: Just the communication piece. We deserved to know more sooner. Everything else has been handled well.

name: Jasmine Robinson
q1: The system itself seems to work fine, but honestly it's created more work on the admin side. We're fielding questions from staff constantly, and the new interface for making announcements takes longer than the old one did. I'm still getting used to it.
q2: Training was okay. It covered the basics but I feel like there were features that got glossed over. I had to figure out a few things on my own just by clicking around.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. We got emails and there was a staff meeting about it beforehand. It would have been nice to have a quick-reference sheet at each phone station though.
q4: The call quality is noticeably better, and the directory lookup is faster than our old system.
q5: The announcement scheduling interface is not intuitive. It takes me three or four clicks to do what used to take one.

name: Patrick Davis
q1: It's been alright for the most part, but there's definitely spots in the building where you can barely hear the announcements. I've had teachers flag me about the speakers near the B wing hallway more than once.
q2: Training was just kind of a quick walkthrough. Enough to get by I guess.
q3: Felt like we were one of the last to hear about it. Would've been nice to have more of a heads up, especially since we're the ones getting called when something isn't working right.
q4: The paging from the front office comes through clear in most areas.
q5: The audio is really weak on one side of the building. Some of those speakers sound like they're underwater. I've put in a work order but haven't heard back.

name: Doug Parker
q1: I've been here thirty years and every time they put in new equipment it's the same story. Half the building works great, the other half has issues. I spend time going back and forth checking on speakers that aren't putting out any sound near the end of the hall by room 14.
q2: They showed us the panel and how to use the intercom. It was fine.
q3: Nobody asked us anything before they started ripping stuff out of the walls. I found out the timeline had changed when the installers showed up on a Monday morning. A little more notice would go a long way.
q5: Dead spots. There's classrooms where the speaker barely makes a sound, and then you walk twenty feet down and it's blasting. The old system wasn't perfect but at least it was consistent.

name: Jennifer Huang
q1: It hasn't changed my work too much. I still do my rounds the same way. The new panels on the wall are different but not hard to use.
q2: Could have been better. They went through it fast and I had questions after but wasn't sure who to ask.
q3: We got some information but it felt like it was more aimed at teachers and office staff. Something specific to facilities would have helped.
q4: It works. The sound quality is decent.
q5: The training could have been more hands-on for our department.

name: Kenneth Yang
q1: Honestly this system is way better than what I've dealt with before. Everything works, the sound is clear, and I don't have to jury-rig anything to get it to function. Makes my job easier.
q2: Training was pretty good. I picked it up quick. Wouldn't have minded a refresher a couple weeks in, but it wasn't bad.
q3: They kept us in the loop. Emails, meetings, the whole thing. I've been at places where you don't get any of that so I appreciated it.
q4: Reliability. This thing just works. I've seen systems where you're troubleshooting every other day and this isn't that.
q5: No real complaints.

name: Francisco Reyes
q1: It's been okay. Not a huge difference for me day to day. I notice the new speakers and the wiring is cleaner, which I appreciate from a maintenance standpoint.
q2: Training wasn't great. Felt rushed. I would've liked more time actually using the system instead of just watching someone click through screens.
q3: Communication was so-so. We knew it was coming but the specifics of the timeline were fuzzy. I had to ask my supervisor for details a couple of times.
q4: The wiring and installation look professional. Cleaner setup than the old one.
q5: More hands-on training for facilities crew specifically. We have different needs than the teachers.

name: Rebecca Thompson
q1: It hasn't disrupted my work too much, but I'm open to the upgrade. The old system was showing its age for sure. I just wish this one was more dependable — it's cut out on me a couple of times already.
q2: The training felt like it was geared toward somebody else. I sat through it and got the gist but they didn't really address what we'd need to know as facilities. Pretty bare bones.
q3: We weren't really in the loop until it was already happening. I would have liked to see a timeline posted somewhere or get updates along the way instead of just being told 'it's done.'
q4: I like that they're investing in the infrastructure. The old speakers were from the nineties.
q5: It's glitchy. Cuts in and out sometimes. And the training situation needs work.

name: Maria Garcia
q1: It's been fine. Works when I need it to. Hasn't really changed my routine much.
q2: It was adequate. Nothing fancy but I got what I needed from it.
q3: I heard about it but didn't get a ton of details. Could've used more specifics about what was actually changing and when.
q4: Reliable so far.

name: Sofia Ramirez
q1: I'm glad they upgraded, I really am, but it's messed with my workflow a bit. The zones are different now so when I get paged I'm not always sure which area they mean. It'll probably get better once I'm more used to it.
q2: The training was not enough. They showed us the basics in like fifteen minutes and sent us on our way. I'm still figuring stuff out weeks later. I would have liked a follow-up session.
q3: Communication about the change was good. I knew what was coming and roughly when. No complaints there.
q4: Sound quality is great. Crystal clear.
q5: The zone labeling is confusing and the training was way too short.

name: Mark Phillips
q1: Not much different honestly. System works, I do my job. It's fine.
q2: Pretty thin. I've been here long enough to figure things out on my own but new people would probably struggle with what they gave us.
q3: We got some notice. Could've been more detailed.
q4: It works.
q5: Training. That's what I'd change.

name: Heather Thompson
q1: During the installation our lunch periods got shifted around with almost no warning. That was a mess. The system itself is better than what I had at my old district, I'll give it that, but the rollout was rough on our end with all the schedule changes.
q2: It was basic. Showed us the intercom button and that was about it.
q3: I had no idea things were changing until they changed. Nobody told the kitchen staff anything. At my last district communication wasn't great either but this was worse. Would have helped to get a heads up even just a week out.
q4: The sound is better than what I'm used to, at least in the cafeteria area. I've worked with much worse.
q5: I can barely hear announcements from where I work near the back hallway. And the schedule disruptions during install were really hard on us.

name: Pedro Garcia
q1: The bell schedule kept changing during the install and that threw off our whole prep and serving timeline. Other than that, the system is what it is. I can hear it fine in the cafeteria but back in the kitchen it's pretty muffled.
q2: Barely any training for us. Someone showed me one time and that was it.
q3: Actually I felt like I knew it was coming. They communicated it well enough. The timeline was clear.
q4: N/A
q5: The speakers near the kitchen are really hard to hear. Announcements come through but it's like listening through a wall. And the schedule changes during installation were a headache.

name: Diane Clark
q1: It's been terrible for our scheduling. They moved lunch periods around three different times during the install and we're still not fully back to normal. I've been here twenty-five years and I have never seen this much disruption to the lunch schedule. We had trays prepped for second lunch and then got told first lunch was starting early. Just chaos.
q2: Training was fine for what we needed. They showed us the call button. Not much else to know from our end.
q3: Nobody told us anything until the day of. I heard about the timeline changes from a teacher, not from admin. A simple email to kitchen staff would have gone a long way.
q4: The system itself seems to work okay. Sound is clear.
q5: The entire installation process and how it affected our lunch scheduling. We were an afterthought.

name: Jamal Dixon
q1: The new system messed up our lunch schedule for weeks. Bells were going off at wrong times, lunch periods got shifted without us being told. I've been doing this a long time and you can't just move lunch around on food service and expect it to go smooth.
q2: It was alright. Basic.
q3: They let us know something was happening but didn't give us the details that mattered to us, like how it would affect the bell schedule. That information would have helped a lot.
q4: Nothing
q5: The schedule disruptions. Lunch times should be the last thing you mess with and they were the first thing that got thrown off.

name: Tammy Stewart
q1: The bell schedule changes during installation really threw us off. We had kids showing up early, late, you name it. The system seems to work fine now but those first few weeks were rough.
q2: Training was good actually. Quick and to the point. I felt like I knew what I needed to know.
q3: We got some communication but it was pretty general. More detail about how the schedule would be impacted during installation would have been helpful.
q4: It's reliable. No complaints about the actual system.
q5: Better planning around how installations affect lunch service. We were scrambling.

name: Erin Parker
q1: Another change nobody asked us about. The bell schedule got moved around and we had to just deal with it. Kids coming in at different times, having to adjust portions and timing on the fly. After twenty years here you'd think they'd check with the kitchen before changing when lunch happens.
q2: Barely trained us. I asked a question and the guy didn't even know the answer. I figured it out myself.
q3: We were completely left out of the loop. Found out the schedule was changing when a teacher mentioned it in passing. That's not communication, that's gossip. An email, a meeting, literally anything directed at our department would have been appreciated.
q4: The system works I suppose. It does what it's supposed to do.
q5: Communication with food service. We're always the last to know and the first ones affected when schedules change.

name: Sharon Nakamura
q1: The system itself is fine but the lunch period changes during the switchover really disrupted our work. We had to redo our prep schedule multiple times. It's settling down now but those weeks were stressful.
q2: Not much training for kitchen staff. I would've liked more than what we got. Even just a handout.
q3: I felt informed about the change itself. The emails were clear. That part was good.
q4: It's dependable. I hear announcements clearly and it hasn't gone down once that I've noticed.
q5: Wish they'd thought more about how the installation schedule would impact food service timing. We do better when we can plan ahead.

name: Nora Salazar
q1: The lunch schedule kept getting moved during installation. We're used to a set routine and when the bells change, everything changes for us. It's been disruptive.
q2: Training was okay. Not great, not terrible.
q3: We knew about the new system ahead of time but the specifics about timing shifts during install were not shared with us. That's the part that mattered most to our work.
q4: Sound is clear. System seems solid.
q5: Include food service staff in the planning conversations when changes affect the bell schedule. We're always playing catch-up.

name: Patricia Morales
q1: It hasn't been great. Bell schedule changes during the install messed with our lunch periods. We're getting back to normal but it was a rough stretch.
q2: They gave us a quick overview. I would've liked something more but honestly we don't use many features so it was probably enough.
q3: I knew the system was coming. The timing details were less clear.
q4: Works fine from what I can tell.

name: Kristin Clark
q1: I've been here over thirty years and I've been through a lot of changes. This one affected our lunch periods more than most. The bells were unreliable for a while and we had kids and teachers confused about when lunch was. It's better now but it shouldn't have been that disorganized.
q2: The training was nothing special. Somebody came in and showed us the basics. For what I need it was fine I guess.
q3: I was blindsided. Nobody from admin came to talk to us beforehand. I heard about it secondhand. After all my years here it would be nice to be included when big changes are happening. Even just a courtesy email.
q4: No.
q5: The whole rollout was poorly planned from the kitchen's perspective. Talk to food service before you change the bell schedule. We are directly impacted every single time.

name: Patricia Ruiz
q1: Honestly the system itself is fine, it works good. The biggest issue was during the install when they kept changing up the bell schedule and lunch periods got moved around with no warning. That messed us up for a couple weeks.
q2: They showed us a few things but it was quick. I figured it out on my own mostly.
q3: Could have been better. We found out about schedule changes day-of sometimes. Compared to where I worked before though, at least here they tried to tell us something. I've been places where nobody tells you anything at all.
q4: The sound is clear and it works. Way better than what I've dealt with at other places.
q5: The timing of the install was rough. Lunch schedules were all over the place and we had to scramble to keep up.

name: Emily Kim
q1: The new system has been disruptive for me. The intercom interruptions during student sessions are really problematic — I work with students on sensitive matters and sudden announcements break trust in those moments. On top of that, the audio in my office area is genuinely hard to hear half the time, so I'm never sure if I missed something important or if it just didn't come through clearly.
q2: Training was adequate. I felt like it covered the basics well enough and I was able to follow along without too much difficulty.
q3: I was pretty blindsided by this whole transition. I don't recall being consulted or even given much advance notice. It would have been helpful to have some kind of timeline shared with staff well before things started changing, especially for those of us whose work requires a controlled, predictable environment.
q4: I do appreciate the emergency alert capabilities. Knowing that we have a reliable way to communicate during a lockdown or crisis is genuinely valuable in my line of work.
q5: The audio quality on my end of the building is really inconsistent — sometimes announcements are muffled or I can barely make them out. And I cannot stress enough how disruptive unscheduled intercom messages are when I'm in a session with a student. There needs to be a way to control when the intercom activates in counseling spaces.

name: Amy Martin
q1: I've been here long enough to have gone through several system changes, and this one has added more disruption than I expected. The intercom goes off at unpredictable times during sessions, and in my part of the building the speaker quality is poor — I often can't make out what's being said. It's created an additional layer of stress in my day that I didn't need.
q2: The training was fine. Not the best I've had over the years, but it got the job done. I appreciated having a hands-on component.
q3: Communication about this change was lacking. I've been through the portable classrooms era, the building C renovation, all of it — and usually we got more heads up than we did for this. An email a few weeks before doesn't cut it when it's going to affect how we do our jobs daily. I would have liked a staff meeting early in the process.
q4: The safety features are a real improvement. The emergency notification system is more robust than what we had before, and in my role I can see how that matters.
q5: The speakers in my area are subpar — garbled audio, dead spots in the hallway right outside my office. And the lack of any way to mute or delay the intercom during confidential student meetings is a significant oversight.

name: Daniel Carter
q1: Day to day it hasn't been too bad. The workflow is manageable. My main gripe is the reliability — the system cuts out or has static in my area pretty regularly. There have been a few times where I couldn't tell if an announcement was a drill or an actual alert because the audio was so garbled in my office.
q2: It was okay. Average. I felt like I knew enough to use the basic functions but not much beyond that.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I knew it was coming, roughly when. Nothing to complain about there.
q5: The speakers in my part of the building are not working well. Sound quality is poor and I've reported it but nothing's changed yet. For a counselor's office, it would also be great to have a do-not-disturb option for the intercom.

name: Isaiah Kealoha
q1: It's been a mixed bag. I value having a modern system for safety purposes, but the intercom interruptions during sensitive conversations with students are a real problem. It's hard to build rapport when a loudspeaker blares in the middle of a session.
q2: The training felt rushed and incomplete. I walked away without a clear understanding of the more advanced features, and there was no follow-up session offered. I would have appreciated a refresher or at least some written reference materials.
q3: I felt out of the loop. We weren't given much information about the timeline or what to expect during installation. Even a simple FAQ document would have gone a long way.
q4: The emergency alert functionality is solid and I feel more confident about crisis communication than I did before.
q5: Intercom interruptions during student sessions. This is the single biggest issue for me. There should be a way to suppress non-emergency announcements in counseling offices.

name: Karen Gupta
q1: Overall the system works well day to day. I've been here a long time and this is a genuine upgrade in terms of functionality. The intercom disruptions during sessions bother me, but I've adapted by scheduling around likely announcement times when I can.
q2: Training was decent. I've seen worse over the years. It covered the essentials, though I had to figure out a few things on my own afterward.
q3: This is where I have a real issue. I have been at this district for nearly three decades and I found out about this change practically at the last minute. That is unacceptable. Staff who have invested this much time deserve to be part of the conversation, not informed after decisions are made. A simple advisory committee including veteran staff would have made a world of difference.
q4: The system is reliable and the emergency features give me confidence. The audio clarity is good in most areas I work in.
q5: The communication around this rollout was the worst part. I also think counseling and specialist offices need the ability to silence non-emergency announcements during appointments.

name: Patrick Nelson
q1: It's been disruptive. The intercom cuts into sessions without warning, and I feel like my ability to do my job effectively has taken a hit. Students get startled, the moment is lost, and we have to start over.
q2: Training was pretty weak. It felt like a checkbox exercise rather than an actual effort to get us comfortable with the system. I still don't know how to use several features.
q3: I knew it was happening, timeline was communicated. That part was fine.
q4: N/A
q5: The training needs to be redone for anyone who wants it. And honestly, the biggest fix would be giving counselors control over the intercom volume or a mute option during sessions. That would solve most of my frustrations.

name: Lucia Castillo
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The system itself is extremely reliable, which I appreciate greatly — I've never once questioned whether an alert actually went through. But the intercom interruptions during my sessions with students are a constant frustration. It undercuts the confidential, safe environment I try to create.
q2: The training was mediocre. I felt like I needed more time and more specifics, especially around the features most relevant to my role. It was geared toward classroom teachers.
q3: Communication was good. I was informed well in advance and felt like the timeline was clear. I appreciated the emails that went out.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. I have never experienced a missed alert or a system failure, and that matters enormously for student safety.
q5: Non-emergency intercom announcements should not broadcast into counseling offices by default. That's the change I want.

name: Alejandra Castillo
q1: I'm still relatively new to this site, so I don't have a strong comparison to the old system. What I can say is that the intercom can be jarring during one-on-one work with students. I've had kids shut down completely after a sudden announcement mid-session.
q2: Training was okay. I've been doing this work a long time elsewhere, so I'm used to picking things up quickly, but I would have liked more depth.
q3: I felt somewhat in the dark. Being newer here, I wasn't sure if information was shared before I arrived or if it just wasn't communicated well. Either way, I would have liked a clearer picture of what was changing and when.
q4: It seems to work consistently. I trust that when an emergency message goes out, it will reach everyone.
q5: The intercom interruptions during confidential sessions. That's my primary concern.

name: Sage Santiago
q1: Compared to what I had at my previous district, this system is a massive improvement. It's reliable, the sound quality is clear, and it actually works when you need it. The only issue specific to my role is the intercom breaking into counseling sessions, but on the whole it's been positive.
q2: The training was not great. I'm someone who embraces new technology and I still found it insufficient. It was too short and didn't cover role-specific use cases. I ended up just experimenting on my own.
q3: I was informed about the change and the communication was reasonable. I've been through transitions where nobody said a word, so I appreciated that there was at least a plan communicated.
q4: The reliability is excellent. Coming from where I came from, having a system that just works consistently is something I don't take for granted. The emergency features are top-notch.
q5: Training needs improvement. And counselors need an option to suppress routine announcements during sessions.

name: Enrique Aguilar
q1: It's been a step backward for my daily workflow. I've been doing this work for a long time and the old system, while imperfect, didn't intrude on my sessions the way this one does. The intercom announcements are louder and more frequent, and I find myself losing productive time with students because of it.
q2: The training was inadequate. One session does not prepare someone to use a system they'll rely on daily. I've been here nearly twenty years and I've seen better rollouts for far smaller changes.
q3: Communication was average. I heard about it but I wouldn't say I felt informed. There's a difference between being told something is happening and actually being brought into the process.
q4: Nothing
q5: The training, the intercom interruptions during confidential work, and the general sense that staff input wasn't meaningfully sought. All of these could have been handled better.

name: Brenda Clark
q1: It has made my work harder. The constant intercom interruptions are a real problem when you're sitting with a student who's in crisis or sharing something deeply personal. I've had to rearrange my schedule to try to avoid peak announcement times, which is not an acceptable workaround. I remember when the old PA system was installed in Building B back in the early 2000s — even that transition was smoother.
q2: Training was passable but not thorough. I've been through enough of these transitions to know what good training looks like, and this wasn't it. One session isn't enough for a system this complex.
q3: I did not feel well-informed. After thirty-plus years in this district, I would have expected to be consulted or at minimum given substantial advance notice. What would have helped is a town hall or open forum before the decision was finalized.
q4: The system does seem to function reliably. Emergency notifications are clearer than the old system.
q5: The lack of counselor-specific considerations is the biggest miss. We need the ability to control the intercom in our spaces. Also, the communication leading up to this rollout was poor for a change of this magnitude.

name: Keith Williams
q1: For the most part the workflow impact has been manageable. I've been here over twenty years so I've adapted to plenty of changes. The intercom can be disruptive during sessions but I've worked around it.
q2: Training was lacking. I expected more, especially given how much this system can do. We got a surface-level overview and were expected to figure out the rest. I know for a fact the media center training back in 2015 was more thorough than this.
q3: Communication could have been much better. I found out about specifics through the grapevine more than through any official channel. That's not how you roll out a building-wide change.
q5: Better training, better communication, and some kind of mute or quiet mode for counseling offices during appointments.

name: Destiny St. Claire
q1: It's been a positive change overall. The system works well and I feel like day-to-day operations have improved. As a newer counselor, the emergency features give me a lot of peace of mind. The only downside is the intercom going off during student meetings, but it's manageable.
q2: Training was decent but could have gone deeper. I'm still early in my career so I had a lot of questions, and the session felt a bit too fast-paced for someone still learning the ropes.
q3: I felt well-informed. The communication was clear and I knew what to expect and when. That was helpful.
q4: The emergency alert system is great. Clear audio, reliable, and I trust it. The overall system quality feels modern and capable.
q5: Just the intercom during sessions — if there were a way to delay non-emergency announcements to counseling offices, that would be ideal.

name: Janet Stewart
q1: From an administrative standpoint, the new system has been a net positive for daily operations. The workflow improvements are noticeable, particularly around scheduling and coordination. However, I do have concerns about reliability — we've experienced intermittent issues that, while not catastrophic, do undermine staff confidence in the system.
q2: The training provided was solid. Our staff received adequate preparation, and the vendor was responsive to questions. I felt it was well-organized.
q3: Communication about this transition was handled well at both the district and site level. Staff were informed through multiple channels and given a reasonable timeline. I'm satisfied with how that aspect was managed.
q4: The integration with our existing scheduling tools has been seamless, and the overall user interface is intuitive. Staff have generally adapted well.
q5: The reliability issues need to be addressed. We've had instances where the system has not performed as expected, and that erodes trust. I would recommend the vendor conduct a thorough review of the hardware in our building.

name: Gary Carter
q1: The transition has gone reasonably well from an operational perspective. Daily workflows have improved in several areas. I do have some lingering concerns about the system's reliability, as we've seen a few hiccups that gave me pause, but nothing that has caused a major disruption.
q2: I want to commend the training team. The sessions were well-structured, informative, and accessible to staff at varying comfort levels with technology. This was one of the stronger training rollouts I've been part of in my time here.
q3: Communication was thorough. The district office kept us informed at every stage, and I was able to relay that information to my staff with confidence. I felt like we had the information we needed when we needed it.
q4: The training was excellent. The overall system, when it's working properly, is a clear upgrade from what we had.
q5: Reliability. That's the area that needs the most attention. The system has potential, but until the intermittent issues are fully resolved, there will be a trust gap among staff.

name: Silvia Torres
q1: The new system has been a welcome addition to our building. Day-to-day operations feel more streamlined, and the communication capabilities are a noticeable improvement. I've been pleased with how the transition has gone.
q2: Training was adequate. I think most staff felt prepared, though there were a few who could have used additional time. A follow-up session might be worth considering.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. There were a few moments where I wished I had more detail about specific timelines, but overall the communication was sufficient.
