q4: The system's reliability has been good, and the integration with our daily schedule has worked well. Staff have been able to adapt without major issues.
q5: A follow-up training session would be beneficial. Also, I think gathering staff feedback earlier in the process could have smoothed out some of the concerns that came up.

name: Molly Peterson
q1: This has been a very positive change. Coming from my previous district, I can say without hesitation that this system is significantly better than what I was working with before. The workflow improvements have been meaningful and the staff have responded well.
q2: The training was well done. It was thorough, well-paced, and the materials were useful. I felt confident using the system after the training, which is not always the case with these kinds of rollouts.
q3: Communication was excellent. I was kept in the loop throughout the process and felt like my input was valued. This is how change management should be done.
q4: Nearly everything. The system is more capable, more reliable, and better supported than what I've experienced elsewhere. The communication and rollout process were also handled professionally.

name: Raymond Yang
q1: The system itself is a clear improvement over what I worked with at my previous district. That said, the transition has created some workflow disruptions that I think are taking longer to resolve than anticipated. Overall it's positive, but there's still work to do.
q2: Training was decent. It covered the fundamentals, but I think some staff could have benefited from more advanced or role-specific sessions. The format was fine.
q3: Communication was acceptable. I knew what was coming and when, which I appreciate. At my last district, transitions like this happened with almost no warning, so by comparison this was well-managed.
q4: The system quality is noticeably better than what I've used before. The audio is clear, the interface is modern, and the emergency capabilities are strong.
q5: The workflow disruptions during the transition period could have been minimized with better phased implementation. I'd also advocate for ongoing training opportunities rather than a one-time session.

name: Camille Okamoto
q1: The new system has been a positive development for our building overall. Daily operations have improved, and I've heard generally favorable feedback from staff. It's been a smoother transition than I anticipated.
q2: Training was satisfactory but not exceptional. I believe some classified staff in particular could have used more support. The sessions were a good start, but ongoing support will be important.
q3: I would have liked more communication earlier in the process. By the time information reached site-level administrators, some decisions had already been made. Involving us earlier would have been beneficial, especially since we're the ones managing the change at the building level.
q4: The overall functionality and the improvement in daily communication capabilities. The system works well for what we need it to do.
q5: Earlier involvement of site administrators in the planning process, and more robust training support for non-teaching staff. Those are the two areas I'd focus on improving for future rollouts.

name: Shannon Campbell
q1: In my space it's actually been pretty helpful. The coverage in the theater is noticeably better than the old system, and I can hear announcements clearly even from the back of the house. That said, there have been a few glitchy moments where the audio cuts out mid-announcement, which is annoying when you're trying to get 40 kids to pay attention.
q2: The training was adequate but not great. I felt like it covered the basics but didn't really address the specific needs of someone running a large performance space. I had to figure out some of the zone controls on my own.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well-informed about the rollout. I heard about it mostly through hallway conversations before anything official came my way. An email timeline or even a brief FAQ would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality in my room is genuinely impressive. It fills the whole space evenly, which is something I've struggled with for years with the old setup.
q5: The system has had a few reliability hiccups — random drops in audio mid-day. Also, the training didn't really prepare me for the unique setup in my room, so I was left troubleshooting on my own for the first couple weeks.

name: Claudia Aguilar
q1: It's been a smooth transition for me. The new system works well in my classroom, and I appreciate how clear the audio is even in a larger instructional space. It hasn't disrupted my routine much at all.
q2: Training was solid. I came away feeling confident about the day-to-day functions. The hands-on portion was the most useful part — I learn better by doing than by watching a slideshow.
q3: Communication was okay. I knew it was coming, but the specifics of the timeline were a little fuzzy. It would have helped to get a one-page summary of what was changing and when.
q4: The reliability has been excellent. I haven't had a single issue with the system going down or missing an announcement. The audio reaches every corner of my room, which is a big improvement.

name: Diana Morales
q1: Honestly this system is really good compared to what I've worked with before. At my previous district we barely had functioning intercoms, so having clear audio that actually reaches the back of my workshop is a huge step up. It's made my day-to-day easier.
q2: The training was great. They walked us through everything clearly and I felt comfortable using the system right away. Coming from a place where we basically got no training on anything tech-related, I was impressed.
q3: I wish they had communicated more about the timeline. I found out about some of the changes kind of last minute, but honestly I'm new enough here that I might have just missed the memo.
q4: Everything about the audio quality. I teach in a big space and this system covers it so well. Where I came from before, you couldn't hear announcements past the first few rows. This is miles ahead.
q5: Nothing major. Maybe just more heads-up about when things are going to change.

name: Graciela Martinez
q1: The new system has been a positive change for my work. The audio coverage in my space is excellent and I no longer have to repeat announcements to students who couldn't hear. It's streamlined how I start and end class periods.
q2: Training was fine but could have been more in-depth. I feel like we got the overview version and then were left to figure out the details. A follow-up session would be helpful.
q3: I heard about it but felt like the communication was somewhat surface-level. I would have appreciated more detail about what specifically was changing and how it would affect my classroom.
q4: The system is rock solid. I haven't experienced a single outage or malfunction. The audio quality in my large classroom is fantastic — clear and consistent no matter where you're standing.
q5: N/A

name: Jennifer Wilson
q1: It's been a net positive for sure. The sound carries well throughout my entire teaching space, which makes a real difference when you're working with a large group. I've noticed fewer disruptions because students can actually hear what's being announced without me having to stop everything and quiet them down.
q2: The training was pretty good. I appreciated that they gave us hands-on time with the equipment. My only critique is that it was a one-and-done session — I think a refresher a few weeks in would have been valuable once we'd had time to actually use the system and develop real questions.
q3: Communication could have been better. I got the general sense that a new system was coming, but the details about the actual rollout were vague. A clear timeline sent via email would have helped me plan around the transition.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. The audio quality in my space is crystal clear and consistent. I genuinely have no complaints about how the system performs.
q5: I think the communication around the change was the weakest link. The system itself is great.

name: Drew Davis
q1: Workflow-wise it's actually been a real improvement. Having consistent, clear announcements that reach every part of my space has eliminated the old problem of kids missing bells or not hearing lockdown drills. That part has been great. The system itself, though, has had some reliability issues — audio dropping out, the occasional weird feedback loop.
q2: The training was pretty underwhelming. It felt rushed and didn't go deep enough into the features. I left with more questions than answers, and when I tried to follow up, I wasn't sure who to even ask. There should have been a reference guide or at least a FAQ we could consult afterward.
q3: They communicated that it was happening, which I appreciated. The timing and scope of the change were reasonably clear. I just wish there had been more opportunity for input from teachers before decisions were finalized.
q4: The impact on my daily workflow has been genuinely positive. Announcements reach my entire room, which is a big deal in the space I teach in. That alone makes it worth the switch.
q5: The reliability needs work. I've had multiple instances of the audio cutting out or producing feedback. And the training was insufficient — I still don't feel fully comfortable with all the system's capabilities.

name: Stephanie Edwards
q1: It's been a mixed bag. The system itself is very reliable, which I appreciate. But the transition disrupted some of my routines, and I'm still adjusting to the new interface for things like the paging features. In my space the audio coverage is excellent, though, so at least that part works well.
q2: I was disappointed with the training. It was too short and too general. We spent a lot of time on things I could have figured out myself and not enough on the more advanced features that I actually need help with. I would have preferred a hands-on workshop tailored to our specific room setups.
q3: I felt like I was somewhat in the dark about the specifics. I knew a change was coming, but the details about timing and what would be different were communicated late. I found out about some changes from colleagues rather than from admin.
q4: The system hasn't gone down once since it was installed. That kind of reliability is exactly what we need, especially in a space like mine where I can't just run to the office if something goes wrong during a class.
q5: The training was a letdown and the communication leading up to the switch could have been much better. I feel like I'm still figuring things out that I should have been taught from the start.

name: Javier Chavez
q1: I've been through enough of these system changes over the years to know that the transition is always the hardest part. This one has been fine — the audio in my shop area is better than anything we've had before, and I can actually hear announcements over the equipment noise now. The first couple weeks were bumpy but things have settled in.
q2: Training was okay. I've sat through a lot of these sessions over three decades and this one was about average. Got the basics, moved on. I figured out the rest by pressing buttons, same as always.
q3: Communication was lacking. I've been here long enough to know when something's being rolled out without enough teacher input. We got told it was happening, but I don't recall being asked what we needed or what the old system's problems were from our perspective. Would have been nice to feel like our experience counted for something.
q4: The coverage in my workshop is significantly better. That's the single biggest improvement — I used to miss half the announcements because the old speakers couldn't compete with the saws and ventilation. Now I can hear clearly from anywhere in the room.
q5: The system has some intermittent quirks — occasionally the audio quality dips for no apparent reason. And I'll say again, 34 years in this building and nobody thought to ask what the people actually using these classrooms wanted before picking a new system.

name: Jade Okafor
q1: This system has genuinely improved my day-to-day. I've worked in districts where the intercom barely functioned and you just learned to live without it. Here, the audio fills my entire space clearly and I actually rely on the announcements now. It's made transitions between periods smoother and emergency communication much more reliable.
q2: The training was good. It was well-organized and the trainers knew their stuff. I've been through much worse onboarding for tech systems at other places, so I was pleasantly surprised by how prepared I felt afterward.
q3: The communication about the change could have been more thorough. I didn't get a lot of advance detail, but honestly, coming from where I came from, even getting any notice at all felt like a step up. A detailed email or staff meeting overview would have been ideal.
q4: Everything. The reliability, the audio quality, the coverage in large spaces — it all works. I am genuinely impressed. This is leagues ahead of what I had before.
q5: No.

name: Crystal Jefferson
q1: It's been okay. The audio in my room is great — way better coverage than the old system, which matters a lot in a larger space. I'm not someone who loves change for the sake of change, but I can admit this is an improvement in terms of daily functionality.
q2: Training was decent. Not amazing, not terrible. I felt like I got enough to get started, but I still had to figure some things out through trial and error. A cheat sheet for common tasks would have been useful.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. The timeline could have been shared earlier, but overall I knew what was coming and roughly when. No major surprises.
q4: The sound quality in my space is noticeably better. Announcements are clear from every part of the room, and I've had very few technical issues.
q5: There have been a couple of times the system seemed to lag or the audio quality dropped briefly. Minor stuff, but worth noting. I also think the system was introduced without enough consideration for how different teachers use their spaces differently.

name: Alejandra Salazar
q1: This has been a really positive change. Coming from a district where the communication system was basically nonfunctional, I was thrilled to see how well this one works. The coverage in my room is excellent and announcements come through clearly every time. It's made my day noticeably smoother.
q2: Training was fine. I picked things up quickly because honestly the system is pretty intuitive. I've dealt with much worse interfaces, so this felt straightforward. I wouldn't have minded a more in-depth session, but what we got was sufficient.
q3: I felt a little out of the loop on the details. I knew the system was being replaced, but specifics about the timeline and what to expect were hard to come by. At my last district they didn't communicate anything at all, so at least here I had some notice, but I think there's room for improvement.
q4: The reliability is phenomenal. It just works, every single time. The audio quality in a big space like mine is outstanding. I've seen what bad systems look like and this is not that — not even close.

name: Jade Santiago
q1: After 33 years in this district, I've watched us go through three or four different systems. This one is by far the best in terms of actual audio performance. In my space, I can finally hear every announcement without having to stop what I'm doing and walk closer to the speaker. It hasn't disrupted my workflow — if anything, it's made things more efficient.
q2: The training was light. I would have preferred something more comprehensive, especially for someone like me who remembers how every previous system worked and wants to understand how this one is different, not just how to press the basic buttons. I had to seek out additional information on my own.
q3: I did not feel well-informed. For a change this significant, I expected more lead time and more detail. I've been here long enough to remember when we used to have proper staff input sessions before big rollouts. That didn't happen this time, and it's a missed opportunity.
q4: The audio quality and coverage are superb, especially in larger rooms like mine. The system has also been quite reliable — I can count on one hand the number of issues I've had since it went live.
q5: Communication and training both fell short. The system is good, but the rollout process didn't respect the experience and institutional knowledge that veteran staff bring to the table.

name: Helen Zhang
q1: It's been a significant improvement for my workflow. I teach in a large space and the old system simply could not reach every corner. Now I don't have to relay announcements myself or worry about students in the back missing critical information. I've been eager for an upgrade like this for years and it delivers.
q2: The training was passable. I got the essentials but felt like there was more to learn that we simply didn't have time for. After 32 years I've learned to just experiment until I figure things out, but not everyone works that way. A follow-up Q&A session would have been worthwhile.
q3: The communication was not great. I found out about key dates through the grapevine rather than through official channels. I remember when we installed the system back in room 14B years ago, we had a whole staff meeting about it. This time it felt like it was just sprung on us.
q4: The audio coverage is the standout feature. My entire space gets even, clear sound. I also appreciate how responsive the system is — no lag between when announcements start and when I hear them.
q5: The lack of communication beforehand was disappointing. I wanted to be excited about this and I am, but the process left a sour taste.

name: Megan Lewis
q1: It hasn't dramatically changed my day-to-day, which I suppose is a good thing. The audio works well in my space, and the transition was relatively painless. I've had a few minor hiccups with sound quality but nothing that's seriously disrupted instruction.
q2: Training was adequate. It covered what I needed to know without being overwhelming. I appreciated the practical demonstration, though I think a short reference document would have been a nice supplement for those moments when you can't quite remember which button does what.
q3: I felt reasonably informed. I knew it was coming and had a general sense of the timeline. More specificity about exactly when different features would go live would have been helpful, but overall the communication was acceptable.
q4: The coverage in my room is much better than before. Announcements reach every part of the space, which has been a long-standing issue with previous systems. That's a clear win.
q5: Some occasional audio inconsistency — sometimes crystal clear, sometimes a little muddy. It's not a major issue but it would be nice if it were more uniform. I'm also not a huge fan of change for change's sake, so I hope we stick with this one for a while.

name: Karla Sandoval
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. The audio quality in my space is good, and I can hear everything clearly, which is a step up. But the transition itself disrupted some of my routines, and I'm still getting used to a couple of the new features. After 16 years here I had the old system pretty dialed in, so there's a learning curve.
q2: Training was reasonable. Not the best I've attended but it gave me enough to function. I had to ask a colleague to show me a few things afterward, which suggests the training could have been more thorough on certain features.
q3: I knew the change was happening but didn't feel like I had a clear picture of the details until pretty late in the process. A more structured communication plan with dates and expectations would have reduced some of the anxiety on our end.
q4: The audio coverage is excellent in my large teaching space. That has been a consistent bright spot.
q5: The workflow disruption during the transition was frustrating. I would also say the communication about the change was a weak point — we should have been brought into the conversation earlier.

name: Diego Rivera
q1: The system works well in my space. The audio fills the entire room without any dead spots, which is exactly what I needed. Day-to-day it's been a positive change. I don't have to strain to hear or worry about students missing announcements anymore.
q2: Training was just okay. It covered the fundamentals but I left feeling like there were features I still didn't understand. I've been here 20 years and I like to know a system inside and out, so a surface-level walkthrough doesn't really cut it for me.
q3: This is where I have a real issue. I was essentially blindsided by the timeline. I heard rumblings that something was changing, but the actual rollout happened before I got any formal communication about what to expect. After two decades in this district, I think I've earned the courtesy of being informed in advance. That was badly handled.
q4: The system is extremely reliable and the audio quality in my room is outstanding. No complaints about the technology itself.
q5: The communication was terrible. Full stop. The system is good but the way it was rolled out showed a real lack of respect for the staff who have to use it every day.

name: Tiffany Price
q1: It's made my day-to-day noticeably better. The audio quality in my space is great — clear, consistent, and loud enough to reach every student without being overwhelming. I don't have to pause instruction to relay missed announcements anymore, which saves me time every single day.
q2: I thought the training was well done. The pace was good and the facilitator was knowledgeable. I felt ready to use the system by the time the session ended. My only suggestion would be offering an advanced session for people who want to explore the less obvious features.
q3: Communication was fine. I got enough information to feel prepared, and the transition matched what I was told to expect. Could there have been more detail? Sure, but I wasn't left confused or scrambling.
q4: The workflow improvement is the biggest thing. Everything just runs more smoothly now. The system is reliable and the sound in my large classroom is excellent.

name: Margaret Peterson
q1: Another new system. I've been through enough of these to know the drill. This one works fine — the audio in my space is better than the last one, I'll give it that. But I wasn't exactly clamoring for a change. The old system worked well enough, and the disruption of switching over was unnecessary in my opinion.
q2: Training was fine. At this point in my career I can figure most things out, so I didn't need a lot of hand-holding. It was a standard session, nothing special.
q3: I did not feel well-informed. I got the same vague email everyone else got, and then suddenly the installers were in my room. After 31 years you'd think they'd loop the veteran teachers in a little more directly. A heads-up about specific dates and disruptions to my space would have been appreciated.
q4: The sound quality in the room is improved. I teach in a big space and the new speakers do a better job of reaching all areas evenly.
q5: I didn't ask for this change and I don't think it was communicated well. The system itself is fine, but I question whether the disruption and expense were justified when what we had was working.

name: Destiny Dubois
q1: My workflow has improved quite a bit. The audio coverage in my room is really solid now, and I don't have the old problem of students in the far corners not hearing bells or announcements. Transitions between activities are smoother because the system is so clear and consistent.
q2: The training was decent. I got enough to be functional but would have liked more depth on some of the features. It felt like a one-size-fits-all session when different room setups really have different needs.
q3: I was aware a change was coming but didn't feel like I had great visibility into the specifics. More proactive communication — maybe a weekly update during the rollout — would have been welcome.
q4: The audio performance in my large space is the biggest win. It reaches everywhere and the quality is consistent. The system also hasn't gone down on me, which I appreciate.
q5: Just the communication piece. I would have felt more comfortable with the transition if I'd had more information up front about what was happening and when.

name: Margaret Parker
q1: It's been a positive change overall. The new system covers my entire room much better than the old one, and announcements come through clearly. I've adjusted to it pretty quickly and it hasn't caused any real disruption to my teaching routine.
q2: Training was good enough. I felt prepared for the basics, and the session was well-paced. I would have appreciated a follow-up opportunity to ask questions once I'd been using the system for a few weeks, but the initial session was solid.
q3: Communication was a weak spot. I knew something was changing but the details were slow to arrive. I had to ask around to get answers to pretty basic questions about the timeline. A simple email update schedule would have solved that.
q4: The sound coverage. In a large classroom like mine, that's everything. The system delivers clear, even audio throughout the space, and it's been very dependable.
q5: Nothing

name: Susan Thompson
q1: Honestly, it has been pretty disruptive. I feel like I'm constantly adjusting my lesson flow because of unexpected announcements or system hiccups. The timing of bells has thrown off my class transitions more than once, and I've lost instructional minutes dealing with it.
q2: The training was okay but felt rushed. I left with more questions than answers, and by the time I actually needed to use certain features, I'd already forgotten what was covered. A follow-up session would have been helpful.
q3: I didn't feel well-informed at all. I heard about the switch mostly through hallway conversations with other teachers. An email or two came out, but they were vague. It would have been nice to have a clear timeline and know what to expect before installation started.
q4: The sound quality is decent when it works properly.
q5: The disruption to my daily schedule has been significant. I wish the rollout had been planned in a way that minimized impact on actual teaching time. Also, the volume levels seem inconsistent throughout the day.

name: Daniel Bailey
q1: I've been teaching long enough to have gone through several system changes, and this one has been middling. Not the worst transition I've seen, but the workflow disruption during the first few weeks was noticeable. It's settling down now.
q2: The training left something to be desired. I felt like it was designed for people who already understood the system rather than those of us coming in cold. More hands-on time would have helped.
q3: Communication was sparse. I'm new to this district so I was already playing catch-up, and learning about a major system change through a brief mention at a staff meeting wasn't ideal. A dedicated informational handout or FAQ would have gone a long way.
q4: N/A
q5: The reliability has been hit or miss. Some days it works fine, other days there are random glitches. I'd like to see more consistency before I can say it's an improvement over the old setup.

name: Catherine Baker
q1: It hasn't made my life easier or harder in a dramatic way, but the system has had enough reliability issues that I don't fully trust it yet. I remember when they installed the previous system back in the late 2000s and that transition was smoother. There have been a few mornings where announcements just didn't come through in my room at all.
q2: Training was adequate. I've been through enough of these rollouts to know what questions to ask, and the trainer was responsive when I raised concerns. The materials were decent.
q3: They communicated it reasonably well. I got the emails and there was discussion at department meetings. Could have started the conversation earlier in the year, but it wasn't a total surprise.
q4: Nothing stands out as a major improvement yet.
q5: The reliability needs work. I've had two instances where the intercom didn't function properly during what would have been important announcements. After 29 years here, I know what reliable looks like, and this isn't it yet.

name: Emily Campbell
q1: The system itself seems solid and reliable, which I appreciate. However, the transition period disrupted my classroom routines more than I expected. Getting used to the new bell tones and announcement patterns took some adjustment, and it affected pacing in my classes for a couple of weeks.
q2: I felt like the training could have been more thorough. We got the basics but I still don't feel confident using some of the more advanced features. I had to figure out a few things on my own through trial and error.
q3: Communication was fine overall. I knew it was coming and had a general sense of the timeline. I would have appreciated more detail about what specifically was changing and how it would affect classroom teachers day to day.
q4: The system is very reliable. I haven't had any technical failures, which is a huge improvement. When announcements come through, they're clear and consistent.
q5: The workflow disruption during the transition was harder than it needed to be. I think a phased rollout or at least better preparation for teachers on what to expect during installation would have helped a lot.

name: Michelle Johnson
q1: It's been a bit of a headache during the transition. I had my routines dialed in and the changeover disrupted the flow I'd built over years. The system works when it works, but the adjustment period was longer than I would have liked.
q2: Training was average. It covered the essentials but didn't go deep enough for me to feel fully prepared. I've been here 25 years and I know how to figure things out, but newer staff might have struggled more.
q3: I heard about it but didn't feel like the communication gave me a full picture. There were emails but they were high-level. What would have helped is someone sitting down with us at a department meeting and walking through the specifics, especially the timeline and what we'd need to do differently.
q4: I will say the hardware seems well-built. No issues with the actual equipment.
q5: The disruption to daily workflow was my main frustration. I also think the pacing of the rollout could have been better coordinated with the academic calendar.

name: Karen Baker
q1: Not great. The system has been unreliable enough that I've stopped counting on announcements actually coming through clearly. I've had to build in extra buffer time to check if my students actually heard what was said, and that eats into instructional time.
q2: Training was fine, nothing special. I didn't walk away feeling unprepared, but I also didn't walk away feeling excited about the new system. It was very by-the-book.
q3: Communication was middling. I knew it was happening but the details were fuzzy. I would have liked to know more about why this particular system was chosen and what improvements we were supposed to see.
q5: The reliability is my biggest concern. Glitchy audio, dropped announcements, inconsistent volume. After 18 years here I've seen upgrades that actually felt like upgrades, and this one hasn't cleared that bar yet.

name: Carolyn Collins
q1: Overall it's been a positive change for me. The system works well and hasn't caused major disruptions to my teaching. I adapted to it quickly.
q2: The training was really good. It was well-organized and the trainer made time for questions. I felt prepared to use the system by the time the session ended.
q3: This is where I have a complaint. I'm relatively new here and I felt completely blindsided by the change. No one told me it was coming until literally the week of installation. I found out from a colleague, not from any official communication. That needs to be better.
q4: It's reliable and the audio quality is clear. The interface for the classroom unit is intuitive.
q5: Just the communication issue. I shouldn't have to hear about a major building-wide change through the grapevine.

name: Shonda Brooks
q1: It's been a mixed bag. The system functions adequately on most days, but there have been enough technical issues to make me wary. I wouldn't say it's dramatically changed my workflow, but the interruptions from troubleshooting have been frustrating.
q2: Training was decent. I felt prepared enough to use the basic functions and appreciated that they gave us reference materials to take back to our classrooms.
q3: Communication was excellent, actually. I felt very well-informed throughout the process. The administration did a good job of keeping us updated with timelines, rationale, and what to expect. This was probably the strongest part of the whole rollout.
q4: The communication from admin about the change was handled well. That part deserves recognition.
q5: The reliability needs improvement. I've experienced enough audio dropouts and static to be concerned, especially during emergency-type announcements. That can't be acceptable.

name: Graciela Mendoza
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. The transition period was rocky, and even now I feel like the system isn't quite where it should be. My daily routine has been affected mostly by inconsistent bell timing and announcements that sometimes cut into instruction.
q2: The training was just okay. It covered the basics but I walked out of there feeling like I only got half the picture. I would have liked a second session once we'd had some time to actually use the system and formulate real questions.
q3: I didn't feel particularly well-informed. There was some communication but it was scattered and not very detailed. As someone who's been here over a decade, I expected more transparency about why the change was being made and what the expected benefits were.
q5: The reliability is inconsistent and the transition was poorly timed. I'd change the rollout schedule to avoid doing this kind of work during the academic year.

name: Linda Chung
q1: It's actually been a positive experience for me. The new system integrates well into my daily routines and I've found it pretty easy to work with. If anything, transitions between periods feel smoother now.
q2: The training was sufficient but I think it could have been a bit more comprehensive. I'm fairly tech-savvy so I figured things out, but I could see how others might have struggled with some of the features.
q3: Communication was a bit lacking. I got the general idea that a change was coming, but specifics were hard to come by until it was already happening. More proactive communication would have been welcome.
q4: The workflow improvement has been noticeable. Things feel more streamlined and the audio quality is clear. I'm glad the district invested in this.
q5: No major complaints. Maybe just better advance notice next time.

name: Mei Zhang
q1: The new system has been a moderate adjustment. It works well enough that I don't dread using it, and I've noticed some improvements in audio clarity compared to what we had before. Day-to-day impact has been fairly neutral overall.
q2: I was pleased with the training. It was thorough and well-paced, and the facilitator was knowledgeable. I left feeling confident about using the core features.
q3: Communication was adequate but not exceptional. I was aware the change was coming, though I would have appreciated earlier notification and perhaps a chance to provide input before the decision was finalized.
q4: The audio quality is a clear improvement and the system feels well-designed. I also appreciate that the training was done thoughtfully.
q5: Nothing major. I think the system is solid.

name: Raul Flores
q1: It's been an adjustment. I'm generally open to new technology and was optimistic going in, but the day-to-day reality has been a slight downgrade in terms of workflow. Some features I rely on don't feel as intuitive as the old system.
q2: The training was solid. I didn't have any major issues with it and thought the pace was appropriate. Having a hands-on component was useful.
q3: I was informed but it felt very top-down. We were told what was happening rather than asked for input. A survey or focus group beforehand would have been a nice touch.
q4: I appreciate the effort to modernize. The interface looks updated and the emergency features seem well-designed.
q5: Some of the daily-use features feel clunkier than what we had. I'd also say the volume levels need calibrating in some rooms — mine tends to be too loud during announcements.

name: Noah Dubois
q1: It's been a neutral to slightly positive change. The system works, my day proceeds roughly the same way it did before. Some of the bells took getting used to but that's minor.
q2: Training was passable. Nothing memorable but I didn't feel lost either. I figured out most of what I needed through daily use.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. Administration sent out communications and addressed it at staff meetings. I knew what was happening and roughly when. No major complaints there.
q4: It works. Announcements are generally clear and the system hasn't had any major failures that I've noticed.
q5: The reliability could be a touch better — there's an occasional glitch where audio cuts out mid-announcement. It's not frequent but it's noticeable after 18 years of the old system working consistently.

name: Deborah Mitchell
q1: It has been noticeably disruptive to my teaching. The transition period was rough, and even now I find that the timing of announcements and bells doesn't quite align with how I've structured my classes over the past 19 years. I've had to rework my daily schedule and it's been frustrating.
q2: Training was mediocre. It felt like a checklist exercise rather than a genuine attempt to help us transition smoothly. I would have appreciated more time for questions and scenarios specific to classroom teachers.
q3: Actually, the communication was one of the better aspects. I was kept in the loop about what was happening and when. I wish they'd communicated more about the why, but the logistical information was there.
q4: The communication about the change was handled well.
q5: The impact on my daily workflow has been the most frustrating part. I also question whether the system is truly more reliable than what we had — it feels about the same to me, just with a learning curve on top.

name: Rebecca White
q1: Honestly, the transition has been smoother than I expected. Once I got past the initial learning curve, the system has fit well into my daily routine. I'm cautiously optimistic about it.
q2: The training was genuinely helpful. It was one of the better professional development sessions I've attended, and the trainer was responsive to our questions. I felt ready to use the system afterward.
q3: Communication was disappointing. I've been here 24 years and I feel like I had to find out about this change on my own rather than being properly informed by the district. An email a week before installation isn't adequate communication for something this significant.
q4: The training was excellent, and the system itself works well for daily classroom use. I'm happy with how it sounds and functions.
q5: Reliability is my concern. There have been random outages and audio quality drops that seem to happen without explanation. I've reported them but haven't heard back about fixes.

name: Nancy Wu
q1: The new system has been a welcome change. It works smoothly and I've found that my daily routines have adjusted well. Announcements come through clearly and on time, which makes managing class transitions easier.
q2: Training covered the essentials but could have gone deeper on some of the features I'm most interested in, like the scheduling and zone controls. I figured those out on my own but it took some time.
q3: I was well-informed. The district did a good job communicating the timeline and the reasons behind the switch. I felt prepared for it.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. I haven't experienced a single malfunction. The audio is crystal clear and consistent. This is a genuine upgrade from what we had before.
q5: No real complaints. The training could have been slightly more in-depth but that's a minor point.

name: Stephanie Reed
q1: The system works well technically, but the transition has disrupted my classroom flow. Announcements come at slightly different times than I'm used to and it's thrown off the pacing of my lessons. I've been adapting but it's been a process.
q2: Training was good. I felt well-prepared and the trainers were approachable. The session was well-structured and gave us time to practice.
q3: I was kept informed throughout the process, which I appreciated. The emails and staff meeting updates were clear and timely. This is how changes should be communicated.
q4: The system is reliable and the training was well done. I also appreciate that administration was transparent about the rollout process.
q5: The disruption to classroom workflow has been real. I'd suggest that future technology changes be timed to coincide with a break so teachers have a natural adjustment period.

name: Aiden Medeiros
q1: Since I'm new here, I don't have much to compare it to, but the system seems to work fine for daily use. It hasn't caused any major issues for me.
q2: Training was helpful. I appreciated having a structured session to learn the system since I didn't have context on the old one.
q3: I felt informed. Even as a new staff member, the communication I received about the change was clear and gave me what I needed to know.
q4: It works well for everyday use. Clear audio, consistent performance.

name: Linh Wu
q1: Coming from where I was before, this system is leagues ahead. I'm genuinely impressed by how well it functions compared to what I was used to. My daily work has been a slight adjustment, but the technology itself is not the problem — it's just learning the routines at a new school.
q2: The training was decent but I already felt somewhat familiar with this type of system because my previous district's setup was so limited that I'd done a lot of troubleshooting on my own. A refresher on the specific features here would have been nice.
q3: Communication was reasonable. I received information through the normal channels and had a general understanding of what was happening. Being new, I didn't have strong expectations one way or the other.
q4: The reliability is excellent. At my previous school, we dealt with constant breakdowns and workarounds. This system just works. I don't take that for granted.
q5: I'd say the workflow impact during the transition could have been smoother, but compared to what I've experienced in the past, this is miles better. No real complaints.

name: Jose Torres
q1: It's been a mixed experience. I'm generally positive about upgrading technology, but the system has been unreliable enough to be a distraction. There have been days where I wasn't sure if an announcement had actually gone out or if the system just didn't work in my room.
q2: The training was underwhelming. It felt like it was thrown together quickly and didn't cover enough of the practical, day-to-day stuff I actually needed. I wanted more and didn't get it.
q3: Communication was actually quite good. I knew what was happening, when, and why. The administration was upfront about the timeline and kept us updated. I have no issues there.
q4: The communication from leadership about this project was well handled. I also like the concept of the new system — when it works, it's great.
q5: Reliability is my number one issue. Too many glitches, inconsistent sound, and unexplained outages. I want to be enthusiastic about this but the system needs to earn my trust through consistent performance.

name: Erin Stewart
q1: It has honestly been a disruption. The system itself seems to work okay, but the constant interruptions and adjustments during my class periods have made it harder to keep lessons on track. I feel like I lose five minutes every time there's an announcement I wasn't expecting.
q2: The training was adequate but not great. I walked away with a basic understanding of how to use the intercom features, but I still had to figure some things out on my own afterward.
q3: I got some emails about the timeline, which was fine, but I wish there had been more detail about how the installation would actually impact the school day. A heads-up about specific disruptions would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality is noticeably better than the old system.
q5: The number of interruptions during instructional time has been frustrating. I would like some kind of schedule or protocol for when announcements happen so teachers can plan around them.

name: Mariana Lopez
q1: Day to day it's been manageable, though I've had some intermittent issues with the system cutting out or making a buzzing sound. After 31 years here I've been through several system changes and this one has been somewhere in the middle in terms of disruption.
q2: Training was actually pretty decent. The hands-on session was helpful and I felt comfortable using the new panel in my room after that. I appreciated that they didn't rush through it.
q3: Honestly, I felt blindsided. I found out about the switch when contractors showed up in the B building hallway. I've been here over three decades and I don't think it's too much to ask for a staff meeting or at least a formal announcement before something like this happens. An email buried in a Friday newsletter doesn't count.
q5: The reliability has been inconsistent. Some days it works perfectly, other days I get static or it just doesn't respond when I try to use the call button. I reported it twice and I'm still waiting.

name: Alma Garcia
q1: It's been okay overall. Not a huge improvement but not terrible either. My daily routine hasn't changed dramatically because of it.
q2: The training felt rushed and incomplete. We got one session and that was it. I had questions afterward and there was no follow-up opportunity. I ended up asking a colleague in room 214 how to adjust the volume on the classroom unit because the training didn't cover that.
q3: I was not informed. I came back from winter break and there was new equipment in my classroom. Nobody told me it was happening, nobody asked for input, nothing. This is how it always goes around here. Twenty years and they still don't loop us in on decisions that directly affect our teaching spaces.
q4: The system does seem to function reliably when I use it. I haven't had technical issues.
q5: The rollout communication was nonexistent from my perspective. I would change the entire process of how decisions like this are shared with staff.

name: Angela Howard
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some things are a little easier now, like the two-way communication feature, but there have also been glitches that interrupt my lessons. Overall I'd say it's a lateral move so far.
q2: Training was fine. Not spectacular but I got the basics down. I do wish they had offered a refresher a few weeks after we started using it, because that's when the real questions come up.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew it was coming and roughly when, though the exact timeline shifted a couple of times. A more concrete schedule shared earlier would have been helpful.
q4: The two-way call feature to the front office is genuinely useful. I've used it a few times and it's much faster than the old process of sending a student down with a note.
q5: There have been occasional glitches where the speaker makes a popping noise during class. It's startling for the students and breaks the flow of instruction.

name: Patricia Foster
q1: The new system has been somewhat disruptive. I've had multiple instances where announcements come through garbled or the system just doesn't work, and those interruptions during my AP classes are particularly costly in terms of lost instructional time. The old system had its problems too, but at least it was predictable.
q2: Training was solid. The facilitator was knowledgeable and patient with questions. I felt well-prepared to use the system, which makes the reliability issues all the more frustrating.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. There were emails and a presentation at a staff meeting. The timeline was communicated clearly enough.
q4: N/A
q5: Reliability. The system needs to work consistently before anything else matters. I've submitted work orders twice in the last month for my classroom unit alone. When it works, the sound quality is better than the old system. But it doesn't always work.

name: Lisa Anderson
q1: It's been fine, I suppose. At my previous district the communications system was seamless and basically invisible — you never had to think about it. Here, I feel like I'm constantly aware of the system in a way I shouldn't have to be.
q2: The training was pretty weak. One session, very surface-level. I'm used to systems that come with proper onboarding and reference materials. Here it was kind of learn-as-you-go, which isn't ideal when you're also new to the district.
q3: I was informed about the change and the general timeline, which was fine. No major complaints there.
q4: The emergency alert function seems well-designed. That's important and I'm glad they got that part right.
q5: Compared to what I've worked with before, this system feels a step behind. The interface is less intuitive, the audio quality is just okay, and there are small lag issues that add up. I'd want the district to benchmark against what other districts are using.

name: Cynthia Simmons
q1: I've actually been pleasantly surprised. The new system has been reliable and the improvements to sound clarity have been noticeable. After 26 years of dealing with the old intercom crackling through every announcement, this is a welcome change. My workflow hasn't been negatively impacted.
q2: The training was well-organized and thorough. I appreciated that they provided a step-by-step handout I could refer back to. I'm not the most tech-savvy person, but I felt confident using the system after the session.
q3: Communication was good. I knew what was happening and when. The principal kept us updated at staff meetings and there were follow-up emails. I would have liked maybe one more update during the actual installation week, but overall it was handled well.
q4: The sound quality is excellent, and the system has been rock solid. I also like the integration with the emergency protocols — it feels much more secure than what we had before.

name: Jade Okamoto
q1: It's been a real adjustment and not entirely for the better. The transition period was rough — there were days when nothing worked properly and I had no way to communicate with the office from my classroom in the 300 wing. Now that it's up and running, I still find that it interrupts my teaching more than the old system did.
q2: Training was okay. Not bad, not great. I learned the basics but felt like there were features nobody really explained well.
q3: Communication could have been better. I got the general idea that changes were coming, but the specifics were vague. Dates kept shifting and no one seemed to have a definitive answer about when my part of the building would be affected.
q4: The call button response time to the office is faster now.
q5: The disruption to instructional time has been my biggest issue. Announcements seem louder and more frequent, and the system doesn't have a way for teachers to temporarily mute non-emergency messages during testing or critical instruction. That feature would be a game-changer.

name: Maria Ramirez
q1: It's been a mostly positive change for me. The system works well and I find the features helpful. I'm still getting used to everything at this school so it's hard to compare to what was here before, but it seems like a good system.
q2: The training covered the essentials. I felt like I could use the system afterward, though I would have appreciated a quick-reference card or something to keep at my desk.
q3: I heard about it but the communication felt a bit scattered. I wasn't always sure where to find updates or who to ask questions. A centralized page or FAQ would have been useful.
q4: I like the overall sound quality and the ease of use. It's intuitive once you get the hang of it.
q5: Nothing major. Maybe more signage or instructions posted near the units for substitutes who aren't familiar with the system.

name: Rocio Martinez
q1: The transition has been disruptive to my daily routine. I've been teaching here long enough to remember the last two system changes, and this one has probably had the most impact on my instructional flow. The installation itself was chaotic — workers in and out of the 400 hallway for weeks — and now I'm dealing with more frequent interruptions than before.
q2: I thought the training was well done. The trainer was clear and approachable, and the pace was appropriate. I actually feel like this was one of the better professional development sessions we've had.
q3: We were told it was happening but the details were thin. I would have liked a building-level meeting where we could ask questions and give input before the decision was finalized, not after.
q4: I'm open to new technology and I can see the potential. The emergency features are a clear upgrade.
q5: The volume of non-essential announcements needs to be addressed. The system is only as good as how it's used, and right now it's being used to broadcast things that could easily be an email. That's not a technology problem, it's a policy problem.

name: Andrea Roberts
q1: Since I'm new to the district, I don't have much to compare it to. The system seems to work reasonably well and hasn't caused major issues for me. A few hiccups here and there but nothing that derailed my day.
q2: Training was average. It got the job done but wasn't particularly memorable.
q3: I felt very well-informed. As a new hire, all the communications about the system change were part of my onboarding, so I had a clear picture of what was happening and why. I think they did a nice job keeping people in the loop.
q4: The integration with lockdown procedures is reassuring. Knowing I can communicate quickly with administration in an emergency gives me peace of mind.
q5: The reliability could be better. I've had a few instances where the system was unresponsive, and as someone new to the building, I wasn't sure if it was a building-wide issue or something specific to my room.

name: Crystal Jones
q1: The system itself seems solid, but the way it's being used has negatively impacted my classroom. More announcements, more interruptions. I've lost count of how many times I've been mid-lesson and had to stop because of a broadcast that could have waited.
q2: Training was insufficient. One session is not enough to fully understand a new system, especially when there are features we weren't even shown. I had to ask colleagues and figure things out through trial and error. After 21 years I know how to adapt, but that doesn't mean the training was adequate.
q3: Communication was excellent. I'll give credit where it's due — the administration was transparent about the timeline, the reasons for the change, and what to expect. This was handled well on the information side.
q4: The hardware is reliable. I haven't had a single malfunction in my classroom, which is more than I can say for the old system that used to cut out during morning announcements at least twice a month.

name: Elena Aguilar
q1: It's been kind of frustrating honestly. Where I was before, the system worked smoothly and you never really thought about it. Here it feels clunkier and I've had to adjust my routines because of unexpected announcements and timing issues.
q2: The training was bare minimum. I didn't feel prepared to troubleshoot anything, and when I had issues the first week I didn't know who to contact.
q3: I got some information but not enough context. Being new, I didn't know what the old system was like, so the communications didn't really land for me. Something geared toward new staff would have helped.
q4: It works most of the time, I guess.
q5: The system feels like a downgrade from what I had at my last school. The interface isn't as clean, and the audio has a slight delay that's noticeable when you're used to something better. I'd want the district to look at how other districts handle this.

name: Dwayne Price
q1: It's been okay. Not a huge change day to day. Some things are better, some aren't. I've adapted.
q2: Training was lacking. I felt like they skimmed through the important stuff and spent too much time on features most of us will never use. A more targeted session for classroom teachers would have been better.
q3: I was aware it was coming, got a few emails. That was fine. I didn't feel left in the dark.
q4: No.
q5: The system occasionally has connectivity issues that I've had to report. Nothing catastrophic but annoying when you're trying to reach the office and the call doesn't go through.

name: Casey Taylor
q1: The impact has been modest. There's been a slight uptick in interruptions during class, which is annoying, but the system itself functions reasonably well. I wouldn't say it's dramatically changed my work one way or the other.
q2: Training was decent. I appreciated the hands-on component and felt like the key features were covered. I would have liked a follow-up session once we'd had a chance to actually use the system for a few weeks.
q3: I felt well-informed. The communications were clear and timely, and I knew what to expect before the installation began in our area of the building.
q4: The clarity of announcements is better than the old system. I can actually understand what's being said now, which is a meaningful improvement.
q5: I'd like a way to control announcement volume at the classroom level, or at least a do-not-disturb mode during testing periods. The current setup doesn't give teachers any agency over the interruptions.

name: Marvin Porter
q1: It's been a step backward from what I'm used to. At my previous district, the system was more modern and integrated, and it just worked without anyone having to think about it. This one requires more manual intervention and has caused real disruptions to my teaching.
q2: Training was inadequate. I came in expecting a thorough walkthrough and got a brief overview that left me with more questions than answers. No reference materials, no follow-up support.
q3: I was told about the change and given some timeline info. It was acceptable I suppose.
q4: Nothing.
q5: Frankly, the whole system feels outdated compared to what's available. The audio quality is mediocre, the interface is not user-friendly, and it has introduced unnecessary complexity into my day. I don't understand why the district didn't invest in something more current.

name: Rachel Davis
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. I'm generally open to new things and I can see the potential, but right now it's adding some friction to my day that I didn't have before. The old system in the 200 building was second nature to all of us.
q2: Honestly, the training was poor. It was too short, too surface-level, and didn't account for the fact that different people have different comfort levels with technology. I needed more time and more practice, and I know I'm not alone in that.
q3: Communication was not great. I heard rumors before I heard anything official, and by the time the formal announcement came it felt like old news. The whole process felt like it was designed to check a box rather than genuinely keep staff informed.
q4: I like that the district is investing in updated infrastructure. The intent behind the change is good even if the execution has been bumpy.
q5: Better training and better advance communication would solve most of my complaints. The technology itself is fine — it's the human side of the rollout that fell short.

name: Michelle Carter
q1: It's been a slight negative overall. The system works but has added some unpredictability to my day. Announcements come at odd times and the transition period involved too many disruptions during instruction.
q2: The training was really well done. The trainer was engaging, the materials were clear, and I felt confident using the system by the end. This was actually one of the better trainings I've attended in my ten years here.
q3: Communication was lacking. I didn't feel like I had a clear picture of what was changing, when, or why until pretty late in the process. Earlier and more detailed communication would have made a big difference.
q4: The training itself was the highlight of this whole process.
q5: I'd improve the communication around the rollout significantly. Teachers need to know how and when their classrooms will be affected so they can plan accordingly. Also, the announcement scheduling could use some guardrails to protect instructional time.

name: Maria Vasquez
q1: Coming from where I was before, this is honestly a big upgrade. The system is more reliable and the features are way beyond what I was working with previously. It's been a positive change for my daily work.
q2: Training was great. Very thorough and well-paced. I felt ready to use everything right away, which was nice since I'm still fairly new to the profession and didn't want one more thing to stress about.
q3: The communication could have been better. I didn't always know where to find updates and some of the information came through informal channels rather than official ones. A single source of truth — like a dedicated webpage or shared document — would have been really helpful.
q4: The audio quality and reliability are excellent compared to what I've experienced before. The emergency features are also really impressive. I feel much safer here.
q5: Just the communication piece. The system itself is great.

name: Lucia Rivera
q1: The system works perfectly — I haven't had a single technical issue. But it hasn't really improved my day-to-day work either. If anything, the frequency of announcements has gone up now that the system is easier to use, which cuts into my instructional time.
q2: Training was okay. It covered the basics but didn't go deep enough into the features that would be most useful for classroom teachers. I felt like it was designed for administrators and we were an afterthought.
q3: Communication was poor. I found out details from colleagues more often than from official channels. For a project this size, I expected town halls or at least a dedicated Q&A session. None of that happened.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. It works every single time, which is a massive improvement over the old system that would go down at least once a month.
q5: The district needs to establish clear guidelines about when and how often the system is used for announcements. Having a perfectly reliable system is great, but if it's being used to broadcast every minor update, it becomes a distraction rather than a tool.

name: Marisol Morales
q1: Honestly, it's been a pretty smooth transition. Compared to what I was working with before I came to this district, this system is leagues ahead. I can actually hear announcements clearly in my classroom, and the integration with the bell schedule has been seamless for the most part.
q2: The training was well organized. I appreciated that they gave us hands-on time with the equipment rather than just a slideshow. The follow-up materials were helpful too.
q3: I felt very well informed throughout the process. The emails and staff meeting updates kept us in the loop, and I knew what to expect before the installation even started. I honestly can't think of anything that was missing communication-wise.
q4: The audio quality is noticeably better than what I've experienced at other places I've worked. The emergency alert features give me real peace of mind. At my previous district, we didn't even have half of this capability.
q5: There have been a couple of minor glitches with the intercom cutting out briefly, but nothing that really disrupted my day. I'd maybe like to see a quick-reference card posted near the wall unit.

name: Darlene Jackson
q1: The system works fine day-to-day. I haven't had any major issues with it affecting my teaching routine, and if anything it's made a few things slightly easier, like knowing exactly when passing periods start because the tones are consistent now.
q2: The training was underwhelming. I went to the session they offered and it felt rushed. I had questions about specific features and there wasn't time to address them. I ended up figuring most of it out on my own, which is frustrating after nineteen years here.
q3: Communication was okay but could have been better. I heard about the switch through the grapevine before I ever got an official email about it. A timeline shared earlier in the process would have helped me plan around the disruptions.
q4: The sound quality is good and the system is reliable. I haven't had it go down on me.
q5: The training piece really needs to be revisited. Also, I'd like the option to adjust the volume in my own classroom without having to call someone.

name: Matthew Baker
q1: It's been disruptive, frankly. The system has had enough hiccups that I've lost instructional time dealing with random announcements cutting in at odd moments or the intercom not working when I actually need it. I was perfectly fine with what we had before and I don't understand why we needed to change.
q2: Training was adequate, I suppose. I attended and took notes. But no amount of training fixes a system that doesn't work consistently. I would have preferred they spent the training budget on getting the old system properly maintained.
q3: They communicated the timeline well enough. I knew it was coming. My issue isn't with the communication about the change, it's with the change itself.
q5: The reliability is not where it needs to be. I've had the system freeze up or produce static during class at least a handful of times. I've been in this building a long time and I've seen these rollouts come and go. I'd change the whole decision if I could, but at minimum they need to fix the intermittent connectivity issues in the B wing classrooms.

name: Tessa Achebe
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. I'm all for upgrading our technology, and I think the new system has potential, but the transition period has made some of my routines harder. The timing of announcements doesn't always line up with my class schedule the way it used to.
q2: Training was decent. Not amazing, not terrible. I felt like I got a basic understanding, but I still discover features I didn't know about. Maybe a follow-up session after a few weeks of use would be helpful.
q3: This is where I have the biggest concern. I felt like the decision was made and then we were told about it after the fact. I would have appreciated being consulted, or at least surveyed about our needs before the vendor was selected. I've been here almost twenty years and nobody asked what I thought.
q4: I like the modern interface and the fact that it integrates with the emergency notification system. That's genuinely an improvement.
q5: The rollout communication was poor. I'd change how the whole process was managed from a staff engagement perspective. Also, the volume seems inconsistent between rooms on my floor.

name: Graciela Ortiz
q1: It hasn't been great for my workflow. More interruptions than before, and I feel like the system is a solution looking for a problem. My daily routine has been disrupted by the installation process and the learning curve.
q2: The training was fine. Nothing special, but I learned what I needed to learn. I would have liked more time to practice with the actual equipment in my classroom rather than in the library.
q3: I didn't feel well-informed at all. I found out details from colleagues more than from official channels. A clear FAQ document sent out before the install would have gone a long way.
q4: The system itself seems reliable enough. I haven't had technical failures.
q5: I'd change the communication plan entirely. Also, the transition felt rushed. More lead time and better heads-up about when specific wings would be affected would help.

name: Steven Evans
q1: Day-to-day, it's been mostly neutral. A few minor improvements in clarity but also some headaches with the new interface. I wouldn't say it's changed my life either way, though the installation week was rough with workers in the hallway during my fourth period.
q2: Could have been better. The session I attended was too basic for the questions I had, and the handout they gave us was generic. I've been here long enough to know what I need, and the training didn't really address the specifics.
q3: Communication was passable. I got the emails. I knew roughly when things were happening. Not great, not horrible.
q4: The sound is clearer than the old system, I'll give it that.
q5: Training needs to be differentiated. Newer teachers and veteran teachers don't have the same questions. Also the intercom panel in my room is in an awkward spot on the wall.

name: Shonda Jackson
q1: It's honestly been kind of disruptive. I'm still getting used to how everything works at this school and then they changed the whole communications setup on top of that. The announcements seem to come at random times and it throws off my lesson flow.
q2: The training was okay but I felt a little lost. I'm newer to teaching so I didn't always know what questions to ask. A mentoring setup where I could shadow someone using the system would have been more helpful than the group session.
q3: I was informed reasonably well. My department chair shared most of the details with me, and there were some emails. It wasn't confusing, just a lot of change at once.
q4: I like that there's a page function so I can reach the front office quickly if I need to.
q5: The timing of announcements during instructional time is really my biggest gripe. I lose student attention every time the intercom comes on.

name: Allison Howard
q1: The transition has been a mixed bag. I appreciate the upgrade in principle, but in practice, the new system has introduced some workflow bumps. Announcements sometimes overlap with my teaching, and the interface took some getting used to. I've been teaching over twenty years and I've weathered a lot of these transitions.
q2: Training was pretty good, actually. The trainer was knowledgeable and patient. I felt comfortable using the basic features by the end of the session, though I still haven't explored everything the system can do.
q3: I was kept reasonably well informed. The principal did a good job sharing timelines and what to expect. I appreciated the updates at staff meetings. Maybe a dedicated page on the school website with FAQs would have been a nice addition.
q4: N/A
q5: The system seems to drop out occasionally, which is concerning. Also, I've noticed the announcements are louder in the hallway than in classrooms on the second floor. That inconsistency is annoying when you're mid-lesson and suddenly there's a booming announcement right outside your door.

name: Alejandra Torres
q1: It's been okay. I don't have a lot to compare it to since I'm fairly new here. It works most of the time, though there have been a few days where things seemed glitchy.
q2: I felt like the training moved quickly and assumed we had some background knowledge that I didn't really have. A beginner-level option would have been nice.
q3: I got some information but I wasn't always sure what was happening or when. Clearer timelines shared via email would have helped.
q4: The bell tones are clear and consistent.
q5: I wish the volume could be adjusted room by room. Sometimes it's too loud for my small classroom.

name: Rocio Ramirez
q1: I'll be honest, the system feels like a step backward from what I was used to before coming here. It's not terrible for daily use, but the reliability issues have been frustrating. I'm used to a system that just worked, and this one doesn't always.
q2: The training was actually solid. The materials were clear and the instructor was approachable. That part I have no complaints about.
q3: Communication was really lacking. I felt like I was the last to know about the installation schedule, and when I asked questions, nobody seemed to have answers. At my last school, this kind of rollout was handled with much more transparency.
q4: The training resources they provided are good and the interface is user-friendly once you learn it.
q5: The reliability needs serious attention. I've had the system go completely silent during what was supposed to be an announcement multiple times. Where I worked before, this kind of thing just didn't happen. I'd also change the communication approach because being blindsided by construction in your hallway is not acceptable.

name: Mariana Contreras
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. I've had to adjust my routines around the new announcement schedule, and there have been enough technical hiccups to be annoying. Twenty years in this building and the old system, while outdated, at least worked predictably.
q2: Training was middle of the road. Covered the basics, but I had to figure out some of the more advanced features myself. I would have appreciated a second session after we'd all had a few weeks to actually use the system.
q3: I got the general information but details were lacking. The timeline kept shifting and nobody sent updated information when things changed. That's what frustrated me most.
q4: The emergency alert integration is a real positive. I feel more confident about lockdown communication than I did with the old system.
q5: Fix the reliability. Some days it works perfectly, other days it cuts in and out. Also, the volume in the 200 hall classrooms seems noticeably quieter than other areas.

name: Nicole Hampton
q1: It's been fine, I guess. Not a dramatic change one way or the other. Some days the system works great, other days there are little issues. It hasn't fundamentally changed how I teach.
q2: The training was insufficient. I've been teaching for sixteen years across multiple schools and that was one of the least helpful professional development sessions I've attended. Too short, too surface-level, no time for real questions.
q3: I was somewhat informed. The emails came, but they were vague. I'd rather have one detailed communication than five brief ones that don't really say anything useful.
q5: Better training, full stop. And I'd like to see a feedback mechanism built into the rollout process so we can report issues in real time rather than waiting for a survey months later.

name: Linh Park
q1: The new system has been mostly neutral for my daily work. A few small improvements, a few small annoyances. The announcement clarity is better, but the frequency of intercom use seems to have gone up, which is distracting during lessons.
q2: I thought the training was very well done. The hands-on portion was especially useful, and I liked that they provided a reference guide we could take back to our classrooms. I felt prepared to use the system on day one.
q3: Communication was just okay. I knew the change was coming but wasn't clear on exactly how it would affect my classroom until the installers showed up. More specific, room-level information would have been appreciated.
q4: Sound quality is improved. The training materials are excellent and easy to reference.
q5: The intercom interruptions during class are more frequent now and I'd like to see a policy change around when non-emergency announcements are made. Limit them to passing periods.

name: Tessa Dubois
q1: It hasn't really disrupted my workflow much. I adapted pretty quickly and my students seem to ignore the announcements the same as they always have. If anything, the clearer audio means fewer students asking me what was just said.
q2: The training was okay. Not the best I've ever attended, but serviceable. I would have liked more advanced tips for the features beyond basic announcements.
q3: Information came in dribs and drabs. I felt somewhat informed but had to piece things together from different sources. A centralized hub for all the information about the project would have been useful.
q4: The system hasn't given me problems in terms of my daily routine. It just kind of works in the background, which is what you want.
q5: The reliability could be better. I've had a couple of instances where the speaker in my room produced a loud pop or static burst. Startled me and the kids both times. That needs to be fixed.

name: Amara Achebe
q1: I've been through three of these system changes over my career in this district. This one has been about average in terms of disruption. The new system does its job most of the time, but I've had enough intermittent issues to be skeptical about long-term reliability. We said the same things about the last system when it was new.
q2: Training was decent. Better than some rollouts I've lived through, worse than others. The trainer clearly knew the product. I just wish we'd had time for more Q&A.
q3: Communication was lacking. After thirty-plus years, I've learned that unless you actively seek out information around here, you won't get it. The emails were vague and the timeline was unclear. A printed memo with specific dates would have been more useful than the digital approach they took.
q4: The sound quality is an improvement over the old system when it's actually working properly.
q5: I'd want to see better reliability before I'm sold on this. Also, the announcement schedule needs to be revisited. Too many non-essential interruptions during instructional time.

name: Jerome Howard
q1: It's been an adjustment. The system works reasonably well, but I'm used to something more polished from where I taught before. Day-to-day it handles the basics, but some of the features feel clunky compared to what I had previously.
q2: Training was disappointing. I came in expecting to learn the ins and outs of the system and left feeling like I'd only scratched the surface. The session was too short and too many people were in the room to get individual help.
q3: I was informed well enough about the general timeline. The communication about what to expect during the transition was adequate. No major complaints there.
q4: The system is fairly reliable, which I appreciate. I haven't had it fail during critical moments.
q5: The training really needs an overhaul. Offer smaller group sessions so people can actually ask questions. And I'd suggest looking at how other districts handle their communications systems, because I've seen it done much more smoothly than this.

name: Julia Sullivan
q1: It's been a moderate adjustment. Some things work better, some don't. I've noticed the bell schedule is more consistent now, but I've also had a few mornings where the system seemed slow to respond when I tried to use the intercom.
q2: Training was good. I felt prepared afterward and the handouts have been a nice reference when I forget something. I think offering it was the right call.
q3: Communication was average. I knew the change was happening but wasn't always clear on specifics. Would have helped to have a single point of contact for questions rather than being told to email different people for different issues.
