q4: The bell schedule reliability is a genuine improvement. The old tones were inconsistent and this system is much more predictable.
q5: Nothing major. Minor intercom lag is slightly annoying but not a dealbreaker.

name: Maya Medeiros
q1: It's been frustrating. The system itself is fine when it works, but the whole process of switching over felt chaotic and poorly managed. I had no warning about when the installation would affect my classroom, and my students lost a full day of instruction because of it.
q2: The training was adequate. Not remarkable, but I learned what I needed. I appreciated that they offered make-up sessions for those who missed the first one.
q3: This is where things really fell apart. I was essentially blindsided by the entire project. I found out about the installation from the custodian, not from admin. No email, no heads-up, nothing until it was already happening. That is completely unacceptable. A simple calendar of when each wing would be affected, shared a month in advance, would have made a world of difference.
q4: None.
q5: The communication process was a disaster and whoever managed the rollout schedule needs to hear that directly. I also think the system has some reliability concerns, with occasional static and dropped connections that shouldn't be happening with brand-new equipment.

name: Luis Lopez
q1: It's been a slight positive overall. I've been in education a long time and I've seen far worse transitions. The system generally works and hasn't caused major disruptions to my classes.
q2: The training was a bit thin. After 26 years of teaching, I know what I need to know, but the session didn't even cover all the basic functions. I had to experiment on my own to figure out the paging feature.
q3: I felt well-informed. The administration here did a good job of keeping us posted about the timeline and rationale. I appreciated the transparency about why we were making the switch.
q4: The system is reliable and the sound quality is clear. Emergency announcements come through crisp and loud, which matters.
q5: Just the training. Give us more time and more depth. Twenty-six years in and I can still learn something new if you teach it properly.

name: Diane Johnson
q1: Since I'm new to the district, I don't have much to compare it to. The system seems to work okay for daily announcements and bells. I haven't noticed it making my work harder or easier.
q2: The training could have been better. As someone new to both the school and the profession, I needed more hands-on time. The session was fast-paced and assumed familiarity I didn't have.
q3: I didn't feel very informed, but that might partly be because I was new and still figuring out how communication works at this school in general. Clearer onboarding materials that include the new system information would help future new hires.
q4: The bells and tones are consistent and clear.
q5: I'd like to see more support resources available online, like video tutorials. Also, the speaker in my room sometimes has a slight buzz that's distracting.

name: Karen Sullivan
q1: I'm genuinely open to new technology and was excited about this transition, but the reality has been rough. The system has caused more disruptions than it's solved — bells going off at odd times, announcements cutting into instruction when I'm mid-lesson. I've had to restructure how I manage transitions because I can't rely on the timing the way I used to.
q2: The training was inadequate. We got one session that was basically a product demo, and then we were on our own. For a system this integral to daily operations, I expected hands-on practice and a follow-up session after we'd actually used it for a week or two.
q3: Communication was okay — we knew it was coming and roughly when. But I would have appreciated more detail about what specifically would change in terms of daily schedules and classroom functionality. A simple FAQ sheet would have gone a long way.
q4: The sound quality when it does work is noticeably clearer than the old system.
q5: The workflow disruption has been significant. I'd change the rollout approach — pilot it in one wing first, work out the kinks, then expand. Rolling it out everywhere at once created too many problems simultaneously.

name: Nancy Rao
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. I'm still getting used to how the new system integrates with the daily schedule, but it hasn't been a major issue for me. Some minor hiccups here and there.
q2: The training was decent. I felt like I got enough to get started, and I was able to figure out the rest as I went. It would have been helpful to have a quick reference guide to keep at my desk.
q3: I heard about the change when I started here, so I didn't have the old system to compare to. The information I received was sufficient, though I think longer-tenured staff may have had different needs.
q4: The audio clarity is good, and I like having a more modern system in place.

name: Erin Mitchell
q1: I want to love this system — I'm all for upgrades and modernization — but it has genuinely disrupted my workflow. The timing inconsistencies with bells and announcements have thrown off my lesson pacing more days than not. I've had to build in extra buffer time, which eats into instructional minutes.
q2: Training was just okay. It covered the basics but didn't address the specific scenarios we actually encounter, like what to do when the system glitches mid-class or how to adjust volume in individual rooms. More scenario-based training would have been useful.
q3: We were informed it was happening, but the timeline kept shifting. I remember being told one thing at a staff meeting and then getting an email that contradicted it. Consistent messaging from a single source would have helped.
q4: I appreciate that the district is investing in infrastructure. The potential is there — when the system works correctly, the announcements are clearer and more professional sounding.
q5: The disruption to daily routines has been harder than it needed to be. I would change the implementation timeline to allow for a longer testing phase before going live across the whole campus.

name: Sofia Reyes
q1: After thirty-plus years in education, I've been through several system changes. This one has been manageable. There are some interruptions, but I've adapted my routines around them. It hasn't dramatically changed my day-to-day.
q2: I would have liked more thorough training. The session we had felt rushed, and I still have questions about some of the features. I've mostly figured things out by asking colleagues, which shouldn't be necessary.
q3: The communication was adequate. I knew what was happening and when. I just wish there had been more acknowledgment that this kind of change is harder for some of us who've been doing things a certain way for a long time.
q4: The system itself seems reliable enough. I haven't had major technical issues, which I appreciate.
q5: I'd prefer if announcements could be scheduled more predictably so I can plan around them. The randomness of when they come through is the most disruptive part.

name: Micah Nwosu
q1: I've been here long enough to remember three different systems, and the transition period is always the worst part. This one has been disruptive — not catastrophically, but enough that I notice it daily. Announcements come at unpredictable times, and the bell schedule in the B building was off for nearly two weeks.
q2: Training was essentially nonexistent for those of us who couldn't attend the one scheduled session. I was covering for another teacher that day and never got a make-up opportunity. I've been learning by trial and error, which is frustrating.
q3: Poorly communicated. I found out about specific changes from other teachers more often than from administration. For something this significant, there should have been a dedicated communication plan — not just a mention at the end of a staff meeting.
q4: I'm optimistic it will be good once everything is dialed in. The interface is more intuitive than what we had before.
q5: The communication around the rollout was the weakest link. The technology itself I can work with, but being kept in the dark about timelines and changes made the whole process feel chaotic.

name: Lorena Reyes
q1: It's been a mixed bag. I've been at Valley for a long time and the old system had its problems too, so I'm not opposed to the change. But there have been reliability issues — the system cuts out sometimes, and I've had announcements that were garbled or didn't come through at all in room 214.
q2: The training was actually pretty good. I felt prepared to use the basic functions, and the trainer was knowledgeable. I'd give it a solid B.
q3: This is where things fell short. I heard rumors before anything official came out, which is never a good sign. When the official communication did come, it was vague on specifics. I would have wanted a clear timeline posted somewhere we could reference.
q4: N/A
q5: The reliability needs to improve. When I'm trying to get information to my students and the system isn't working, that's a real problem. I've had to send students to the office to get information that should have come through the intercom.

name: Christopher Johnson
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. I'm not against new systems, but this one has had enough glitches that it's been more of a headache than a help so far. The unreliable timing of bells has made passing periods confusing for students.
q2: The training didn't really prepare me for how to troubleshoot issues, which is what I've needed most. It was surface-level at best.
q3: I felt pretty blindsided honestly. I didn't get clear information about when things were changing or what to expect during the transition. An email a week before isn't sufficient communication for something that affects every period of every day.
q4: Not much yet, to be honest.
q5: Better communication before the change and better training on what to do when things go wrong. Those two things alone would have made this a much smoother experience.

name: Todd Phillips
q1: It's been neutral overall. Some days the system works fine and I don't think about it. Other days there are small issues that require workarounds. Nothing major, but it adds up over time.
q2: I didn't feel the training gave me what I needed. It was one session and then we were expected to just know how everything worked. For someone relatively new to this district, more support would have been appreciated.
q3: Communication was middle of the road. I knew something was changing but the details were sparse. I would have liked a written overview of what was changing and what to expect during the first few weeks.
q4: The system seems modern and capable when it's functioning properly.
q5: More robust training and a point person to contact when issues come up. Right now it feels like you just have to deal with whatever happens.

name: Emily Johnson
q1: It's been a slight disruption but nothing I can't manage. The transition period was bumpy — some inconsistencies with scheduling announcements — but things seem to be stabilizing. I'd say it's affected my work modestly.
q2: Training was fine. Not exceptional, but I came away feeling like I could use the system. I appreciated that they offered it, even if it could have been more in-depth.
q3: I felt a bit out of the loop. We got some information but it came late and didn't fully explain the scope of changes. A timeline with milestones would have been helpful so I could mentally prepare for each phase.
q4: The sound quality is better than the old system. Announcements are clearer and easier to understand.
q5: I'd improve the pre-change communication. The system itself will probably be fine once everything settles, but the transition could have been handled with more transparency.

name: Sofia Salazar
q1: Honestly, this system is a significant improvement over what I was working with before I came to this district. There have been some workflow adjustments, but the overall quality of the technology is much better than what I'm used to.
q2: The training was solid. I felt like they covered what I needed to know, and the materials they provided afterward were a nice reference. I've seen much worse onboarding for new systems.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed. As someone newer to the district, I was included in the communication and didn't feel left out of the loop. The emails were clear and timely for the most part.
q4: The reliability has been impressive compared to my previous experience. The audio is clear, the system responds consistently, and it feels like a real investment in the school's infrastructure.
q5: Some minor scheduling hiccups during the transition, but nothing that felt unusual for a system changeover. I don't have major complaints.

name: Kevin Lin
q1: It's disrupted things a bit. The system works well enough technically, but the transition created some confusion around scheduling and daily routines. I'm still adjusting to the new normal.
q2: Training was lacking. I understand the basics but there are features I know exist that were never covered. A follow-up session would help.
q3: Communication was good on this one. I felt like I knew what was happening and when. No real complaints there.
q4: The system itself is reliable. I haven't experienced outages or technical failures, which is good.
q5: The training gap is the main thing I'd address. The system has capabilities we're not using because nobody showed us how.

name: Rocio Reyes
q1: It's been disruptive and the reliability has been a real issue. I've had the system go down completely during class at least three times since installation. When you're relying on it for emergency communication, that's concerning. The day-to-day disruptions from inconsistent bells and announcements have been frustrating as well.
q2: Training was adequate but could have been better. We covered the basics, which was helpful, but there was no troubleshooting component. When the system acts up, I have no idea what to do besides call the office.
q3: I was somewhat informed but felt like there were gaps. I heard about the change in broad strokes but didn't get specifics about what my classroom experience would look like during and after the transition. More detailed, classroom-level communication would have been helpful.
q5: Fix the reliability issues. That's number one. A system that goes down regularly is worse than an old system that works consistently. I also think the transition plan should have included more support in the first month after going live.

name: Molly Parker
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. I'm used to a system that just worked seamlessly in the background — you didn't have to think about it. Here, the system requires more attention and workarounds than I'd expect from a new installation. It feels like a step backward from what I had before.
q2: The training itself was well done. The trainers were knowledgeable and the session was organized. I didn't have complaints about the training quality.
q3: Communication could have been better. I got bits and pieces but never felt like I had the complete picture. Coming in as a newer staff member, I didn't have the institutional context to fill in the blanks the way others might.
q4: The training was genuinely good — well-structured and useful.
q5: The system needs to be more reliable and intuitive. I shouldn't have to think about whether the intercom is going to work when I press the button. That's a basic expectation that isn't being met consistently.

name: Isabel Romero
q1: The system itself works fine technically, but the transition has disrupted my workflow more than I expected. At my last school, changes like this were phased in more gradually, and it made a big difference. Here it felt like everything changed overnight and we were just expected to adjust.
q2: Training was good. I felt prepared afterward and knew how to use the core features. No real issues there.
q3: Communication was lacking. I didn't feel well-informed about the timeline or what to expect. I kept finding out about changes after they'd already happened.
q4: The system is reliable — I'll give it that. It works when you need it to, and the audio quality is solid.
q5: The rollout communication and the change management process. The technology is capable, but the way it was introduced made it harder on everyone than it needed to be.

name: Daniel Peterson
q1: It's been a modest improvement overall. There were some disruptions during installation, but day to day things have settled into a reasonable rhythm. I wouldn't say it's dramatically changed my work either way.
q2: Training was pretty good. I felt like I got what I needed to be functional, and the hands-on portion was helpful. I'd say it was above average for district-provided training.
q3: Communication was okay. Not great, not terrible. I knew the change was coming and had a general sense of timing. More specifics about the classroom-level impact would have been useful.
q4: The reliability has been strong. I haven't had any real technical issues, and the audio quality is a clear upgrade from the old system. When it works, it works well.
q5: Nothing major. Maybe a bit more advance communication about exactly when things would be switching over.

name: Irma Ramirez
q1: Aside from the installation period, my daily work hasn't been dramatically affected. I've been able to teach my classes without too much interruption. The workflow has actually been slightly smoother with the new bell schedule integration once they got it right.
q2: Training was passable. I got the gist of things but wouldn't say I feel fully confident with every feature. After twenty-six years of teaching, I've learned to figure things out, but it would be nice not to have to.
q3: They communicated it reasonably well. I knew what was happening. I just would have appreciated more of a voice in the process, maybe a survey beforehand asking what features matter most to us.
q5: The system goes down too much. I've been here long enough to remember when we could go a whole year without the intercom failing. Now it feels like it cuts out every other week. For a brand new system, that's unacceptable. Whatever vendor they picked needs to get the reliability sorted out.

name: Damian Kaur
q1: It's been fine. Some hiccups but nothing crazy.
q2: Training was alright. I got enough to get by.
q3: Could have heard about it sooner. Found out pretty late what was changing.
q4: It works most of the time.
q5: No.

name: Amara Fonseca
q1: It hasn't changed much for me honestly. The system works and I go about my day. A bit more disruption during the changeover but that's expected.
q2: Training was okay but felt like it was geared more toward teachers. For those of us in support roles, a shorter, more targeted session would have been better.
q3: I didn't feel very informed. Most of what I learned was through word of mouth from other staff. Would have been nice to get a direct heads up about what was changing and when.
q4: It's reliable. Works every time I've needed it.
q5: Nothing comes to mind.

name: Allison Foster
q1: It's made things a little more complicated during the transition but the system itself works well. I just wish the changeover hadn't been so abrupt.
q2: Training was good. I felt like I understood what I needed to.
q3: Didn't feel well-informed at all. I basically showed up one day and things were different. Nobody pulled the aides aside to explain what was happening or why.
q4: The system is solid. Clear sound, works when you need it.
q5: Better communication with support staff. We're part of this building too and deserve to be kept in the loop.

name: Andrea Foster
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. I've been here over twenty years and every time they change something it takes a while to get used to it. The new system works but the transition disrupted routines that were well established.
q2: Training wasn't great. It felt like an afterthought for paras. We got the tail end of a session that was really designed for classroom teachers.
q3: Communication was poor in my experience. I found out about changes from teachers I work with, not from admin. That's backwards.
q4: The system is dependable. I'll give it that.
q5: Include support staff in the communication and training plan from the beginning. We shouldn't be an afterthought.

name: Michelle Bennett
q1: It's been kind of disruptive honestly. I'm open to new things but the transition period has thrown off routines I had down pat. The announcements come at different times now and it messes with the flow in the classroom I support.
q2: Training was not great. They showed us the basics in like one session and expected us to figure out the rest. I would have liked a hands-on follow-up after we'd actually been using it for a week.
q3: They told us it was coming, which I appreciated. Could have been more specific about the timeline though — things seemed to shift around a lot.
q4: The sound quality is clearer than the old system when it's working right.
q5: The daily schedule feels off now. Bells and announcements don't always line up the way they used to and it causes confusion with the kids.

name: Destiny Kealoha
q1: It's made things harder. I've been doing this kind of work for over twenty years and I can adapt, but the new system has added confusion to my day that I didn't need.
q2: What training? I'm serious. I got a one-page handout and that was it. After 22 years in education I know what good PD looks like and that wasn't it.
q3: I heard about it through the grapevine before anyone officially told us. The emails came eventually but they were pretty vague.
q5: The whole rollout felt rushed. Nobody asked the paras what we needed or how we use the system differently than teachers do.

name: Margaret Lewis
q1: It hasn't really improved anything for me. The system itself seems reliable enough, but it's changed some of the routines I follow with students and that's been an adjustment.
q2: Training was okay. Not amazing but I got enough to get by. I've picked up the rest on my own over the weeks.
q3: Communication could have been better. I felt like the information came in pieces and I had to put it together myself to get the full picture.
q4: It does seem more dependable than the old one. Haven't had it cut out on me yet.
q5: I'd change the timing of how everything got rolled out. It felt like we were learning on the fly.

name: Brian Lewis
q1: It's fine. Not much different day to day for me.
q2: Barely any training. I've been around long enough to figure things out but it would've been nice to actually get shown how to use the intercom panel in room 114 where I usually am.
q3: I got an email or two. Didn't really explain much.
q4: Nothing.
q5: The training was a joke. That's what I'd change.

name: Christine Miller
q1: Honestly this system is way better than what I was used to before I came here. It's made my day smoother if anything. The announcements are clear and the whole setup just feels more modern.
q2: Could have been more thorough. I figured it out pretty quickly because I'm comfortable with tech, but I can see how others might struggle without more support.
q3: I felt like they kept us in the loop. I got updates before and during the switch which was more than I've experienced at other places.
q4: Everything about it is a step up from what I've seen before. The audio quality, the reliability — I have zero complaints on that front.
q5: Just more training for people who need it. The system itself is solid.

name: Erin Reed
q1: It's been a disruption. I've been here 18 years and every time they change something it takes a while to settle in. This is no different. The daily flow got thrown off during the install and it's still not quite back to normal.
q2: Not enough. I got one quick session that didn't cover half of what I needed to know. I ended up asking the teachers in the rooms I work in how things work.
q3: Honestly I didn't feel well-informed at all. I heard bits and pieces from different people but there was no clear communication plan for support staff. A simple meeting would have gone a long way.
q4: The system itself works. I'll give it that.
q5: Tell people what's happening before you do it, not after. And actually train the paras — we use the system too.

name: Molly Baker
q1: It's been a hassle. More interruptions, more confusion about when things are happening. I don't love change to begin with and this hasn't won me over.
q2: Pretty much nonexistent for paras. The teachers got something but we were kind of left to fend for ourselves.
q3: They did send out information ahead of time, I'll say that. The emails were fine. I just wish someone had walked us through it in person.
q5: The disruption to the daily schedule has been the worst part. Kids get confused, I get confused, it's a mess.

name: Shonda Franklin
q1: It's slowed things down for me. I move between classrooms a lot and the new system works a little differently in each room, which is confusing.
q2: The training was decent. They covered the basics and I felt like I could use the system afterward. Not perfect but adequate.
q3: Communication was pretty good. I knew it was coming and roughly when.
q4: N/A
q5: Make the interface the same in every room. It shouldn't work differently depending on where you are.

name: Margaret Bailey
q1: It hasn't changed much for me since I'm still pretty new here. The system seems to work well — I don't have a lot to compare it to at this school.
q2: Training was alright. I picked up most of it. A refresher session might be helpful down the road.
q3: I felt very well informed. The administration did a good job communicating the change through emails and staff meetings. I knew what to expect.
q4: It's very reliable. I haven't had any issues with it cutting out or malfunctioning.
q5: Not much. It's been fine for me.

name: Erin Lewis
q1: Another new system. I've been through several of these over the years. This one has been a bit disruptive — it changed routines I've had for a long time. The transition period in particular was rough near the 200 building.
q2: Training was okay. I've learned enough to get through the day. At my age I'm not going to pretend I love learning new technology but I managed.
q3: Nobody really told us much. I found out details from other staff more than from administration. After 15 years here I'd expect better communication than that.
q4: It works most of the time. That's about all I can say.
q5: Communicate with your long-term staff. We've been through enough changes to know when we're being left out of the loop.

name: Rafael Rivera
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment but I'm all for upgrading. The day-to-day impact has been minor — some schedule hiccups here and there but nothing I can't handle.
q2: Training was lacking. I've used similar systems at other schools over the years so I was fine, but the formal training here was too short and too basic.
q3: I got the general idea of what was happening but specifics were thin. Would have liked a clearer timeline.
q4: The system itself is solid. Good sound, works when you need it to.
q5: More training. That's the easy answer. People shouldn't have to figure it out on their own.

name: Adriana Aguilar
q1: The new system has been a positive change for the office. It's streamlined how we handle announcements and communications throughout the building. There were some minor bumps during the transition but overall it's been good for our workflow.
q2: The training was excellent. Very thorough and well-organized. I came away feeling confident about using every feature we need in the front office.
q3: Communication was great. We were informed early on and kept in the loop throughout the process. I appreciated the regular updates from administration.
q4: The ease of making building-wide announcements from the office has been a real improvement. The integration with our daily schedule is also a nice touch.
q5: No major complaints. There are occasional glitches but nothing that has caused real problems.

name: Timothy Davis
q1: Day to day it's been pretty seamless honestly. The new system handles most of what the old one did and the workflow in the office hasn't changed dramatically. A few hiccups with reliability but nothing major.
q2: Training was just okay. Covered the basics but I felt like there were features we didn't get into that would be useful for the office. I've had to learn some things through trial and error.
q3: We were kept in the loop, which I appreciated. The timeline was communicated well and I felt prepared for the switch.
q4: The scheduling features work well for what we need in the office.
q5: The system can be a bit glitchy. It's frozen on us a couple of times and we've had to restart. Would like that to be more stable.

name: Doug Evans
q1: It's been a mixed bag. The workflow in the office is about the same, maybe slightly better in some areas. But the reliability issues have been frustrating — we've had the system go down a few times during important moments.
q2: Training was adequate but not outstanding. I learned enough to do my job but wouldn't say I feel like a power user.
q3: We were well informed about the timeline and the reasons for the change. No complaints there. The communication from the district was solid.
q4: When it's working, the interface is cleaner and more intuitive than the old system.
q5: Fix the reliability. We can't have the system dropping during announcements or emergencies. That's a non-negotiable.

name: Jennifer Cooper
q1: It's made things harder for the front office. The system goes down more than it should and when it does we're the ones fielding calls and complaints from teachers. It's added work, not reduced it.
q2: Training was okay. Could have been more detailed on troubleshooting because that's what we end up doing the most — trying to fix things when they aren't working.
q3: Communication about the change itself was actually really good. We knew what was coming and when. I just wish the actual system matched the promises.
q4: The communication from admin about the rollout was good. The system itself? Hard to find positives when it's this unreliable.
q5: Reliability, period. We need a system that works consistently. I shouldn't be rebooting equipment multiple times a week.

name: Mark Rogers
q1: I've been through every system change this district has made in 30 years. This one has potential but it's not there yet. Day to day it's been slightly disruptive — the reliability isn't where it needs to be but I'm optimistic it'll get sorted out.
q2: Training was fine. I've seen worse rollouts. The materials were decent and the session covered what we needed for the office.
q3: Could have been communicated better. I've been here long enough to know when information is being held back or rolled out too slowly. A more straightforward approach would have been better.
q4: I like the direction they're going with it. The concept is sound and when it works it's an improvement over the old system. I'm hopeful.
q5: Get the bugs worked out. I've seen systems like this before and they always have growing pains, but the reliability issues need to be addressed sooner rather than later.

name: Greg Thompson
q1: I didn't really see the need for a new system but it is what it is. Day to day it works fine. Not noticeably better or worse than what we had.
q2: Training was decent. I picked it up quickly enough.
q3: We didn't get a lot of advance notice. Felt like one day it was just happening. An earlier heads-up would have been nice.
q4: It works reliably, I'll give it that. Haven't had issues with it going down or anything like that.
q5: I just don't see why we needed to change. The old system worked fine.

name: Oscar Rivera
q1: It's been kind of a headache. I wasn't excited about the change and it's added some extra steps to my day that I didn't have before. The workflow disruption has been noticeable.
q2: Training was alright. Nothing to write home about but I got what I needed from it.
q3: Communication was average. I knew it was coming but the details were a bit fuzzy until close to the actual switch.
q4: The system works okay. Sound quality is decent.
q5: Simplify the process for making announcements from the front office. It takes more steps now than it used to.

name: Andrea Reed
q1: Overall it's been a positive transition for the office. There was a learning curve but the system is dependable and has actually improved a few things in our daily operations. After 23 years here I've seen much worse rollouts.
q2: I wish the training had been more comprehensive. The session we got covered the surface but didn't go deep enough on the administrative features that the office staff actually use. I ended up learning a lot on my own.
q3: Communication was okay. Not terrible but not great either. For a change this significant I would have expected more direct communication with staff who use the system the most — meaning us in the front office.
q4: The reliability has been excellent. That matters more than anything else to me. When you need to reach the building, you need it to work, and it does.
q5: Better training for office staff specifically. We use the system differently than teachers and the training didn't reflect that.

name: Latoya Robinson
q1: It hasn't been great for workflow. Things are a little clunkier now and I'm not sure the new system is an improvement over what we had for everyday office tasks.
q2: Training was fine. Average. I learned enough to use the system day to day.
q3: We were kept informed, which I appreciated. The emails and updates were helpful.
q4: None
q5: The system needs to be more intuitive. I shouldn't have to think this hard about making a simple announcement.

name: Deborah Thompson
q1: It's been a pretty smooth transition overall. The system works well for what I need it for at the front desk, and I appreciate that I can page specific areas without having to call around. It hasn't really disrupted my routine.
q2: The training was a bit thin honestly. I felt like we got one quick session and then were left to figure things out. I've been in office roles for a while so I picked it up, but a follow-up session or a reference sheet would've been nice.
q3: I think we were kept in the loop pretty well. Our principal shared the timeline early and the emails from the district were clear about what was happening and when.
q4: The paging features are really useful. Being able to reach specific rooms from the front office has made my job easier.
q5: More hands-on training would have been good. I'm still not confident with some of the advanced features.

name: Kristin Howard
q1: Honestly it's made things harder. I'm generally open to new technology, but the workflow in our office has been disrupted pretty significantly. Calls get routed differently now and I'm still figuring out the new process for daily announcements. It takes me longer to do things that used to be second nature.
q2: The training was not adequate. We had one session that was mostly a demo, and then we were on our own. For a system that fundamentally changed how we handle communications from the front office, that wasn't enough. I've had to ask the tech department multiple times for help.
q3: Communication about the change was fine. We knew it was coming and roughly when. I just wish the communication had included more about what would actually change in our daily procedures.
q4: The system itself seems reliable enough. I don't have complaints about it going down or anything like that.
q5: The rollout needed way more training for office staff specifically. We're the ones using this system all day every day and we got the same generic training as everyone else. I would change the entire training approach.

name: Molly Johnson
q1: Day to day it's been okay. There are some quirks with how calls come through that are different from the old system, but nothing I can't work around. I've been here long enough to remember the last system change and this one has gone more smoothly in terms of actual daily operations.
q2: Training was actually good. They did a dedicated session for office staff which I thought was really helpful, and the materials they gave us were useful to reference afterward. I felt prepared.
q3: This is where I have issues. I found out about the installation timeline from a colleague, not from administration. For something that directly impacts the front office this much, we should have been consulted or at least notified much earlier. I've been here fourteen years and it was frustrating to feel like an afterthought.
q4: It works. The call quality is decent and the interface for managing announcements is more intuitive than the old one.
q5: Communication from leadership about the change was poor. We deserved a seat at the table or at minimum a heads-up before it was a done deal.

name: Gabriela Garcia
q1: It's been a mixed bag. Some things work fine, but there are days where the system acts up and I have to troubleshoot instead of doing my actual job. It hasn't been a huge improvement or a huge problem, just kind of different.
q2: Training could have been better. It felt rushed and didn't really cover the scenarios we deal with at the front desk. I still have questions about some features that I just haven't had time to figure out.
q3: They communicated it well enough. We got updates leading up to the install and knew what to expect timeline-wise.
q5: The reliability needs work. There are times it just doesn't connect right or there's a delay. I'm not someone who loves switching to new things to begin with, so having it be glitchy doesn't help.

name: Sandy Singh
q1: It's been alright. I've used similar systems before so the transition wasn't too jarring. There are some reliability hiccups that I wasn't expecting, but the general functionality is what I'd expect from this type of system. Workflow-wise things are mostly fine.
q2: Training was okay. Not bad, not great. I picked things up pretty quickly since I'd used something comparable before, but I could see how it might be insufficient for someone starting from scratch.
q3: I felt well-informed. The district did a good job of laying out the plan and the reasoning. I appreciated the transparency about the timeline.
q4: I'm enthusiastic about the potential. The platform itself has good features and I think once the kinks are worked out it'll be a solid system.
q5: The connection drops are concerning. A communications system needs to be rock solid and right now it's not quite there.

name: Keith Howard
q1: Hasn't changed much for me. I do my rounds same as always. The new panel in the custodial office works fine for what I need.
q2: Training was so-so. They showed us the basics but I had to figure out the zone paging on my own. Been here 31 years, I've seen plenty of these come and go.
q3: Felt like we were the last to hear about it. Found out when the installers showed up in B building.
q4: It doesn't get in my way, I'll give it that.
q5: The speakers in the C wing stairwell cut out sometimes. Wiring issue maybe.

name: Irma Garcia
q1: It's been good. Way better than what I had at my last job. The system actually works and I can hear announcements clearly wherever I am in the building.
q2: Could've used more training. They went through it pretty fast and I was still new here so I was learning everything at once.
q3: They let us know ahead of time which was good. I felt like I knew what was happening.
q4: The coverage is great. I've worked places where you couldn't hear anything past the main hallway. This is way better than that.
q5: Just more training. That's it.

name: Matthew White
q1: Works fine for me. I can hear what I need to hear and the radios still work the same. No big complaints about the day-to-day.
q2: It was adequate. Nothing special but I got the gist. Would've liked a cheat sheet for the wall panel controls.
q3: I heard about it but not with a lot of lead time. Seemed like it was decided before anyone in facilities was asked. I've been here 12 years and I remember when they put in the last system — same deal, we find out after the fact.
q4: N/A
q5: Tell us sooner next time. We're the ones who have to work around the installers and rearrange our schedules.

name: Greg Bailey
q1: It's another system. I've been through three of these changeovers now. It works, mostly. Doesn't really change what I do day to day.
q2: Fine. I know how to use what I need to use.
q3: They told us. Could've been earlier but they told us.
q4: No.
q5: The volume on some of the outdoor speakers near the portables is too low. Hard to hear when the HVAC units are running.

name: Dennis Murphy
q1: It's been fine. System works well, no real issues there. My actual work hasn't changed much because of it.
q2: Training was light. Quick overview and that was it. I figured it out but some of the newer folks on the crew had more questions.
q3: Good communication from what I saw. They sent out info ahead of time and the timeline was pretty accurate.
q4: Rock solid system honestly. Haven't had a single outage or problem with the equipment itself. The speakers and panels are quality hardware.

name: Ashton Santiago
q1: Another change nobody asked for. It doesn't make my job better or worse, it's just different buttons to push. I don't see why we needed to spend money on this.
q2: Training was whatever. Short. Didn't cover much that was useful for my role.
q3: We were told it was happening. Not really asked for input.
q4: Nothing.
q5: I'd change the decision-making process. Ask the people who actually work in the building what they need before buying something new.

name: Rio Aguilar
q1: It's been disruptive. During installation they had areas blocked off that I needed to clean and it threw off my whole schedule. Now the system is in but honestly my work has gotten harder because the new bell schedule and announcement timing is different and it messes with when I can get into certain areas.
q2: Barely any training. They showed us a video and that was it. I've been doing this 20 years here, I know how to use a panel, but it would've been nice to be treated like we matter enough to get real training.
q3: Nobody told us anything. I found out from a teacher that the system was being replaced. Twenty years I've been here and I'm hearing about major building changes secondhand. That's not right.
q5: Everything about how this was communicated to facilities staff. We should have been the first ones informed, not the last. And the installation crew left cable runs exposed in two utility closets that I've reported three times now.

name: David Bennett
q1: Hasn't done much for me either way. My job is my job regardless of what intercom system is on the wall. I will say the workflow got a little disrupted during install but it's settled now.
q2: Training was basic. Got the job done I guess.
q3: Heard about it through the grapevine mostly. Official communication came late. After 31 years here I'm used to that.
q4: The equipment is solid. No failures, no glitches. That's more than I can say for the last system — that thing in the gym used to cut out every other week.
q5: None

name: Nicole Hall
q1: I'm new here so I don't have anything to compare it to at this school. It works well enough. Hasn't caused me any problems.
q2: Training was short. I've worked with systems like this before so it wasn't hard to pick up, but I think more time would've been helpful.
q3: I'd just started so I wasn't really part of the lead-up communication. What I did hear seemed okay.
q4: Equipment seems really reliable. Good quality speakers, clear sound.
q5: More training, especially for people who are new to the building.

name: Pedro Chavez
q1: It's been a little disruptive to be honest. The system works great, no problems with the equipment, but some of the scheduling changes that came along with it have messed with my routine. I've been doing this 43 years and I have a way of doing things.
q2: Training was decent. They covered the main points and I didn't have trouble with it.
q3: Communication was okay. Not the best, not the worst. I would have liked more notice about the installation schedule specifically.
q4: Sound quality is good. The speakers in the maintenance areas actually work now which is nice.
q5: The timing of the whole project could've been better. Doing installs during the school year when we're trying to keep the building running is tough.

name: Erin Murphy
q1: It's made things a little easier actually. I can hear pages clearly and the new system is straightforward to use. Hasn't disrupted my routine at all.
q2: Training was pretty minimal. I'm not new to this kind of work so I figured it out, but they could've spent more time on it.
q3: I heard about it from my supervisor. Seemed like the information was there if you looked for it.
q4: Reliable. Clear audio. Does what it's supposed to do.
q5: N/A

name: Leticia Vasquez
q1: It's been a step down from what I was used to before I came here, honestly. The system works okay but it's disrupted how I plan my shifts. Where I worked before, everything was more integrated and you didn't have to think about it.
q2: Training was alright. Nothing great. I've used better systems that were more intuitive out of the box so less training was needed.
q3: Communication was fine. I'm still fairly new here so I appreciated being included in the updates.
q4: The equipment itself is reliable. I'll give it that.
q5: The whole system feels clunkier than what I'm used to. I'd look at streamlining the interface. It shouldn't take this many steps to do basic functions.

name: Jennifer Johnson
q1: It's been okay. Not a huge change for me either way. Some things are a little different but nothing major.
q2: Training was actually really good. They did a hands-on session where we could practice using the panels and ask questions. Best training I've had for any system change here in 12 years.
q3: We were informed reasonably well. Could've been a little earlier but I knew what was coming.
q4: The training was the highlight. And the system seems well-built.
q5: Some of the speaker placements could be better. The one outside the loading dock is too close to the compressor and you can't hear it when the kitchen equipment is running.

name: Christopher Wilson
q1: It's been a bit of an adjustment. I had my routine down and now some things take longer because I'm still getting used to the new setup. Not terrible, just different.
q2: Training was lacking. Quick runthrough and done. After 36 years in this line of work I can usually figure things out, but the younger guys on our crew needed more.
q3: They communicated it okay. I heard about it from the principal's email.
q4: Works when you need it to.
q5: Better training. And consult facilities staff before deciding where to mount equipment. They put a panel right where we used to store the floor buffer.

name: Teresa Rodriguez
q1: It really messed up our lunch schedule. During the installation the bell times kept changing and we'd have kids showing up before we were ready, or we'd have food sitting there getting cold because the period got pushed back. The system itself is fine but the disruption to our timing has been a real problem. We're still dealing with inconsistent bell schedules some days.
q2: We got a short training. It wasn't really relevant to what we do in the kitchen honestly. We just need to hear the bells and announcements.
q3: We were told about the new system but nobody warned us that the lunch schedule was going to be all over the place during the transition. That's the information we actually needed.
q4: The system works. No issues with hearing announcements in the kitchen.
q5: The scheduling chaos. We had about three weeks where lunch periods were unpredictable and nobody in the cafeteria knew when to expect the rush. That should have been planned better. We prep based on timing and when that changes without warning it's a big deal.

name: Vivian Wong
q1: The bell schedule kept changing during the install and it threw off our whole lunch service. We had kids showing up early, showing up late, nobody knew what was going on. It's settled down now but those first few weeks were rough.
q2: Training was okay but short. They showed us how to use the intercom panel but it was fast and I had to ask someone to show me again later.
q3: I didn't really hear much about this until they were already pulling wires through the ceiling in the kitchen. Would have been nice to get a heads up about the schedule changes ahead of time.
q4: The sound quality is good, I can actually hear the announcements from back in the kitchen now.
q5: The lunch schedule disruptions. We've been running the same schedule here for years and suddenly everything was shifted around with no warning.

name: Guillermo Gutierrez
q1: It messed with the bell schedule for a while so we were scrambling to keep up. I'm open to it though, change happens. Just wish it didn't hit us during the busiest part of the year.
q2: Barely got any training honestly. Somebody showed me a button on the wall for like two minutes. I figured the rest out on my own.
q3: They let us know it was coming, that part was fine.
q4: System works great, no complaints on that end. Clear sound every time.
q5: The training. Just give us a real walkthrough next time.

name: Rocio Ortiz
q1: The schedule kept getting moved around during installation and it really disrupted how we prep and serve. I've been doing this a long time and I can adjust, but it was a lot of back and forth.
q2: Could have been better. I felt like I was just figuring things out as I went.
q3: It was so-so. I heard about it but the details on how it would affect our area came late.
q5: All the lunch period shuffling. That was the hardest part for us.

name: Tammy Lewis
q1: Lunch periods got moved around three different times during the installation. We had to redo our prep schedule each time. After 26 years here I know how the kitchen runs and this threw everything off.
q2: Nobody trained us on anything. I still don't really know how half of it works.
q3: Communication was fine, I knew it was coming. Just didn't know how much it would affect us.
q4: Not much from where I stand.
q5: The scheduling chaos. And some kind of actual training would have been nice.

name: Leticia Romero
q1: There were some schedule changes that were inconvenient but honestly this system is way better than what I've worked with before. At my last job we barely had a working intercom.
q2: Training was pretty good. I felt like I got what I needed.
q3: They communicated it okay. I knew what was happening and roughly when.
q4: The whole system is a big step up from what I'm used to. Sound is clear, it actually works consistently.
q5: Just the timing stuff with lunch periods during the switch. That's about it.

name: Amara Kaur
q1: It was a disaster for us in the kitchen. Bell schedules changed constantly, kids coming in at random times, we couldn't keep up. I've worked food service for 40 years and I've never had this much confusion over a schedule.
q2: The training was minimal. Not really geared toward us.
q3: They told us about the new system but not about how much the schedule would bounce around. That's what we needed to know.
q4: The system itself seems fine I guess.
q5: Everything about how they handled the lunch timing. That needed to be planned out way better.

name: Cristina Rodriguez
q1: The schedule changes during installation were hard on us. I don't love change to begin with and having lunch periods moved around made everything harder. That said, at my old district we didn't even have a system this reliable.
q2: Training was good, no complaints there.
q3: They kept us informed, I'll give them that. Emails went out and our supervisor passed along updates.
q4: It works well. I've seen much worse so I appreciate having something dependable.
q5: The disruption to our daily routine. I wish they could have done the install without messing with the bell schedule so much.

name: Danielle Ward
q1: It's been disruptive. The lunch schedule kept shifting and we're already understaffed so that made things worse. I don't really see why we needed a new system in the first place.
q2: Training was adequate for what we need to do with it.
q3: Communication was fine.
q4: N/A
q5: The scheduling mess during installation. We're still catching up.

name: Sandy Huang
q1: Bell schedule changes affected our lunch service quite a bit. It's mostly back to normal now but for a while it was stressful trying to have food ready when kids could show up at different times than expected.
q2: It was okay. Basic. I know how to use the intercom if I need to.
q3: They communicated it well ahead of time, which I appreciated. We knew the timeline.
q4: Sound is clear and I can hear pages from the kitchen area.
q5: The lunch period disruptions. That part could have been handled better.

name: Vivian Park
q1: Not great. The schedule changes during the install were really hard on our team. I don't think anybody thought about how shifting bells around would affect food service. I wasn't really asking for a new system.
q2: Training was just okay. Felt rushed.
q3: We heard about it but the specifics about timing were vague.
q5: They need to think about how construction and installation timelines affect everybody, not just classrooms.

name: Vivian Liu
q1: It hasn't really changed my work much now that things are settled. During the install it was a different story with the schedule changes but we got through it.
q2: Training was decent. I've been through enough of these things over the years to pick it up.
q3: I wish they'd told us more about the timeline. I kind of heard about it secondhand.
q4: It works fine.

name: Kevin Reed
q1: Honestly I don't see what was wrong with the old system. The schedule disruptions during the switchover were not worth it in my opinion.
q2: Training was short and not very helpful. I've been doing this long enough to know that when they rush the training it means they didn't plan it well.
q3: They told us it was happening. That's about it.
q4: System works I guess. It does what it's supposed to do.
q5: Should have left the old one alone.

name: Casey Rogers
q1: Terrible. The bell schedule was all over the place for weeks and we had to just deal with it. After 35 years here I've seen a lot of changes and this was one of the worst handled ones. The new system cuts out sometimes too.
q2: The training was a joke. One quick session and that was it.
q3: They mentioned it was coming but I didn't feel like anyone asked for our input. Would've been nice to be part of the conversation.
q4: None.
q5: The system isn't even reliable half the time. Announcements cut out or sound garbled. And the whole installation process was a mess for the kitchen.

name: Lisa Parker
q1: The schedule changes during installation were a pain. But the system itself works great now so I can deal with it.
q2: Training was good enough.
q3: They kept us in the loop pretty well. I felt informed about what was happening and when.
q4: It's very reliable. Haven't had a single issue with the system itself.
q5: Just the lunch period disruptions during the install. Everything else is fine.

name: Connie Park
q1: The emergency alert features are genuinely excellent and give me real peace of mind, particularly when I think about lockdown scenarios. However, the intercom interruptions during counseling sessions have been very disruptive. I'm often in the middle of sensitive conversations with students and the overhead pages break the trust and focus we've built. I've had students completely shut down after an interruption.
q2: The training covered the basics but I would have liked more time to understand the system's capabilities, especially the emergency features. It felt like a one-size-fits-all approach that didn't address how different roles interact with the system.
q3: I was not well informed at all. After 25 years in this district I would have expected to at least be consulted about how this would affect counseling services. A brief survey or even a conversation with our department would have gone a long way.
q4: The emergency and safety alert system is outstanding. It's fast, clear, and reliable. That alone makes the system worthwhile from a student safety perspective.
q5: There needs to be a way to suppress non-emergency intercom pages in counseling offices. The constant interruptions undermine confidential work with students. Even a do-not-disturb mode would help.

name: Diana Liu
q1: It's been a mixed experience. I value having a reliable alert system for safety purposes but the routine announcements coming through during student sessions is a real problem. I've had to pause crisis interventions because of a page about a schedule change. That shouldn't happen.
q2: Training was reasonable. I felt prepared to use the system for what I need day to day.
q3: Communication was good overall. We got updates and timelines. I felt like I knew what to expect.
q4: The training component was solid and the system is easy to use. I also appreciate the safety features.
q5: The intercom goes off at the worst possible times during sessions. There's no way to mute routine pages in my office without also losing emergency alerts. We need that distinction built in.

name: Graciela Rivera
q1: It's been somewhat disruptive. The pages come through at unpredictable times and when I'm working with a student on something personal, that interruption really matters. The system itself seems to have some glitches too.
q2: I didn't get much training. I think there was one session but I missed it and nobody followed up with me.
q3: I heard about it in general terms but nothing specific about the timeline or what to expect during the transition.
q4: I can see the value of the emergency features even if I haven't needed them yet.
q5: The reliability is inconsistent and the intercom disrupts confidential sessions. I'd love a way to set my office to only receive emergency alerts.

name: Diana Garcia
q1: It's had a noticeable impact. On one hand the safety features are reassuring. On the other hand I now have to plan around potential intercom interruptions, which adds stress to an already demanding role. I've started scheduling sensitive conversations around times when announcements are less likely, which is not ideal.
q2: The training was insufficient. One session that barely covered the basics and didn't address counselor-specific concerns at all. I still don't fully understand the alert tiers.
q3: We were informed well enough about the change itself. I knew it was coming and I knew the general timeline.
q5: We need the ability to silence non-emergency pages. This is a counseling office, not a classroom. The system should account for different use cases.

name: Hector Gonzalez
q1: I appreciate the safety improvements a lot. The system is clearly more reliable than what we had before. But the trade-off is that intercom traffic has increased and that's tough when you're doing confidential work with students. I've been here almost 20 years and this is the first time I've had to worry about announcements interrupting a session.
q2: Training was okay. Covered what we needed to know but I would have liked a deeper dive into the emergency protocols and how they differ from routine pages.
q3: Communication was solid. Our admin kept us updated and the timeline was mostly accurate.
q4: The reliability is a huge step forward. I trust that if there's an emergency the system will work. That matters.
q5: Just the intercom interruption issue. If they could add a way to filter routine pages in counseling offices that would solve my biggest complaint.

name: Jennifer Baker
q1: Overall it's been a positive change for me. The system is more dependable and I feel good about the safety improvements. The intercom pages during sessions are annoying but I've adjusted by putting a sign on my door and working with the front office on timing.
q2: I wish the training had been more thorough. It was pretty quick and surface level. I had to figure out some things on my own.
q3: I felt a little out of the loop on the specifics. I knew something was changing but the details came late and I would have liked more advance notice.
q4: The system is reliable and the emergency features are excellent. I also like that the audio quality is better than the old system.
q5: More training and a way to manage intercom volume or mute routine pages during sessions.

name: Nicole Taylor
q1: It's been a mixed bag honestly. The emergency alert features give me peace of mind, which I genuinely appreciate, but the intercom announcements cut into sessions with students at the worst possible moments. I'm in the middle of something sensitive and suddenly there's a broadcast overhead. It disrupts the flow and sometimes undoes the trust I've been building in that conversation.
q2: Training was okay. It covered the basics but didn't really address how to manage the intercom settings for spaces like counseling offices where privacy is critical. I would have liked more guidance specific to our role.
q3: I didn't feel especially well informed. I heard bits and pieces from colleagues before anything official came to us. An email explaining the timeline and what to expect for counseling staff specifically would have gone a long way.
q4: The lockdown alert system is clear and immediate. That part is genuinely well done.
q5: There needs to be a way to mute or delay non-emergency announcements in counseling offices. We can't have routine broadcasts interrupting a student in crisis.

name: Nora Vargas
q1: Day to day, I've adjusted. The system works and I don't have major complaints about the technology itself. My workflow has actually smoothed out in some respects since we can receive building-wide updates more efficiently. But I'll always be the person who would rather not have another new system to learn if the old one was working fine.
q2: The training was thorough. I appreciated that they offered multiple sessions at different times so I could attend one that fit my schedule. The materials were clear and well organized.
q3: Communication was decent. I knew it was coming, I knew roughly when, and I had a contact person to reach out to with questions. That's about all you can ask for with something like this.
q4: The integration with the emergency notification protocols is solid. And the overall audio quality is much better than the old system, which always sounded like it was underwater.
q5: N/A

name: Alex Patel
q1: I'm still relatively new here so I'm adjusting to a lot at once. The system functions fine technically, but the way announcements interrupt my sessions is really problematic. At my previous district the intercom system had a setting where non-emergency pages could be silenced in designated offices. This feels like a step backward from what I had before in terms of managing disruptions.
q2: The training was adequate but pretty surface-level. I picked up on the basics quickly enough, though I felt like some of the more nuanced features weren't covered well.
q3: I wasn't told much beforehand. Being new, I wasn't sure if I just missed the communications or if there weren't many to begin with. Either way I felt a little in the dark about what was happening and when.
q4: The system itself is reliable. I haven't experienced outages or technical failures, which is good.
q5: The lack of a do-not-disturb function for counseling offices is a real oversight. I'm used to having that kind of control and it's frustrating not to have it here.

name: Sergio Estrada
q1: The new system has created more interruptions during my sessions than the old one did. I work with students on sensitive issues — IEP meetings, crisis interventions, college planning conversations that require focus — and the intercom going off breaks that concentration. On the safety side, yes, I can see the value. But the daily disruptions are wearing on me.
q2: I felt like the training was rushed. One session, a lot of information, not much follow-up. For people who've been here as long as I have, it was manageable, but I wouldn't call it adequate. I had to figure out a few things on my own afterward.
q3: We were informed reasonably well. I remember getting emails and there was a staff meeting that covered the timeline. I don't have major complaints about the communication process itself.
q4: The emergency notification capability is a significant improvement. When we had a lockdown drill last month it was crystal clear and the zones functioned as intended.
q5: I'd change the ability to control intercom volume or set quiet hours in counseling spaces. We should be able to receive emergency alerts without getting every attendance call and schedule announcement.

name: Kiara Tehrani
q1: It hasn't been great. The system itself is a bit unreliable — I've had times where the intercom just didn't work or the audio cut out mid-announcement. And then other times it works perfectly fine and interrupts me when I'm sitting with a student who's telling me something really difficult. It's inconsistent and disruptive at the same time.
q2: Training was fine. Not exceptional, not terrible. I learned what I needed to learn.
q3: I heard about it ahead of time through the usual channels. I think a bit more detail about what would actually change for us on a daily basis would have helped set expectations. The big-picture stuff was communicated but the day-to-day implications weren't.
q4: When it works, the sound quality is better than the old system. And the safety alert features are important.
q5: Fix the reliability issues. I shouldn't have to wonder whether an announcement actually went through or not. And give counselors the option to manage non-emergency interruptions.

name: Timothy Evans
q1: Look, I'm someone who embraces new technology. I think this system is reliable and the safety features are excellent. That said, the impact on my actual daily work as a counselor has been negative because of the constant intercom interruptions during sessions. I've been here twenty years and I've adapted to a lot of changes, but this one requires some adjustment on the implementation side, not just from staff.
q2: Training was decent. I would have liked a second session to go deeper on some features, but the initial walkthrough covered the essentials.
q3: This is where I have a real concern. I found out about the installation timeline mostly through hallway conversations. For something this significant, there should have been a dedicated communication plan — not just a mention at a staff meeting. I expected more from the district on this.
q4: The system is rock solid from a technical standpoint. Emergency alerts are clear, immediate, and well-zoned. The hardware quality is noticeably better than what we had before.
q5: Implement a priority-level system for announcements so counseling offices only receive emergency-level broadcasts during session hours. Routine announcements can wait.

name: Marisol Gonzalez
q1: Compared to what I've worked with before, this system is honestly much better from a technology standpoint. The audio quality and the emergency features are far beyond what I'm used to. But the disruptions to my counseling sessions have been significant. Announcements come through at all hours and there's no way to filter what's urgent from what's routine. That part has been really challenging for my work.
q2: The training was fine. I've dealt with much worse onboarding for new systems at previous places, so I can't complain too much. It covered the basics and I was able to get up and running.
q3: I felt well informed. There were emails, a timeline was shared, and my department head kept us updated. It was a reasonably transparent process.
q4: The emergency alert system. Hands down. Having a reliable, clear safety notification system is something I never take for granted after seeing what inadequate ones look like.
q5: The intercom needs a do-not-disturb option for counseling offices. Non-emergency announcements disrupt confidential sessions and it's been a real problem for me and my students.

name: Daniel Turner
q1: It's been disruptive. I've been in this building a long time and I understand the need for system upgrades, but the intercom interruptions during student sessions are a real problem. I had a student shut down completely last week because an announcement startled them during a difficult conversation. The system works fine technically, but it doesn't account for the work we do in counseling offices.
q2: Training was adequate. Nothing I couldn't handle, though I think a refresher session a few weeks in would have been useful once we'd actually had time to use the system and develop real questions.
q3: Communication could have been better. I felt like I was finding things out after the fact — like installation dates for our wing or when features were going to change. More proactive updates would have helped.
q4: The safety and emergency features are well designed. I trust that if there's an emergency, the system will do its job.
q5: Give us control over non-emergency announcements in counseling spaces. This should have been considered from the start given what we do.

name: Emily Anderson
q1: The new system has made my daily work harder. I understand the safety rationale and the system is reliable, but the constant announcements through the intercom are incredibly disruptive during student sessions. I'm not someone who gets excited about new tech changes to begin with, and this hasn't done anything to change my mind. The disruption to my counseling work outweighs the benefits I've seen so far.
q2: The training was not sufficient for my needs. It was one session that felt like it was designed for classroom teachers, not counselors. I had specific questions about muting capabilities and scheduling quiet periods that nobody could answer.
q3: Communication was middling. I knew it was coming, but the specifics about how it would affect our office spaces were vague. I would have appreciated a conversation with our department about what this would actually look like day to day.
q4: The emergency alert feature is genuinely valuable. Reliable and clear.
q5: The entire approach to non-emergency announcements needs to be rethought for counseling spaces. We need quiet mode capability. This should be a priority.

name: Shonda Owens
q1: It's been a challenge. The system is reliable and I appreciate that, but the way it interrupts my work with students is problematic. I don't love change for change's sake, and while I can see this was an upgrade in some ways, the impact on my daily counseling sessions has been negative.
q2: Training was good enough. I felt prepared to use the basic functions. Nothing exceptional but nothing missing either.
q3: We were kept in the loop for the most part. I appreciated the advance notice and the timeline that was shared with staff. If I had to improve anything, maybe more role-specific information for counselors.
q4: Safety features are strong. The system is dependable and the alert quality is clear.

name: Claudia Castillo
q1: I've been through many system changes in my thirty years here, and this one has been one of the more disruptive ones for my actual work. The technology is fine — reliable, good audio — but the intercom broadcasts during counseling sessions are a real problem. I've had to pause sessions with students in Room 214 multiple times in a single period because of non-emergency announcements. That's not acceptable.
q2: Training was okay. At this point in my career I can figure most things out, but I would have appreciated more attention to the counseling-specific implications. The training felt very one-size-fits-all.
q3: I was well informed. Administration communicated the plan clearly and I felt like I knew what was happening and when. No complaints there.
q4: The emergency notification system is a genuine improvement over what we had. After the incident two years ago, having a system I trust for safety alerts matters to me deeply.
q5: We need to be able to silence routine announcements during session hours. I've raised this with administration and I hope it's being addressed.

name: Ariana St. Claire
q1: The system itself works well and I'm open to the upgrade, but the workflow impact has been frustrating. Intercom interruptions during sensitive student meetings are disruptive and undermine the work I'm trying to do. I find myself rushing through conversations because I know an announcement could come at any moment.
q2: Training was solid. I felt well-prepared and the trainers were knowledgeable. The hands-on portion was especially helpful.
q3: Communication was lacking. I didn't feel like counseling staff were consulted or even considered in the planning. We found out details at the same time as everyone else, when our needs are quite different from a typical classroom.
q4: The reliability is impressive and the emergency features are exactly what a school needs. The system does what it's supposed to do from a safety perspective.
q5: Add the ability to set counseling offices to emergency-only mode during certain hours. That single change would resolve most of my concerns.

name: Greg Carter
q1: I appreciate the upgrade and I think the system is a step forward for the school in terms of safety infrastructure. That said, it has significantly disrupted my counseling sessions. The intercom goes off for routine things — schedule changes, attendance — and each time it breaks the momentum of whatever conversation I'm having with a student. It's been one of the more frustrating aspects of my daily work lately.
q2: The training was good. Well-organized, clear materials, and the presenter knew the system inside and out. I felt confident using it afterward.
q3: Communication was mostly good. I knew what was happening and the timeline was shared in advance. I can't think of major gaps.
q4: The emergency alert system is excellent. Reliable, clear audio, well-zoned. It gives me confidence that we're prepared for the worst.
q5: Non-emergency announcements should not broadcast into counseling offices during session hours. Period. This needs to be configurable.

name: Diana Shah
q1: My day-to-day work has gotten harder. The system is functional and I understand the reasoning behind the upgrade, but the intercom interruptions in my office are constant and they undermine the confidential, focused environment I need for student sessions. I'm not opposed to new systems, but this one wasn't designed with counselors in mind.
q2: Training was good. Comprehensive and well-paced. I felt like I had what I needed.
q3: I knew about the change ahead of time but the details about implementation in the counseling wing were vague. More specific information about how our spaces would be affected would have been helpful.
q4: Reliable emergency notifications. That's a meaningful improvement.
q5: Allow counseling staff to control non-emergency intercom settings in their offices. We should have been part of the planning conversation for this.

name: Amy Tran
q1: The system is extremely reliable and I've been impressed with the technology. It's a clear upgrade from what we had. However, even with all its strengths, the interruptions during counseling sessions have affected my workflow. I manage, but it's an ongoing friction point.
q2: The training was excellent. Honestly one of the better professional development experiences I've had. Clear, practical, and they offered follow-up support afterward.
q3: I would have appreciated more communication specifically about the counseling department implications. The general information was fine, but we have unique needs that weren't addressed in the broader messaging.
q4: Almost everything from a technical standpoint. The audio quality, the reliability, the emergency system — all top notch. The hardware itself is a major improvement.
q5: The only thing I'd change is adding a quiet mode for counseling offices. Everything else works very well.

name: Sheryl Robinson
q1: The system works well and I value the safety improvements. The impact on my daily counseling work is the issue — announcements come through during sessions and it breaks the flow. I've adapted by trying to schedule sensitive conversations during quieter periods, but that's a workaround, not a solution.
q2: Training was good. I felt prepared and the materials were helpful to reference later. I appreciated the hands-on approach.
q3: Communication was adequate. I was informed about the timeline and the rationale for the change. No major complaints.
q4: The emergency alert capabilities are strong. The system is reliable and the audio is clear. I feel safer knowing it's in place.

name: Renee Saunders
q1: It's been tough. I'm not naturally a fan of big system changes and this one has genuinely made my counseling work harder. The intercom interruptions are my primary complaint. I can be mid-session with a student who's finally opening up and then — announcement. It's jarring for them and frustrating for me.
q2: The training was fine. Sufficient for what I needed to know.
q3: I felt well-informed. The communication was timely and clear. I knew what was coming and when.
q4: The safety features. Having a dependable emergency notification system is important and this one delivers.
q5: Counseling offices need to be treated differently than classrooms for intercom purposes. We need the ability to receive only emergency-level alerts during sessions.

name: David Reed
q1: Honestly, it's made things worse for me. I've been here twenty years and the old system, while not perfect, didn't interrupt my work the way this one does. Every routine announcement comes blasting into my office during student sessions. The system is reliable, sure, but reliability doesn't help when the reliable thing it does is disrupt my work with students who need confidentiality and focus.
q2: Training was fine. Standard. Nothing I'd complain about but nothing I'd praise either.
q3: Communication was poor. I felt like decisions were made and we were told after the fact. For a change this significant, counseling staff should have been consulted about our specific needs during the planning phase, not informed once everything was already decided.
q4: The emergency features are solid. That's the one area where I see clear improvement.
q5: Overhaul how non-emergency announcements are handled in counseling spaces. This should have been addressed before installation, not after. I also think the district needs to establish a protocol for quiet hours in sensitive spaces.

name: Patricia Owens
q1: The system has been unreliable in ways that actually affect my work. There have been several instances where announcements cut out or came through garbled, which is concerning when you think about emergency situations. On top of that, the routine announcements that do work fine are constantly interrupting my counseling sessions. So I'm dealing with a system that's inconsistent when I need it and intrusive when I don't.
q2: The training was excellent — probably the best part of this whole rollout. It was comprehensive, well-structured, and the trainers were responsive to questions. I have no complaints about the training itself.
q3: Communication was good. I was informed early and updates were consistent throughout the process. I felt like administration handled this part well.
q4: Nothing
