q5: The reliability problems need to be fixed immediately. An emergency system that doesn't work consistently is worse than no system at all because it creates false confidence. And the interruption issue during counseling sessions needs a real solution.

name: Shannon Hall
q1: The system is very reliable, which I appreciate — I've never had a technical issue with it. I'm generally positive about the upgrade. But the workflow impact on counseling has been noticeable. Intercom announcements during student sessions disrupt confidential conversations and I haven't found a great workaround yet.
q2: Training could have been better. It felt like a quick overview rather than a deep dive. I would have benefited from a second session once we'd been using the system for a few weeks and could ask informed questions.
q3: I was reasonably informed. The timeline was communicated and I knew what to expect. I might have appreciated a bit more specificity about what was changing for counseling offices in particular.
q4: The reliability is outstanding. The emergency features are clear and well-implemented. I trust this system completely from a safety standpoint.
q5: Create a way for counseling offices to opt into emergency-only mode during session hours. That would solve the biggest problem with the current setup.

name: Tina Cho
q1: Honestly, the day-to-day impact has been manageable, but the reliability issues have created some headaches for our front office. We have had multiple instances where the system just dropped out during morning announcements, which is not acceptable when you are trying to run a building. I would say the workflow itself has not fundamentally changed, but the unpredictability of the equipment has added a layer of stress.
q2: The training was insufficient for what was being asked of us. We received one session before go-live, and it did not cover troubleshooting at all. When things started failing, my staff and I were left scrambling to figure it out on our own.
q3: Communication was adequate in terms of the timeline, but the details about what to expect during the transition were lacking. It would have been helpful to have a more realistic picture of the growing pains rather than just a sales pitch.
q4: The system interface is more modern, and in theory the features are an upgrade from what we had before.
q5: The reliability has to improve. We cannot have a communications system that cuts out mid-announcement. I would also like to see more robust training offered on an ongoing basis, not just a one-time session before rollout.

name: Jessica Reed
q1: It has been a bit of an adjustment. The system works well enough most of the time, but there have been moments where it has slowed us down while we figure out the new process.
q2: The training covered the basics, but I felt like there were gaps. I left the session with questions I did not feel comfortable asking in a large group, and there was no follow-up opportunity to address them.
q3: I felt reasonably well-informed about the change. The emails from central office were helpful, and our principal kept us updated. A brief FAQ document would have been a nice addition.
q4: The paging feature is cleaner and more intuitive than the old system.
q5: I think the training could have been more tailored to administrators specifically. We use the system differently than teachers do, and the one-size-fits-all approach did not serve us well.

name: Michelle Collins
q1: The transition has been somewhat disruptive. There was a period where we were toggling between old habits and new procedures, and that created confusion among staff. I am open to the change, but the workflow disruption was real.
q2: I actually thought the training was well done. The presenters were knowledgeable and the hands-on portion was useful. I felt prepared to use the core features.
q3: This is where I have concerns. The decision seemed to come out of nowhere for those of us at the site level. I heard about it through the grapevine before any official communication reached us, which did not instill confidence. A heads-up earlier in the process and some input from site leaders would have gone a long way.
q4: The training team did a good job, and the system itself has some nice features when it is working properly.
q5: The rollout communication to sites needs to be much better. We should not be learning about major infrastructure changes secondhand.

name: Molly Carter
q1: Day to day, things are mostly the same. There are some hiccups here and there with the system not responding as expected, but nothing that has derailed operations. I would say it is a lateral move so far in terms of daily workflow.
q2: Training was fine. It was not the most comprehensive I have seen, but it gave us enough to get started. I appreciate that they offered a makeup session for those who could not attend the first one.
q3: I felt very well-informed throughout the process. The superintendent's updates were clear, the board presentations were transparent, and we had ample notice before the installation began. This was handled well on the communication front.
q4: The notification features are a step up, and the overall look and feel of the interface is more modern.
q5: Some intermittent connectivity issues that I hope are just growing pains. I would like to see those resolved within the next few weeks.

name: Jeffrey Harris
q1: It has made things harder, frankly. The learning curve combined with some reliability concerns has meant more time troubleshooting and less time on instructional leadership. I know there is an adjustment period with any new system, but the disruption has been more than I anticipated.
q2: The training was decent. Not exceptional, not terrible. I would have preferred a second session after we had been using the system for a couple of weeks so we could ask informed questions.
q3: Communication could have been better. I learned specifics about the installation timeline pretty late, which made it difficult to prepare my staff. An earlier and more detailed rollout plan shared directly with site administrators would have helped.
q4: N/A
q5: The workflow disruption has been significant. I would change the rollout approach to be more phased, allowing sites to transition one at a time rather than all at once.

name: Renee Brown
q1: The impact on daily operations has been considerable. My team has had to develop workarounds for functions that used to be straightforward with the previous system. I support the district's effort to modernize, but the transition has not been smooth from an operational standpoint.
q2: Training was surface-level. It covered the basics but did not address real-world scenarios that administrators deal with, such as emergency protocols or managing building-wide communications during events. I ended up figuring out a lot on my own.
q3: I would have appreciated more direct communication to site leaders. Much of what we received was general messaging to the entire district, which did not address site-specific concerns or timelines.
q4: The system does have potential. The digital interface is user-friendly when it works.
q5: The workflow disruption has been the biggest issue. We need better admin-specific training and a dedicated support contact for sites during the transition period.

name: Miles Baptiste
q1: It has been a mixed bag. Some things are easier, but the system has not been as reliable as I would like. There have been days where I have had to revert to using my cell phone to communicate with the front office because the intercom was not cooperating.
q2: Honestly, the training felt rushed and incomplete. I left without a clear understanding of several features, and the documentation we were given was not helpful. I would have appreciated a more structured, hands-on approach.
q3: I was generally aware the change was coming, so no major surprises there. But the specifics about what the system would and would not do were not communicated well in advance.
q4: The concept is good. Having a unified platform for communications makes sense.
q5: Better training, period. And the reliability needs to be addressed before we can truly say this was a worthwhile investment.

name: Jasmine Chung
q1: The new system has been a neutral-to-slight-positive change for my daily work. I am generally comfortable adopting new technology, so the learning curve was not a barrier for me. That said, the occasional technical glitches have been frustrating.
q2: Training was solid. I felt like I had enough to get started, and I appreciated the follow-up resources that were shared via email.
q3: Communication about the change was somewhat vague. I knew it was happening, but the rationale and the expected benefits were not articulated clearly. A stronger case for why this system was chosen would have helped build buy-in.
q4: I like the modern interface and the flexibility of the scheduling features.
q5: The reliability has room for improvement. I have also noticed that the system can be slow to connect during peak hours.

name: Jasmine Jordan
q1: From my perspective at the district level, the new system has been a reasonable transition. We anticipated some bumps during rollout, and while some sites have had more issues than others, overall the day-to-day impact on our office has been manageable.
q2: Training was adequate for our needs at the district office. We were involved early in the process, so much of what was covered in formal training was already familiar to us. I do think the site-level training could have been more comprehensive based on the feedback we have been getting.
q3: We were part of the communication planning, so I felt well-informed. I recognize that the messaging may not have landed the same way at every site, and that is something we are working to address.
q4: The vendor has been responsive to our concerns, and the system's reporting capabilities are genuinely better than what we had. The centralized management dashboard has been valuable for our team.
q5: We have heard consistent feedback that site-level staff wanted more advance notice and more detailed training. That is something we need to own and improve for future rollouts.

name: Emily Nelson
q1: I am still fairly new to the district, so I do not have a strong baseline for comparison. The system seems functional, though I have noticed it has created some extra work in terms of coordinating with sites on troubleshooting.
q2: The training was okay but could have been more tailored. As someone newer to the district, I would have appreciated more context about why this particular system was selected and how it compares to what was in place before.
q3: I felt well-informed. The internal communications were clear, and my supervisor kept me in the loop on the project timeline. I appreciated the transparency.
q4: The platform is intuitive and I like the direction the district is going with this upgrade. The ability to manage communications centrally is a real improvement.
q5: The workflow impact has been a bit heavier than expected. We are fielding a lot of support calls from sites, which was not fully planned for in our workload.

name: Tuan Wong
q1: The new system has added some complexity to my work. We are spending more time than anticipated supporting sites with technical issues, which has pulled me away from other priorities.
q2: Training was adequate. I understood the material, though I think the pacing could have been better. Some sections were rushed while others dwelled on things that were fairly intuitive.
q3: Communication about the project was strong. I was kept informed at every stage and felt like I had a clear understanding of the timeline and expectations.
q4: The system architecture is solid and the vendor's support team has been good to work with.
q5: The reliability at some sites has been a concern. We selected this system with confidence, so it is disappointing to see inconsistent performance across locations.

name: Danielle Davis
q1: I have been through several system changes in my time here, and this one has been about average in terms of disruption. The day-to-day impact has been noticeable but not overwhelming. We are managing, but it has required more hands-on support than I think was originally scoped.
q2: The training was fine for our office. I do wish there had been more differentiation between what district staff needed versus what site staff needed. We have different use cases and the training did not fully reflect that.
q3: Communication was decent. Having been involved in the vendor evaluation, I had good visibility into the process. I think for people further from the decision-making, the messaging may have felt less complete.
q5: I would have liked to see a more gradual rollout. Doing everything at once put a strain on our support capacity and I think led to a worse first impression at the sites.

name: Taylor Wilson
q1: As someone who just joined the district, I walked into this transition already in progress. The system itself seems capable, but the adjustment has taken up bandwidth that I had hoped to use getting oriented to other aspects of the role.
q2: Training was okay. I got the basics, but since I did not have context on the previous system, some of the comparisons made during training were lost on me.
q3: I have to say, the communication around this project has been excellent. Even as a newer employee, I felt fully informed about what was happening and why. The project updates were regular and clear.
q4: The system is reliable and well-built. I have not experienced the issues that some sites have reported, and the features we use at the district level work as expected.
q5: The workflow impact has been a bit more than I expected. I think better planning around the support load during transition would have helped.

name: Laura Davis
q1: The system has shifted some of my daily work. I am spending more time on implementation support and less on my regular responsibilities, which is not ideal but understandable during a transition.
q2: I thought the training was well-executed. The trainer was knowledgeable and responsive to questions. I felt prepared to use the system and to support others in using it.
q3: The communication to our office was fine, but I have heard from site staff that they felt out of the loop. Given that we were involved in the selection process, I think we may have overestimated how much information was actually reaching the schools.
q4: The system itself is dependable and the features are a genuine upgrade. The emergency alert functionality in particular is a significant improvement.

name: Carolyn Parker
q1: Honestly, for my work at the district office, the new system has been a positive change. The centralized controls and improved reporting have made my job easier in several respects. I know the experience at sites has been more mixed.
q2: Training was adequate but not remarkable. I would have liked more depth on the administrative backend, but the session gave me enough to be functional.
q3: Communication was reasonable. We were in the loop throughout the process, which I appreciated. I would note that communication to school sites seemed to be less thorough based on what I have been hearing.
q4: The reliability has been strong from our end, and the overall system capabilities are a clear improvement. The scheduling and reporting tools are particularly useful.
q5: I would improve the communication flow to sites. We invested a lot in choosing the right system, and I would hate for poor rollout communication to undermine the reception.

name: Angela Hampton
q1: I am new to this district but I have been through many system transitions in my career. This one has been relatively smooth compared to some I have seen. The daily impact on my work has been minimal and I am optimistic about the system's potential.
q2: The training was good. Having gone through similar implementations elsewhere, I thought the approach was solid. I did notice some colleagues struggling with concepts that could have used more time, so perhaps an additional session would have helped.
q3: Communication was adequate for my needs. Being new, I may have a different perspective than long-tenured staff, but I felt I had the information I needed to do my job.
q4: The system is very reliable and the feature set is impressive. I am excited about where this can go as people get more comfortable with it.
q5: Nothing major. Just continued investment in training and support as people ramp up.

name: Maria Gonzalez
q1: The system change has added work to my plate. I am fielding more support requests from sites and spending time on implementation tasks that were not originally part of my role. I believe in the project, but the workload shift has been real.
q2: Training was sufficient. I felt like I learned what I needed to know, though I would have appreciated a quick-reference guide for the features we use most often.
q3: Communication about the project was good overall. We were kept informed of milestones and timelines, and there was an open-door policy for questions.
q4: The potential is there. Once sites are fully up and running, I think this will be a strong platform for the district.
q5: The workflow disruption at the district level was underestimated. We should have had a clearer support plan in place before going live.

name: Diane Carter
q1: The transition has been fairly smooth from our end. There is always an adjustment period, and this has been no different, but I would not say it has significantly disrupted my daily work.
q2: Training was well-organized and the materials were helpful. I felt prepared going into the launch, which made the transition easier.
q3: I felt well-informed. Being part of the planning process gave me good visibility, and the updates along the way were consistent.
q4: The system works well and the vendor has been a good partner. The centralized management capabilities are exactly what we needed.
q5: I would fine-tune the site communication strategy for next time. Some schools felt they were not given enough advance notice or input.

name: Rafael Aguilar
q1: The system works. I will give it that. But it has not improved my daily workflow in any meaningful way, and the transition itself consumed a lot of time and energy that could have been spent elsewhere. I was not convinced we needed to make this change in the first place, and so far nothing has changed my mind.
q2: Training was passable. I did not find it particularly engaging, but I picked up what I needed. I have been doing this long enough that I can figure most systems out regardless.
q3: I was aware of what was happening. Whether the broader communication was effective is another question. I have heard from many site staff that they felt blindsided, which suggests the messaging did not penetrate the way we thought it would.
q4: The reliability is excellent. I have not experienced a single outage or glitch, and that is worth acknowledging.
q5: I would not have rushed this. A more deliberate, phased approach with genuine input from sites would have generated better buy-in and probably a smoother rollout.

name: Daniel Gupta
q1: The system itself is excellent, but the transition has not improved my day-to-day workflow. If anything, the implementation support burden has added to my responsibilities temporarily. I expect that will level off.
q2: Training was very well done. The sessions were comprehensive, the trainers were responsive, and the follow-up materials were useful. This was one of the stronger training rollouts I have been part of.
q3: Communication was good. We were involved from the beginning, and I appreciated the collaborative approach to the vendor selection. The broader district communication could have started earlier, but from our office it felt well-managed.
q4: The system is extremely reliable and the feature set is a significant upgrade. The training was also a highlight. We made a good choice with this vendor.
q5: The only thing I would change is the timeline. The workflow impact during transition could have been lessened with a more gradual rollout across sites.
